  We introduce PairSAE, which summarizes pairwise tensors via an N-mode SVD into token-wise interaction roles, then uses a sparse autoencoder to learn a shared set of token-level features that decode into both sequence and pair representations. Evaluated on Boltz-2 activations for PLINDER protein-ligand complexes, PairSAE yields interpretable features that align with UniProt annotations and predict Boltz-2 affinity values. These results indicate that PairSAE links the latent space of foundation models for structural biology to interpretable structural concepts, clarifying what the model "knows" while avoiding pairformer-induced pitfalls that limit conventional SAEs.

AI.tldr: PairSAE introduces a sparse autoencoder that uses SVD-compressed pairwise representations to extract interpretable token-level features from pairformer architectures in structural biology foundation models, demonstrated on Boltz-2 for protein-ligand complexes.
AI.motivation: Standard sparse autoencoders fail on pairformer-like architectures due to quadratic feature blow-up from pairwise representations and difficulty capturing concepts distributed across both sequence and pair representations.
AI.method: PairSAE summarizes pairwise tensors via N-mode SVD into token-wise interaction roles, then applies a sparse autoencoder to learn shared token-level features that decode into both sequence and pair representations.
AI.result: Evaluated on Boltz-2 activations for PLINDER protein-ligand complexes, PairSAE yields interpretable features aligned with UniProt annotations and predicts Boltz-2 affinity values.
AI.conclusion: PairSAE links the latent space of structural biology foundation models to interpretable structural concepts, clarifying model knowledge while avoiding pairformer-induced pitfalls that limit conventional SAEs.

title: Test Case Selection for Deep Neural Networks: A Replication Study on LLMs for Code
comment: Accepted at ISSTA 2026, the 35th ACM SIGSOFT International Symposium on Software Testing and Analysis
summary: Recently, test case selection (TCS) techniques have been explored to support the operational evaluation of deep neural networks (DNNs) under limited testing budgets, where labeling cost is a primary concern and uncovering model failures early is a key objective. Although prior studies report promising results, existing empirical evaluations focus almost exclusively on vision-based DNNs and datasets, leaving it unclear whether prior findings generalize to LLM code models. This paper presents a large-scale replication study of TCS techniques in the context of LLM code models. We re-examine established TCS strategies originally proposed for DNNs and complement them with statistical sampling strategies not previously evaluated for TCS. We assess their effectiveness on three code-related classification tasks: clone detection, vulnerability detection, and technical debt prediction. The study spans 17 task-specific fine-tuned model instances, 7 predictive features, and 13 selection strategies, including 12 feature-aware strategies and simple random sampling (SRS) as a feature-agnostic baseline. We evaluate performance along two dimensions: accuracy estimation and early failure discovery. The results indicate that only a subset of findings reported for vision-based DNNs generalize when TCS is applied to LLMs for code. In particular, uncertainty-based features are effective for early failure discovery, while representation-based features are more robust for accuracy estimation. At the same time, performance varies substantially across tasks and models, indicating that TCS effectiveness is context-dependent. Overall, this study provides empirical evidence on the replicability of TCS techniques beyond vision-based deep learning and offers insights into their use for the operational evaluation of LLMs for code.

AI.tldr: This large-scale replication study examines whether test case selection (TCS) techniques originally designed for vision-based DNNs generalize to LLM code models. Across 17 model instances, 7 predictive features, and 13 selection strategies on three code classification tasks, the study finds that only some findings transfer: uncertainty-based features work well for early failure discovery, representation-based features are better for accuracy estimation, and performance is highly context-dependent across tasks and models.
AI.motivation: Prior TCS research focused almost exclusively on vision-based DNNs, leaving it unclear whether established findings apply to LLM code models. This study addresses this gap by replicating TCS techniques in the code domain.
AI.method: Large-scale replication study evaluating 13 selection strategies (12 feature-aware + SRS) across 7 predictive features on 17 fine-tuned LLM instances for three code classification tasks: clone detection, vulnerability detection, and technical debt prediction. Performance evaluated along two dimensions: accuracy estimation and early failure discovery.
AI.result: Only a subset of vision-based DNN findings generalize: uncertainty-based features are effective for early failure discovery; representation-based features are more robust for accuracy estimation; performance varies substantially across tasks and models, showing context dependence.
AI.conclusion: The study provides empirical evidence on the replicability of TCS techniques beyond vision-based deep learning and offers practical insights for operational evaluation of LLMs for code.

title: Grammar-Guided Hierarchical Parsing for Long-form Audio Activity Recognition
summary: Long-form audio exhibits an inherent hierarchy: fine-grained events form sub-activities, which in turn constitute higher-level activities. Prior work often models these levels separately, leading to cross-level inconsistencies and requiring supervision at multiple levels. We formulate the problem as hierarchical parsing from event-level evidence: given detected event segments with class posteriors, we infer an order-consistent Act-Sub-Event parse tree. We propose Hierarchical Activity Grammar, encoding hierarchical composition and temporal-order constraints, and perform grammar-guided decoding that combines event evidence with a grammar prior. This yields a temporally grounded parse tree from which sub-activity segmentation and activity classification are derived, without requiring sub-activity or activity labels for training. Experiments on the long-form MultiAct audio dataset demonstrate improved temporal-order consistency (Edit score) and produces interpretable hierarchies.

AI.tldr: Proposes Hierarchical Activity Grammar for end-to-end hierarchical parsing of long-form audio into Act-Sub-Event parse trees from event-level evidence, achieving temporal-order consistency without sub-activity/activity labels.
AI.motivation: Long-form audio has inherent hierarchical structure but prior work models levels separately, causing cross-level inconsistencies and requiring multi-level supervision.
AI.method: Formulates hierarchical parsing as inferring order-consistent Act-Sub-Event parse trees from detected event segments; proposes Hierarchical Activity Grammar encoding composition and temporal-order constraints; performs grammar-guided decoding combining event evidence with grammar prior.
AI.result: Evaluated on MultiAct audio dataset; achieves improved temporal-order consistency (Edit score) and produces interpretable hierarchies.
AI.conclusion: Grammar-guided hierarchical parsing from event-level evidence enables consistent multi-level structure inference without requiring sub-activity or activity labels for training.

title: HAT-4D: Lifting Monocular Video for 4D Multi-Object Interactions via Human-Agent Collaboration
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 15 pages of main text and 39 pages of appendices. Project page: https://lijiaxin0111.github.io/HAT4D/
summary: Extracting dynamic 4D object interactions from massive, in-the-wild monocular videos offers a highly efficient data collection pathway for scaling Embodied AI and training VLAs. However, existing monocular 4D reconstruction methods primarily focus on isolated objects, often failing under the severe occlusions and complex dynamics inherent in multi-object interactions. To bridge this gap, we propose HAT-4D, the first agentic framework designed to reconstruct the 3D geometry, temporal dynamics, and physical interactions of multiple objects from a single video. By integrating VLMs with a multi-level human-in-the-loop feedback mechanism, HAT-4D efficiently resolves depth ambiguities and interaction-induced occlusions during 3D generation and 4D propagation, yielding physically plausible assets without relying on expensive multicamera rigs. As a scalable data engine, HAT-4D facilitates the creation of MVOIK-4D, an open-world benchmark for monocular 4D interaction reconstruction, accompanied by a novel multi-dimensional evaluation protocol focused on physical plausibility and temporal consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HAT-4D achieves SOTA performance on most evaluation metrics, while maintaining competitive semantic alignment. Ablation studies show that introducing a small amount of human feedback improves interaction reconstruction. Moreover, the data produced by HAT-4D effectively improves baseline performance when used for fine-tuning. Our data and code are available at https://lijiaxin0111.github.io/HAT4D/

AI.tldr: HAT-4D is an agentic framework that reconstructs 4D multi-object interactions from monocular in-the-wild videos using VLMs and human-in-the-loop feedback, achieving SOTA performance and enabling a new benchmark (MVOIK-4D) for open-world 4D interaction reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Existing monocular 4D reconstruction methods focus on isolated objects and fail under severe occlusions and complex dynamics in multi-object interactions. There is a need for a scalable, cost-effective approach to extract dynamic 4D object interactions from massive in-the-wild monocular videos for scaling Embodied AI and training VLAs.
AI.method: HAT-4D integrates Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with a multi-level human-in-the-loop feedback mechanism to resolve depth ambiguities and interaction-induced occlusions during 3D generation and 4D propagation, enabling physically plausible multi-object reconstruction from a single video without expensive multicamera rigs.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA performance on most evaluation metrics with competitive semantic alignment. Ablation studies show small amounts of human feedback improve interaction reconstruction. Data produced by HAT-4D effectively improves baseline performance when used for fine-tuning. Also introduces MVOIK-4D benchmark and a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol focused on physical plausibility and temporal consistency.
AI.conclusion: HAT-4D successfully bridges the gap in monocular 4D reconstruction for multi-object interactions, serving as a scalable data engine. The framework, benchmark, and evaluation protocol advance the field of Embodied AI data acquisition and 4D scene understanding from in-the-wild video.

title: DMV-Bench: Diagnosing Long-Horizon Multimodal Agents' Visual Memory with Incidental Cue Injection
summary: Research on agent memory has matured rapidly, but almost entirely on the text side: few existing benchmarks ask, in an interactive environment, when an agent genuinely needs to remember what it saw rather than what it could write down. We introduce DMV-Bench (Code: https://github.com/yyyujintang/DMV-Bench), the first interactive benchmark for multimodal-agent visual memory. DMV-Bench is built on a controlled home-furnishing e-commerce catalogue of 1,000 product variants in which a text-leakage contract keeps the discriminative signal of each task in the pixels alone. Across a chain of autonomous shopping sessions, every visited product image carries a unique, pre-rendered incidental cue, and the agent is later asked to recall a particular cued product and navigate to its URL. Inspired by dual-coding theory, we propose DualMem, a memory architecture that maintains a visual and a verbal code in parallel. On DMV-Bench, DualMem outperforms a caption baseline and three recent multimodal agent-memory systems at every chain length J in {5, 10, 15, 50} on both Gemini 2.5 Flash and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, with the lead surviving controls for memory-bank size and encoding-position bias, and an asymmetric dual-coding regime in which vision carries the cue end-to-end while the verbal channel plays a smaller query-grounding role.

AI.tldr: DMV-Bench is the first interactive benchmark for multimodal-agent visual memory, testing if agents can remember what they saw (not wrote) in shopping sessions across 1,000 product variants. The proposed DualMem architecture, inspired by dual-coding theory, outperforms baselines by maintaining parallel visual and verbal codes.
AI.motivation: Existing agent memory benchmarks focus almost exclusively on text-based memory, neglecting scenarios where an agent must visually recognize what it has seen in interactive environments. The paper aims to fill this gap by introducing a benchmark that ensures discriminative signal remains in pixels, preventing text leakage.
AI.method: DMV-Bench uses a controlled home-furnishing e-commerce catalogue with 1,000 product variants where each visited product image has a unique pre-rendered incidental cue. A text-leakage contract ensures tasks cannot be solved via text alone. The authors propose DualMem, a dual-coding architecture maintaining parallel visual and verbal codes, evaluated across chain lengths J ∈ {5, 10, 15, 50} on Gemini 2.5 Flash and Qwen2.5-VL-7B.
AI.result: DualMem outperforms a caption baseline and three recent multimodal agent-memory systems at every chain length on both backbones. The advantage persists after controlling for memory-bank size and encoding-position bias. An asymmetric dual-coding regime is identified where vision carries the cue end-to-end while the verbal channel plays a smaller query-grounding role.
AI.conclusion: DMV-Bench provides a controlled testbed for visual memory in multimodal agents, and DualMem demonstrates the effectiveness of parallel visual-verbal coding, with vision being the dominant channel for incidental cue recall.

title: DysLexLens: A Low-Resource LLM Framework for Analysing Dyslexic Learners Insights from Online Forums
summary: Dyslexic learners increasingly use artificial intelligence (AI) tools to support reading, writing, organisation, and study-related tasks. However, their lived experiences with these tools remain largely underexamined. This paper proposes DysLexLens, a low-resource LLM framework, designed to analyse dyslexic learners experience with AI through online forum discussions. DysLexLens is designed as an end-to-end, evidence-traceable architecture which transforms noisy social media posts into a dictionary-driven corpora, provides knowledge-graph (KG)-based question reasoning, generates verifiable query responses, and enables response evaluation through quantitative and human-grounded assessment. DysLexLens has four key features. First, it employs a dictionary-driven filtering method to construct a more focused Reddit corpus on dyslexia and AI, filtering out noisy and weakly related posts to improve the relevance of data collected from low-resource forum contexts. Second, it integrates LLM-assisted semantic analysis with KG-based query reasoning to uncover meaningful patterns. Third, it has quantitative evaluation metrics (RAGAS and Query Robustness) to measure LLM-generated response performance. Fourth, it provides structured qualitative validation guidelines for assessing response quality, with a specific focus on hallucination and evidence alignment. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DysLexLens using dyslexia-related Reddit forum data and 30 questions. The results show its potential generalisability to other low-resource forum data contexts. DysLexLens, sample data, questions and evaluation results are available at Github to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: DysLexLens is a low-resource LLM framework that analyzes dyslexic learners' experiences with AI tools by processing noisy social media forum data (Reddit) through dictionary-driven filtering, knowledge-graph-based reasoning, and both quantitative and human-grounded evaluation, demonstrating effectiveness on dyslexia-related data with 30 questions.
AI.motivation: Dyslexic learners increasingly use AI tools, but their lived experiences with these tools remain largely underexamined. Existing research lacks a systematic way to analyze user opinions from low-resource forum contexts.
AI.method: DysLexLens is an end-to-end, evidence-traceable architecture with four key features: (1) dictionary-driven filtering to construct a focused Reddit corpus on dyslexia and AI; (2) LLM-assisted semantic analysis with KG-based query reasoning; (3) quantitative evaluation metrics (RAGAS and Query Robustness); and (4) structured qualitative validation guidelines for hallucination and evidence alignment assessment.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness using dyslexia-related Reddit forum data and 30 questions, showing potential generalizability to other low-resource forum data contexts.
AI.conclusion: DysLexLens provides a reproducible framework (available on GitHub) for analyzing user experiences in low-resource forum settings, with both quantitative and qualitative evaluation capabilities.

title: Learning in Markovian bandits with non-observable states and constrained decision epochs
summary: This paper studies the problem of regret minimization in Markovian bandits with \emph{non-observable states} and possibly \emph{constrained} decision epochs. The focus is restricted to a ``pure'' regret benchmark, that compares the performance of the learning algorithm to the best \emph{pure policy} which -- akin to optimal policies of stochastic bandits -- picks the optimal arm from start to finish without ever switching. We introduce a generalization of rested Markovian bandits, \emph{self-degrading Markovian bandits}, for which pure policies are always asymptotically optimal.We show that without prior knowledge on the underlying bandit, the regret of algorithms that switch arms rarely necessarily scales super-logarithmically for every bandit, i.e., as $ω(\log(T))$, where $T$ is the learning horizon. Despite the unreachability of the logarithmic regime, we design UCB-NOM, an optimistic algorithm inspired by UCB, of which the regret is nearly logarithmic. Lastly, we show that given prior knowledge on the Markovian bandit in the form of a bound on the bias functions of its arm, a proper instantiation of UCB-NOM achieves $O(\log(T))$ regret. We further show that this prior knowledge allows for a $O(\sqrt{T \log(T)})$ worst-case regret bound for UCB-NOM. Notably, our regret bounds do not depend on the number of states of the underlying Markov chains. Our findings suggest that the non-observability of states is a mild inconvenience in self-degrading Markovian bandits.

AI.tldr: This paper studies regret minimization in Markovian bandits with non-observable states and constrained decision epochs, focusing on a pure regret benchmark. It introduces self-degrading Markovian bandits, shows that rare-switching algorithms must have super-logarithmic regret, designs UCB-NOM which achieves nearly logarithmic regret, and demonstrates that with prior knowledge, logarithmic and worst-case √(T log T) regret bounds are attainable.
AI.motivation: To understand regret minimization in Markovian bandits where states are non-observable and decision epochs may be constrained, and to analyze the feasibility of logarithmic regret under a pure policy benchmark.
AI.method: The paper introduces self-degrading Markovian bandits, a generalization of rested Markovian bandits. It proves lower bounds for rare-switching algorithms and designs UCB-NOM, an optimistic UCB-style algorithm. Different instantiations are analyzed for cases with and without prior knowledge of bias functions.
AI.result: Without prior knowledge, rare-switching algorithms incur ω(log T) regret; UCB-NOM achieves nearly logarithmic regret. With prior knowledge (a bound on bias functions), UCB-NOM achieves O(log T) regret and O(√(T log T)) worst-case regret. Regret bounds do not depend on the number of states.
AI.conclusion: Non-observability of states is a mild inconvenience in self-degrading Markovian bandits, as near-logarithmic regret is achievable, and logarithmic regret is attainable with modest prior knowledge.

title: LLM-Assisted Model-Based GUI Testing for Vue.js Web Applications
summary: Vue.js is a popular framework for building modern web applications. As Vue.js functionality and tooling support grow, ensuring its reliability (through automated testing) is becoming increasingly important. Although model-based testing has been successfully used to automate graphical user interface (GUI) testing on other platforms, its application to Vue.js remains challenging: Transition candidates, which are spread across router configurations and single-file components (SFCs), must be concretized and normalized into an executable page transition graph (PTG) for testing. To address this, we propose the LLMVue framework, which uses a large language model (LLM) to generate a PTG from Vue.js source code. LLMVue infers component hierarchies and route transitions, merging them into a unified PTG across multiple SFCs. We evaluated LLMVue on a collection of ten open-source Vue.js projects from GitHub, using GPT-4o as the LLM backbone. The constructed graphs demonstrate high precision and recall, with low graph edit distance. LLMVue -guided testing also significantly improves the coverage and exploration efficiency, compared to a random exploration baseline (with the same time constraints). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first use of LLMs for model-based GUI testing of Vue.js applications using source-level PTG extraction.

AI.tldr: LLMVue uses LLMs (GPT-4o) to extract a Page Transition Graph (PTG) from Vue.js source code for model-based GUI testing, achieving high precision/recall and improving testing coverage and efficiency over random exploration.
AI.motivation: Model-based testing (MBT) has been applied to GUI testing on other platforms, but applying it to Vue.js is challenging because transition candidates are scattered across router configurations and single-file components (SFCs), and must be concretized and normalized into an executable Page Transition Graph (PTG).
AI.method: LLMVue uses a large language model (GPT-4o) to analyze Vue.js source code (SFCs and router configs) to infer component hierarchies and route transitions, merging them into a unified executable PTG across multiple SFCs for automated GUI testing.
AI.result: Evaluation on 10 open-source Vue.js projects shows high precision and recall with low graph edit distance; LLMVue-guided testing significantly improves coverage and exploration efficiency compared to random exploration under the same time constraints.
AI.conclusion: This is the first use of LLMs for model-based GUI testing of Vue.js applications via source-level PTG extraction, demonstrating that LLM-driven PTG generation is a viable and effective approach for automating Vue.js GUI testing.

title: A Flexible Encoding Model for Non-Unique Note Alignments
comment: Published at the Music Encoding Conference (MEC), 2026
summary: Symbolic music alignment links notes in a symbolic performance to their counterparts in a score. While existing alignment encoding formats provide unique correspondences between these notes, there are various musical practices and forms such as practice repetitions in rehearsal and improvised realizations in basso continuo that require a more flexible approach to encoding their alignments. In this paper, we propose a minimal, backward-compatible extension to the Match file format to support such non-unique and semantically complex alignments. We introduce two virtual pointer notes - virtual score notes and virtual performance notes - which allow to encode multiple links between performance and score notes. In addition we expand the Match file's 'section' line to include semantically meaningful annotations of performance regions beyond score-indicated musical repetitions. We further demonstrate the utility of these extensions through two representative use-cases in piano rehearsal and basso continuo.

AI.tldr: Proposes a backward-compatible extension to the Match file format for encoding non-unique and semantically complex symbolic music alignments, introducing virtual pointer notes (virtual score notes and virtual performance notes) and expanded section annotations, demonstrated through piano rehearsal and basso continuo use cases.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment encoding formats only provide unique correspondences between performance and score notes, which is insufficient for musical practices like practice repetitions in rehearsal and improvised realizations in basso continuo that require more flexible alignment encoding.
AI.method: Proposes a minimal, backward-compatible extension to the Match file format by introducing two virtual pointer notes (virtual score notes and virtual performance notes) to encode multiple links between performance and score notes, and expanding the 'section' line to include semantically meaningful annotations of performance regions beyond score-indicated repetitions.
AI.result: Demonstrates utility through two representative use-cases: piano rehearsal and basso continuo.
AI.conclusion: The proposed extension enables more flexible and semantically rich alignment encoding for non-unique correspondences in symbolic music alignment tasks.

title: Aloe-Vision: Robust Vision-Language Models for Healthcare
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) specialized in healthcare are emerging as a promising research direction due to their potential impact in clinical and biomedical applications. However, progress is constrained by the scarcity of high-quality medical multimodal data, concerns about robustness in safety-critical settings, and the narrow and potentially contaminated evaluation benchmarks that limit reliable assessment. To address these issues, the field requires state-of-the-art solutions to be fully open and reproducible systems in which all components can be inspected, evaluated, and improved. This work introduces Aloe-Vision-Data, a large-scale, quality-filtered mixture which integrates both medical and general domains across multimodal and text-only sources, designed for direct use in model fine-tuning. Building on this dataset, we train the Aloe-Vision family of medical LVLMs, openly released with full weights, training recipes and data, in two scales (7B and 72B). Through comprehensive benchmarking, we demonstrate that high quality training mixtures produce balanced LVLMs which yield significant gains over the baseline models without compromising general capabilities, achieving competitive performance with respect to state-of-the-art alternatives. To support reliable evaluation, we introduce CareQA-Vision, a carefully curated vision benchmark derived from MIR and EIR exams, the residency entrance exams for medical and nursing specialists in Spain, offering novel vision questions with low likelihood of contamination. Finally, we show that current LVLMs remain vulnerable to adversarial and misleading inputs, underscoring reliability challenges in clinical contexts.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Aloe-Vision, a family of open-source medical Large Vision-Language Models (7B and 72B) trained on a large-scale, quality-filtered multimodal/text dataset (Aloe-Vision-Data), along with CareQA-Vision, a carefully curated medical vision benchmark from Spanish residency exams, demonstrating competitive performance while highlighting ongoing reliability challenges against adversarial inputs.
AI.motivation: The field of medical LVLMs is hindered by scarcity of high-quality multimodal data, robustness concerns in safety-critical settings, and narrow/contaminated evaluation benchmarks. There is a need for fully open and reproducible systems where all components can be inspected, evaluated, and improved.
AI.method: The authors construct Aloe-Vision-Data, a large-scale quality-filtered mixture of medical and general domain multimodal and text-only data. They train Aloe-Vision models at 7B and 72B scales using this data, and introduce CareQA-Vision, a curated vision benchmark derived from Spanish MIR/EIR residency exams designed to minimize data contamination.
AI.result: High-quality training mixtures produce balanced LVLMs with significant gains over baselines without compromising general capabilities, achieving competitive performance with state-of-the-art alternatives. However, current LVLMs remain vulnerable to adversarial and misleading inputs, underscoring reliability challenges.
AI.conclusion: Open, reproducible medical LVLMs with carefully filtered training data and clean evaluation benchmarks can advance the field, but robustness issues in clinical contexts remain a critical concern that needs further addressing.

title: MER-R1: Multimodal Emotion Reasoning via Slow-Fast Thinking Synergy
summary: We find that explicit reasoning does not necessarily translate into better multimodal emotion recognition (MER) accuracy, even though it makes predictions more interpretable. Specifically, for reasoning-based MLLMs, fast thinking by triggering direct answers often outperforms slow thinking after deliberative reasoning. Our empirical analyses show that fast thinking improves recall with broader and more confident predictions, whereas slow thinking favors precision through conservative filtering of incorrect categories. Building on these insights, we propose MER-R1, a reinforcement learning framework that turns slow-fast complementarity into explicit optimization. Dual-objective disentanglement separates recall and precision into two optimization signals, allowing them to be jointly optimized rather than traded off against each other. Slow-fast confidence calibration further aligns the final slow-thinking answer with fast-thinking intuition, strengthening correct emotions while suppressing incorrect ones. In this way, MER-R1 unifies the recall-oriented intuition of fast thinking with the precision-oriented selectivity of slow thinking. We further provide theoretical justification for this synergy, showing that it mitigates variance-induced interference during optimization. Extensive experiments on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion show that MER-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance and makes reasoning genuinely benefit emotion recognition.

AI.tldr: MER-R1 is a reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes recall (fast thinking) and precision (slow thinking) for multimodal emotion recognition, achieving state-of-the-art performance by turning their complementarity into explicit optimization through dual-objective disentanglement and confidence calibration.
AI.motivation: Explicit reasoning in MLLMs often improves interpretability but does not necessarily translate into better MER accuracy; slow thinking (deliberative reasoning) favors precision, while fast thinking (direct answers) favors recall. The paper aims to unify these complementary strengths.
AI.method: MER-R1 employs (1) dual-objective disentanglement to separate recall and precision into two optimization signals for joint optimization, and (2) slow-fast confidence calibration to align the slow-thinking answer with fast-thinking intuition, strengthening correct emotions while suppressing incorrect ones.
AI.result: MER-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion benchmarks, with theoretical justification that the synergy mitigates variance-induced interference during optimization.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning can genuinely benefit emotion recognition when slow and fast thinking are explicitly optimized together rather than traded off, unifying recall-oriented intuition with precision-oriented selectivity.

title: Prism Transformer: Progressive Head Schedules for Hierarchical Attention Processing
summary: Multi-head attention conventionally partitions the hidden dimension equally across all heads at every layer, enforcing an identical representational subspace dimension (dh = dmodel/h) throughout the models depth. In this work, we identify this uniform allocation as a fundamental structural bottleneck: due to their restricted dimensional space, early-layer heads are unable to faithfully capture complex, high-dimensional contextual patterns. To resolve this, we introduce the Prism Transformer, a novel architectural paradigm that replaces the static, uniform head configuration with a progressive head schedule. By monotonically increasing the head count across layers, the Prism Transformer naturally establishes a local-to-global representational hierarchy: early layers leverage fewer, exceptionally wide heads to capture complex, local compositional patterns, while deep layers deploy many, narrow heads to decompose these patterns into specialized linguistic features. Crucially, this structural shift is parameter-neutral, compute-neutral, and introduces zero training or inference overhead, preserving identical weight matrices and FLOP budgets as the standard Transformer. Across three model scales (124M, 354M, and 757M), the Prism Transformer consistently outperforms uniform baselines, achieving consistent reductions in validation loss alongside consistent gains on downstream zero-shot benchmarks (including PIQA, HellaSwag, ARC-Easy, and WinoGrande). Our findings demonstrate that non-uniform subspace allocation unlocks latent capacity within the standard Transformer budget, enabling more effective use of model capacity.

AI.tldr: The Prism Transformer replaces standard uniform multi-head attention with a progressive head schedule (fewer, wider heads in early layers; many, narrow heads in deep layers), achieving better performance with identical parameters and compute.
AI.motivation: Standard multi-head attention partitions hidden dimension equally across all heads at every layer, which is identified as a structural bottleneck: early-layer heads with restricted dimensional space cannot faithfully capture complex, high-dimensional contextual patterns.
AI.method: Introduces a progressive head schedule that monotonically increases head count across layers, establishing a local-to-global representational hierarchy: early layers use fewer, wider heads for complex local patterns; deep layers use many, narrow heads for specialized linguistic features. This is parameter-neutral, compute-neutral, and zero-overhead.
AI.result: Across three model scales (124M, 354M, and 757M), the Prism Transformer consistently outperforms uniform baselines with reduced validation loss and improved zero-shot downstream benchmarks (PIQA, HellaSwag, ARC-Easy, WinoGrande).
AI.conclusion: Non-uniform subspace allocation unlocks latent capacity within the standard Transformer budget, enabling more effective use of model capacity.

title: BashCoder-R1: Towards Robust and Explainable Bash Code Generation with Robustness-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization
summary: Bash scripts are the cornerstone of system administration and DevOps automation, where code quality directly impacts system stability and security. In automated Bash script generation using Large Language Models (LLMs), two interconnected failures emerge: unauditable "black box" reasoning and critical robustness vulnerabilities in generated code. To address both, we propose BashCoder-R1, a novel framework for robust and explainable Bash script generation. Our pipeline combines: (1) Continual Pre-training (CPT) to specialize the model on Bash paradigms; (2) Long Chain-of-Thought Supervised Fine-Tuning (L-CoT SFT) on expert-validated reasoning-and-code samples to emulate proactive risk-aware thinking; and (3) Robustness-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (R-GRPO), a reinforcement learning phase optimizing a weighted reward for syntax correctness, robustness (via shellcheck), and format correctness. We evaluate on BashBench, a new benchmark of 952 real-world tasks (773 single-line, 179 multi-line). BashCoder-R1 achieves SyntaxPass (100.00%/94.97%), RobustWarnRate (4.01%/16.47%), RobustPass (95.99%/79.33%), FuncRate (93.01%/93.85%), and FullRate (90.04%/73.18%) for single-line/multi-line tasks, outperforming the strongest baseline DeepSeek-V3.2 (Reasoning) by 37.82% and 20.18% in FullRate. Human evaluation on Functionality, Robustness, and Clarity further confirms BashCoder-R1 achieves the highest quality ratings.

AI.tldr: BashCoder-R1 is a framework for robust and explainable Bash script generation using LLMs, combining Continual Pre-training, Long Chain-of-Thought SFT, and Robustness-Aware RL to outperform DeepSeek-V3.2 by 20-38% in FullRate on a new 952-task benchmark BashBench.
AI.motivation: Bash scripts are critical for system administration and DevOps, but LLM-generated scripts suffer from two failures: unauditable "black box" reasoning and critical robustness vulnerabilities that impact system stability and security.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) Continual Pre-training (CPT) for Bash specialization; (2) Long Chain-of-Thought Supervised Fine-Tuning (L-CoT SFT) on expert-validated reasoning-code samples; (3) Robustness-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (R-GRPO) with a weighted reward optimizing syntax correctness, robustness (shellcheck), and format correctness.
AI.result: On BashBench (952 tasks), BashCoder-R1 achieves SyntaxPass (100%/94.97%), RobustWarnRate (4.01%/16.47%), RobustPass (95.99%/79.33%), FuncRate (93.01%/93.85%), FullRate (90.04%/73.18%) for single/multi-line tasks, outperforming DeepSeek-V3.2 by 37.82% and 20.18% in FullRate.
AI.conclusion: BashCoder-R1 produces high-quality, robust, and explainable Bash scripts, confirmed by both automated metrics and human evaluation on Functionality, Robustness, and Clarity.

title: DG^VoiC: Speaker Clustering for Fraud Investigation under Real Call-Centre Conditions
summary: Insurance fraud remains costly and operationally difficult, particularly in call-centre workflows where many customer interactions begin at FNOL. While recent fraud detection methods mainly rely on structured data, text, or images, repeated speaker identity across calls remains underused as an investigative signal. This paper presents DG^VoiC, a voice clustering framework for customer verification and cross-profile speaker linking on anonymised real call-centre audio. The approach combines sensitive information-aligned anonymisation, speech-focused preprocessing, sliding-window speaker embedding extraction, and cosine similarity based clustering to identify repeated speakers under real telephony conditions. The method was evaluated on 121 recordings, with a curated reference subset of 56 samples in 22 human-agreed speaker clusters. used for validation. The best configuration achieved 96% AMI, 95% ARI, 98% completeness, 100% homogeneity, and 99% V-measure. These results show that speaker clustering can provide a strong additional signal for fraud investigation by helping analysts verify speaker consistency and surface repeated voices across customers.

AI.tldr: DG^VoiC is a voice clustering framework that uses speaker embedding extraction and cosine similarity to identify repeated speakers across anonymized call-centre audio, achieving 96% AMI and strong clustering performance on real telephony data for fraud investigation.
AI.motivation: Insurance fraud detection is costly and challenging in call-centre workflows, and repeated speaker identity across calls is an underused investigative signal in existing fraud detection methods.
AI.method: The framework combines sensitive information-aligned anonymization, speech-focused preprocessing, sliding-window speaker embedding extraction, and cosine similarity-based clustering to identify repeated speakers under real telephony conditions.
AI.result: Evaluated on 121 recordings (56 curated samples in 22 human-agreed clusters), the best configuration achieved 96% AMI, 95% ARI, 98% completeness, 100% homogeneity, and 99% V-measure.
AI.conclusion: Speaker clustering provides a strong additional signal for fraud investigation by enabling analysts to verify speaker consistency and surface repeated voices across customers.

title: ReWorld: Learning Better Representations for World Action Models
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) model future environment evolution under action conditioning, offering a scalable paradigm for autonomous driving. However, existing approaches focus largely on model architecture design, and how a WAM can efficiently learn better world representations for planning remains underexplored. To address this gap, we propose ReWorld, the first representation learning framework specifically designed for autonomous-driving world action models. In WAMs, standard training supervises only the output ends of the generation and planning modules, leaving the intermediate representations that carry world knowledge to be shaped only indirectly, as byproducts of fitting these outputs. The core idea of ReWorld is to treat intermediate representations as direct targets of optimization, shaping them along three complementary dimensions. On the Video DiT responsible for generation, we impose future-predictive supervision on its intermediate representations. On the Action DiT responsible for planning, we first align its intermediate representations cross-modally with the video world representation, then further shape them to be discriminative around safety-critical boundaries via hard-negative supervision. In addition, we systematically analyze the effectiveness of existing representation learning methods in video generation world models, and discuss why their performance is limited on this task. Experiments on nuScenes and NAVSIM show that ReWorld improves fine-tuned video generation by 23.9% in FVD (81.3 to 61.9), raises closed-loop PDMS from 89.1 to 90.4 without any post-training such as RL or post-processing, and accelerates from-scratch convergence by approximately 2x.

AI.tldr: ReWorld is a representation learning framework for autonomous-driving world action models that directly optimizes intermediate representations via future-predictive supervision, cross-modal alignment, and hard-negative safety boundary supervision, achieving significant improvements in video generation quality (FVD 81.3→61.9), closed-loop planning (PDMS 89.1→90.4), and 2x training convergence speedup.
AI.motivation: Existing world action models (WAMs) for autonomous driving focus on architectural design but overlook how intermediate representations carrying world knowledge can be directly optimized for better planning. Standard training only supervises output ends, leaving representations shaped indirectly as byproducts.
AI.method: ReWorld treats intermediate representations as direct optimization targets across three dimensions: (1) future-predictive supervision on Video DiT representations, (2) cross-modal alignment between Action DiT and video world representations, and (3) hard-negative supervision shaping representations around safety-critical boundaries.
AI.result: On nuScenes and NAVSIM: 23.9% FVD improvement (81.3→61.9) for video generation, closed-loop PDMS increased from 89.1 to 90.4 without RL or post-processing, and ~2x convergence speedup from scratch.
AI.conclusion: Directly shaping intermediate representations with targeted supervision signals is an effective paradigm for improving both generation quality and planning performance in world action models for autonomous driving.

title: ToE: A Hierarchical and Explainable Claim Verification Framework with Dynamic Multi-source Evidence Retrieval and Aggregation
summary: The rapid spread of fake news poses increasing threats to information ecosystems, especially as AI-generated misinformation under Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) poisoning allows adversarially crafted content to be systematically surfaced by retrieval systems, contaminating LLM reasoning. In this paper, we propose Tree of Evidence (ToE), a hierarchical evidence reasoning framework for automated fact-checking that models each claim as a dynamically expanding argument tree. ToE integrates a reinforcement learning-driven multi-source retrieval agent, an evidence evaluation agent, and an argument tree aggregation algorithm to iteratively decompose, retrieve, and verify claims through an explainable evidence chain. We further provide a theoretical analysis of the retrieval process, deriving a formal error bound that guarantees the learned policy converges to a neighborhood of the information-theoretically optimal policy. Experiments across multiple datasets and backbone LLMs demonstrate that ToE achieves improvements ranging from 4 to 24 percentage points over competitive baselines, with particularly pronounced gains on adversarially poisoned inputs.

AI.tldr: ToE is a hierarchical evidence reasoning framework for automated fact-checking that uses reinforcement learning-driven retrieval, evidence evaluation, and argument tree aggregation to decompose and verify claims. It achieves 4-24% improvement over baselines, especially against GEO-poisoned misinformation.
AI.motivation: Fake news spread is accelerated by AI-generated misinformation under Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) poisoning, which contaminates LLM reasoning by surfacing adversarially crafted content through retrieval systems.
AI.method: ToE models each claim as a dynamically expanding argument tree, integrating (1) a reinforcement learning-driven multi-source retrieval agent, (2) an evidence evaluation agent, and (3) an argument tree aggregation algorithm for iterative claim decomposition, retrieval, and verification. Theoretical analysis derives a formal error bound for policy convergence.
AI.result: Improvements of 4-24 percentage points over competitive baselines across multiple datasets and backbone LLMs, with strongest gains on adversarially poisoned (GEO) inputs.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical evidence reasoning framework with RL-guided retrieval and explainable evidence chains significantly enhances automated fact-checking robustness against AI-generated misinformation.

title: Operator Learning for Cubic Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation on Periodic Domains
summary: We consider the cubic nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation on two-dimensional flat tori with varying aspect ratios. In this formulation, the choice of aspect ratio governs the Fourier resonance structure, so rational and irrational geometries can exhibit different high-frequency cascade behaviors. We present a geometry-conditioned Fourier neural operator (FNO) for the cubic defocusing NLS equation, where the input consists of the real and imaginary parts of the solution together with the aspect-ratio parameter \(ω^2\). The model is trained to approximate the one-step solution operator and is evaluated on unseen trajectories generated from random-phase initial data using Fourier pseudospectral method. Our numerical experiments show that the learned operator captures the main solution dynamics on both tori and reproduces the distinct Sobolev norm behavior of the two geometries, with stronger \(H^2\)-growth on the rational torus and more constrained behavior on the irrational torus, consistent with the findings of \cite{hrabski2021energy}. We perform ablation studies to examine the roles of retained Fourier modes, activation functions, Fourier-layer depth, and explicit geometry conditioning. The results indicate that including $ω^2$ improves long-time predictive accuracy, especially for the rational geometry, and supports the use of geometry-aware neural operators for learning spectral-transfer phenomena in nonlinear dispersive partial differential equations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a geometry-conditioned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) to solve the cubic nonlinear Schrödinger equation on 2D tori with varying aspect ratios, showing that explicitly conditioning on the aspect ratio parameter ω² improves long-time predictive accuracy and captures distinct Sobolev norm growth behaviors between rational and irrational tori.
AI.motivation: To investigate how aspect ratios of 2D tori affect high-frequency cascade behavior in the cubic NLS equation and to develop a neural operator that can learn these geometry-dependent spectral-transfer phenomena.
AI.method: A geometry-conditioned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) is proposed, taking the real/imaginary parts of the solution and the aspect-ratio parameter ω² as input. It is trained to approximate the one-step solution operator for the cubic defocusing NLS equation on 2D tori, with data generated via Fourier pseudospectral method from random-phase initial conditions.
AI.result: The learned operator captures distinct Sobolev norm behaviors: stronger H²-growth on the rational torus and more constrained behavior on the irrational torus, consistent with prior work. Including ω² improves long-time predictive accuracy, especially for rational geometry. Ablation studies confirm the importance of retained Fourier modes, activation functions, Fourier-layer depth, and explicit geometry conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware neural operators (with explicit aspect-ratio conditioning) are effective for learning spectral-transfer phenomena in nonlinear dispersive PDEs, supporting the use of physics-informed neural architectures for problems where the underlying geometry governs resonance and cascade dynamics.

title: UNICS: Multilingual Code Search via Unified Pseudocode and Contrastive Transfer Learning
comment: Accepted to the ACM International Conference on the Foundations of Software Engineering (FSE 2026). 24.pages
summary: While pre-trained models have achieved remarkable success in code search, their multilingual capabilities remain a major hurdle, plagued by data imbalance, cross-lingual semantic interference, and the loss of critical information from existing unified representations like Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) or Intermediate Representations (IRs). Furthermore, conventional contrastive learning strategies often rely on simplistic hard negative sampling while overlooking the potential of mining hard positives to learn code's intrinsic semantic invariance. To address these challenges, we introduce UNICS, a framework for multilingual code search built on a two-stage training strategy. In the first stage, UNICS is pre-trained on a novel dataset we constructed, which uses pseudo-code as a unified representation to learn a cross-lingual, algorithm-level logic that preserves full semantic fidelity. The second stage employs a multi-task transfer learning strategy that adapts this general knowledge to specific languages by decomposing code into semantic slices (e.g., API calls, function bodies) and incorporating tasks for hard positive mining and cross-lingual dynamic hard negative sampling. Experimental results demonstrate that UNICS achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple multilingual and cross-lingual benchmarks, showcasing superior generalization and performance balance, especially in zero-shot transfer tasks to low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: UNICS is a two-stage multilingual code search framework that uses pseudo-code as a unified representation for pre-training, then applies multi-task transfer learning with hard positive mining and dynamic hard negative sampling, achieving state-of-the-art results across multilingual benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained models struggle with multilingual code search due to data imbalance, cross-lingual semantic interference, information loss from unified representations (ASTs, IRs), and the limitations of conventional contrastive learning that relies on simplistic hard negative sampling while ignoring hard positive mining for learning intrinsic semantic invariance.
AI.method: UNICS employs a two-stage training strategy: (1) pre-training on a novel dataset using pseudo-code as a unified representation to learn cross-lingual, algorithm-level logic with full semantic fidelity; (2) multi-task transfer learning that adapts general knowledge to specific languages by decomposing code into semantic slices (API calls, function bodies) and incorporating hard positive mining and cross-lingual dynamic hard negative sampling tasks.
AI.result: UNICS achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple multilingual and cross-lingual benchmarks, with superior generalization and performance balance, especially in zero-shot transfer tasks to low-resource languages.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage framework with pseudo-code unified representation and multi-task contrastive learning effectively addresses multilingual code search challenges, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization to low-resource languages.

title: What Was That Again? Certified Robustness for Automatic Speech Recognition
summary: Automatic Speech Recognition systems are notoriously both sensitive to adversarial and benign perturbations. While this has been repeatedly demonstrated using reference datasets, detecting such behaviors in deployed systems is incredibly challenging, due to the absence of oracle knowledge of the true transcription. We demonstrate that employing a certification-inspired mechanism can significantly decrease WER, increase recall, and decrease the Spearman correlation between confidence and WER. We achieve this through a dual-gate diagnostic pipeline: a Two-Sided Atomic Audit that accumulates statistical wealth to certify both token existence and adversarial exclusion, and a Rank-Based Tournament that selects the winning sequence. Our evaluations across four diverse architectures demonstrate up to a 55% relative reduction in Word Error Rate, while also providing granular word- and sentence-level certifications to enhance acoustic security.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a certification-inspired dual-gate diagnostic pipeline (Two-Sided Atomic Audit + Rank-Based Tournament) that significantly reduces WER (up to 55% relative improvement), increases recall, and reduces confidence-WER correlation in ASR systems, while providing word- and sentence-level certifications for acoustic security.
AI.motivation: ASR systems are sensitive to adversarial and benign perturbations, but detecting such behaviors in deployed systems is extremely challenging due to the lack of oracle knowledge of the true transcription.
AI.method: A dual-gate diagnostic pipeline consisting of: (1) a Two-Sided Atomic Audit that accumulates statistical wealth to certify both token existence and adversarial exclusion, and (2) a Rank-Based Tournament that selects the winning sequence.
AI.result: Up to 55% relative reduction in Word Error Rate, increased recall, decreased Spearman correlation between confidence and WER, and granular word- and sentence-level certifications across four diverse ASR architectures.
AI.conclusion: A certification-inspired mechanism can effectively enhance ASR robustness and acoustic security without requiring oracle knowledge of true transcriptions.

title: TruEye: Fine-Grained Detection of AI-Generated Human Subjects in Images
summary: AI generated images are proliferating across the Internet. While some are used for entertainment, others are weaponized for fraud and social engineering attacks on social media users. Existing detectors overfit to generators seen during training, treat detection as opaque binary classification, or rely on costly Large Language Models (LLMs) to explain their outputs. In this paper, we present TruEye, a novel model for fine grained detection and localization of AI manipulated or AI generated humans and scenes. Unlike conventional detectors that assign a single authenticity label, TruEye is the first to distinguish among five compositional categories of synthetic content, including the most challenging case in which a real human is composited into a real scene where they were never physically present. At its core is a mask conditioned dual stream transformer that separates human and scene tokens while preserving patch level spatial correspondence. Specialized reasoning within each stream and region gated cross attention enforce semantic coherence between subject and background, while token level supervision and global compositional classification yield robust, interpretable predictions without invoking an LLM. By restricting intra stream attention to semantically coherent tokens, TruEye also runs over $100\times$ faster than LLM based competitors. Experiments on 6 datasets and our newly curated FineSyn dataset, show that TruEye surpasses state of the art detectors with higher accuracy, faster inference, and stronger generalization to unseen AI generated or manipulated images.

AI.tldr: TruEye is a novel model for fine-grained detection and localization of AI-generated or manipulated images, distinguishing five compositional categories of synthetic content including composite humans in real scenes. It uses a mask-conditioned dual-stream transformer with token-level supervision and region-gated cross attention for interpretable, robust predictions that are 100× faster than LLM-based methods and achieve state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-generated image detectors suffer from limitations: they overfit to specific generators, treat detection as opaque binary classification, or rely on costly LLMs for explainability. There is a need for a more nuanced, interpretable, and efficient detector that can handle fine-grained synthetic content categories, especially the challenging case of real humans composited into real scenes.
AI.method: TruEye employs a mask-conditioned dual-stream transformer that separates human and scene tokens while preserving patch-level spatial correspondence. It uses specialized reasoning within each stream and region-gated cross attention to enforce semantic coherence between subject and background. Token-level supervision and global compositional classification enable interpretable predictions without LLMs. Intra-stream attention is restricted to semantically coherent tokens for efficiency.
AI.result: Experiments on 6 datasets and the newly curated FineSyn dataset show that TruEye surpasses state-of-the-art detectors with higher accuracy, faster inference (over 100× faster than LLM-based competitors), and stronger generalization to unseen AI-generated or manipulated images.
AI.conclusion: TruEye effectively addresses key limitations of existing detectors by providing fine-grained, interpretable, and efficient detection of AI-generated/manipulated images, with strong generalization capabilities.

title: Towards Reliable and Robust LLM Planning: Symbolic Feedback-Driven Iterative Self-Refinement Framework
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have attracted widespread attention from academia and industry, yet their deployment raises critical security concerns regarding robustness and reliability. Planning, a core component of intelligent behavior, remains challenging for LLMs, which often produce infeasible or incorrect solutions in long-horizon decision-making tasks due to inherent complexity. In this paper, we propose a symbolic feedback-driven iterative self-refinement framework to enhance the robustness and reliability of LLMs in long-horizon planning. Specifically, a natural language prompting mechanism is introduced to map logical symbols into natural language descriptions, enabling LLMs to better capture task constraints and semantics. We further design a symbolic verifier that identifies errors and converts them into corrective instructions interpretable by the LLM, thereby guiding self-refinement. In addition, we leverage a plan recognizer to infer goal reachability, facilitating more effective guidance toward desired goals. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently improves both feasibility and correctness in long-horizon planning tasks. This highlights its effectiveness in enhancing the reliability of LLM-based planning and potential to enable more trustworthy AI systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a symbolic feedback-driven iterative self-refinement framework that uses natural language prompting, a symbolic verifier, and a plan recognizer to improve LLM robustness and reliability in long-horizon planning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs deployed in real-world scenarios face critical security concerns regarding robustness and reliability, especially in long-horizon planning where they often produce infeasible or incorrect solutions.
AI.method: (1) A natural language prompting mechanism that maps logical symbols into natural language descriptions; (2) A symbolic verifier that identifies errors and converts them into corrective instructions for LLM self-refinement; (3) A plan recognizer that infers goal reachability to guide the process toward desired goals.
AI.result: The framework consistently improves both feasibility and correctness in long-horizon planning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively enhances the reliability of LLM-based planning and demonstrates potential for building more trustworthy AI systems.

title: The Curse of Multiple Mediators: Hidden Interaction Effects in Activation Patching
summary: Activation patching is the primary tool in mechanistic interpretability. It attributes causal responsibility for a model behavior to each of its individual components by estimating its natural indirect effect (NIE). Re-deriving the activation patching estimand from causal mediation analysis, we find that the NIE does not solely capture the causal effect through the specific component. It also contains interaction effects (INT) that measure how much the component's causal effect itself depends on the state of other components in the model. A natural response may be to try to eliminate INT by adjusting the estimator or unit of analysis, but each of these potential remedies has predictable failure modes. We demonstrate these failure modes in the GPT-2 IOI circuit; components whose causal importance is conditional on the state of other components are either invisible or artificially inflated, and INT variance explains the previously documented instability of faithfulness scores. We prove that INT scales with the distance between clean and patched component activations, is negligible when the model is locally affine, and decomposes combinatorially into pairwise and higher-order group interactions. Despite its inevitability, INT is not a nuisance to be eliminated, but rather a diagnostic for interpretability studies. Its individual and group-level magnitude and sign signal when causal conclusions are prompt-dependent, and when greedy NIE-based component ranking will miss mechanisms only discoverable through combinatorial search.

AI.tldr: Activation patching's natural indirect effect (NIE) unavoidably contains interaction effects (INT) that conflate component-specific causality with cross-component dependencies, making INT a diagnostic signal rather than a nuisance to eliminate.
AI.motivation: To formally re-derive activation patching within causal mediation analysis and reveal that the NIE estimand conflates component-specific causal effects with interaction effects (INT) from other components, challenging standard interpretability attributions.
AI.method: Re-derivation of activation patching NIE from causal mediation analysis; empirical validation on GPT-2 IOI circuit; theoretical proofs on INT scaling with activation distance, local affinity conditions, and combinatorial decomposition.
AI.result: INT causes components to be either invisible or artificially inflated in GPT-2 IOI; INT variance explains faithfulness score instability; INT scales with clean-patched activation distance and decomposes into higher-order interactions; standard remedies fail predictably.
AI.conclusion: INT is inevitable in activation patching but useful: its magnitude and sign diagnose prompt-dependency and reveal when greedy NIE-based ranking misses mechanisms only found via combinatorial search.

title: Reasoning Beyond Prediction: From Data-Driven to Causal Software Engineering
comment: Accepted for publication in Communications of the ACM
summary: Software engineering is an intellectually demanding, creative discipline that juggles a web of interdependent tasks to design, build, and assure the quality of increasingly complex systems. As our expectations from software soar - with demands spanning AI-driven products, pervasively distributed and cloud-native architectures, and deeply embedded cyber-physical environments - its complexity steadily increases. In response, a new wave of co-engineering methods and tools, fueled by deep learning, has emerged to augment the process, enhancing automation and decision support. Yet, these advances remain far from delivering the kind of intelligent support that modern software development demands. We call for a new paradigm of human-machine cooperation: one where machines don't just automate routine tasks or predict from learned patterns, but actively amplify engineers' reasoning through the lens of causation. As software becomes smarter, a smarter support is needed.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that as software complexity increases with AI, cloud-native, and cyber-physical systems, current deep learning-based co-engineering tools are insufficient. The authors call for a new paradigm of human-machine cooperation where machines augment engineers' reasoning through causation rather than just automation or pattern prediction.
AI.motivation: Software engineering is increasingly complex due to demands from AI-driven products, distributed architectures, and cyber-physical environments. Existing deep learning-based co-engineering tools still fall short of providing the intelligent support needed for modern software development.
AI.method: The paper proposes a paradigm shift toward human-machine cooperation where machines amplify engineers' causal reasoning, going beyond routine automation and pattern-based prediction.
AI.result: No empirical results reported; this is a position/vision paper outlining a future direction for software engineering support tools.
AI.conclusion: A new paradigm of causation-driven human-machine cooperation is needed to provide smarter support for increasingly smart software systems.

title: Structured-Li-GS: Structured 3D Gaussians Splatting with LiDAR Incorporation and Spatial Constraints
summary: In this study, we develop a Structured framework for Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with LiDAR integration (Structured-Li-GS). It is a lightweight Gaussian Splatting pipeline that leverages LiDAR-inertial-visual SLAM. Structured-Li-GS achieves high-quality 3D reconstructions with fewer Gaussians by training on accurate, dense, colorized point clouds. Gaussian primitives are anchored using sub-sampled point clouds, and their ellipsoidal parameters are initialized from local surface geometry. Our training strategy integrates a comprehensive set of loss terms, including photometric, flattening, offset, depth, and normal losses, guided by the dense point cloud, enabling accurate reconstruction without Gaussian densification. This approach produces up-to-scale, high-fidelity results with a moderate model size. For experimental validation, we develop a custom hardware-synchronized LiDAR-camera handheld scanner. Experiments on both benchmark datasets and our real-world in-house dataset demonstrate that Structured-Li-GS surpasses state-of-the-art methods while using fewer Gaussians.

AI.tldr: Structured-Li-GS is a lightweight 3D Gaussian Splatting pipeline that integrates LiDAR-inertial-visual SLAM to produce high-quality, up-to-scale 3D reconstructions with fewer Gaussians, using accurate dense colorized point clouds and a comprehensive training strategy, outperforming SOTA methods on benchmark and real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: To develop a lightweight 3DGS framework that reduces model complexity (fewer Gaussians) while maintaining high reconstruction quality, by effectively leveraging LiDAR-inertial-visual SLAM to provide accurate, dense, colorized point clouds for initialization and supervision.
AI.method: Structured-Li-GS anchors Gaussian primitives on sub-sampled point clouds with ellipsoidal parameters initialized from local surface geometry. Training uses photometric, flattening, offset, depth, and normal losses guided by dense point clouds, eliminating the need for Gaussian densification. A custom hardware-synchronized LiDAR-camera handheld scanner was built for validation.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both benchmark datasets and real-world in-house datasets while using fewer Gaussians, achieving up-to-scale, high-fidelity reconstructions with moderate model size.
AI.conclusion: LiDAR integration with structured Gaussian initialization and multi-loss training enables efficient, high-quality 3D reconstruction without densification, validated by both public benchmarks and a custom LiDAR-camera handheld scanner.

title: Understanding Rollout Error in Graph World Models
summary: World models are often used for planning by rolling learned dynamics forward. Many planning environments, however, are not vectors or images; they are graphs of agents, tools, skills, routes, and dependencies. In these settings, a local prediction error may stay local or spread through the graph, and the failure mode changes again when edges are predicted rather than fixed. This paper studies long-horizon rollout error in Graph World Models (GWMs). We formulate a unified fixed-edge and dynamic-edge GWM framework with action nodes for node-, edge-, and graph-level decisions. We develop graph-valued rollout bounds that separate topology-induced amplification from model-induced amplification, and we introduce a joint node-edge operator for dynamic-edge rollouts. Guided by the analysis, we propose Error-Aware GWM, which combines spectral regularization, rollout consistency, and critical-node weighting. Across synthetic topologies and heterogeneous agent-graph testbeds, rollout error and planning regret grow with horizon, dynamic-edge training is needed when structure evolves, and Error-Aware GWM prevents long-horizon divergence while preserving prediction accuracy. Real-world graph benchmarks clarify the scope of GWMs: they are most useful for dynamic graph rollout and agent planning, while specialized graph models remain strong on static or sparse prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper studies long-horizon rollout error in Graph World Models (GWMs), develops theoretical bounds separating topology-induced and model-induced error amplification, proposes Error-Aware GWM combining spectral regularization, rollout consistency, and critical-node weighting, and demonstrates its effectiveness in preventing long-horizon divergence across synthetic and heterogeneous agent-graph testbeds.
AI.motivation: World models for planning often assume vector or image inputs, but many real-world planning environments are graph-structured (agents, tools, skills, routes, dependencies). In such settings, prediction errors can propagate through the graph, and the failure mode changes when edges are predicted rather than fixed. The paper aims to understand and mitigate long-horizon rollout error in Graph World Models.
AI.method: The authors formulate a unified fixed-edge and dynamic-edge GWM framework with action nodes for node-, edge-, and graph-level decisions. They develop graph-valued rollout bounds that separate topology-induced amplification from model-induced amplification and introduce a joint node-edge operator for dynamic-edge rollouts. Guided by analysis, they propose Error-Aware GWM combining spectral regularization, rollout consistency, and critical-node weighting.
AI.result: Across synthetic topologies and heterogeneous agent-graph testbeds: (1) rollout error and planning regret grow with horizon, (2) dynamic-edge training is needed when structure evolves, and (3) Error-Aware GWM prevents long-horizon divergence while preserving prediction accuracy. Real-world graph benchmarks clarify that GWMs are most useful for dynamic graph rollout and agent planning, while specialized graph models remain strong on static or sparse prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: Error-Aware GWM effectively addresses long-horizon rollout error in graph-structured planning environments by separating topology-induced and model-induced error amplification and applying spectral regularization, rollout consistency, and critical-node weighting. The scope of GWMs is best suited for dynamic graph rollout and agent planning rather than static prediction tasks.

title: Boundary condition fidelity for bottom-hole pressure and CO2 plume prediction in geological carbon storage
summary: Accurate prediction of bottom-hole pressure (BHP) and CO2 plume migration is essential for safe geological carbon storage, yet practical simulations often rely on truncated domains where artificial boundaries distort pressure diffusion and CO2 saturation footprints. In this study, we evaluate how boundary-condition fidelity affects BHP and CO2 plume prediction by comparing ten reduced-domain boundary treatments against full-domain reference simulations in homogeneous and heterogeneous reservoirs. We test uniform pore-volume multipliers, transmissibility modifiers, corner-adjusted pore-volume corrections, layered corrections, and gradual modifiers using BHP RMSE, NRMSE, peak pressure deviation, and plume Intersection over Union (IoU) as performance metrics. Our results show that conserving corner pore volume is the most important requirement for truncated-domain modeling. We find that uniform treatments which neglect corner storage generate large pressure errors, with BHP RMSE of 362 to 382 psi in the homogeneous model and 250 to 304 psi in the heterogeneous model, and yield plume IoU values near 0.80 to 0.84, indicating roughly 16 to 20% of the combined plume area is misrepresented. Corner-adjusted scenarios substantially reduce pressure errors and raise plume IoU above 0.94, but we observe that transmissibility correction is not universally beneficial. In homogeneous reservoirs, uniform transmissibility adjustment improves pressure fidelity; in heterogeneous reservoirs, it can over-restrict flow across variable-permeability boundary faces, increasing BHP error and contracting the predicted plume. We find the gradual modifier with transmissibility correction provides the most consistent performance, achieving BHP NRMSE below 3.7% and plume IoU above 0.97 in both reservoir types.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates boundary-condition fidelity impacts on bottom-hole pressure (BHP) and CO2 plume predictions in truncated-domain geological carbon storage simulations. Comparing ten reduced-domain boundary treatments against full-domain references, the authors find that conserving corner pore volume is critical; corner-adjusted scenarios reduce BHP error and raise plume IoU above 0.94, while a gradual modifier with transmissibility correction delivers the most consistent performance (BHP NRMSE < 3.7%, plume IoU > 0.97) in both homogeneous and heterogeneous reservoirs.
AI.motivation: Accurate BHP and CO2 plume migration prediction is essential for safe geological carbon storage, but practical simulations using truncated domains suffer from artificial boundaries that distort pressure diffusion and CO2 saturation footprints, motivating a systematic evaluation of boundary-treatment fidelity.
AI.method: Ten reduced-domain boundary treatments (including uniform pore-volume multipliers, transmissibility modifiers, corner-adjusted pore-volume corrections, layered corrections, and gradual modifiers) were compared against full-domain reference simulations in both homogeneous and heterogeneous reservoirs. Performance was assessed using BHP RMSE, NRMSE, peak pressure deviation, and plume Intersection over Union (IoU).
AI.result: Uniform treatments neglecting corner storage produced large errors (BHP RMSE 362-382 psi homogeneous, 250-304 psi heterogeneous; plume IoU ~0.80-0.84). Corner-adjusted scenarios reduced errors substantially (IoU > 0.94). Transmissibility correction was not universally beneficial: it helped in homogeneous reservoirs but over-restricted flow in heterogeneous reservoirs. The gradual modifier with transmissibility correction achieved the best overall performance (BHP NRMSE < 3.7%, plume IoU > 0.97).
AI.conclusion: Conserving corner pore volume is the most critical requirement for accurate truncated-domain CO2 storage modeling. A gradual modifier with transmissibility correction provides the most consistent and accurate predictions across both homogeneous and heterogeneous reservoir types.

title: Evolution-Aware Regression Test Prioritization of ML-Enabled Systems Using Gradient-Based Behavior Vectors
comment: Accepted to the 41st IEEE/ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE 2026)
summary: The machine learning(ML) component of an ML-enabled system evolves through retraining, fine-tuning, and optimization, so previously valid test results may no longer hold. A single evolution step can worsen performance on some test cases while improving others, making regression test prioritization inherently directional. We present Gradient-based Behavior Vector-Parameter Delta(GBV-PD), the first approach to operationalize the behavior vector space for evolution-aware regression test prioritization. GBV-PD represents each test case as a gradient-based vector(GBV), a low-dimensional projection of its loss gradient under the original model. It then projects the observed parameter update of the evolved model onto the same PCA basis and uses the resulting alignment to estimate whether each test case's loss is likely to increase or decrease, without running the evolved model on test cases during prioritization. In an empirical study across classification and regression tasks, GBV-PD consistently outperformed non-directional baselines and remained competitive with a full-gradient reference, while offering better time and storage profiles for repeated updates via reusable GBV caching. These results show that behavior-space ideas can be operationalized into a practical and efficient mechanism for repeated-update regression testing of evolving ML-enabled systems.

AI.tldr: GBV-PD is a novel approach for evolution-aware regression test prioritization in ML systems, using gradient-based behavior vectors to predict test case loss changes without running the evolved model, outperforming non-directional baselines.
AI.motivation: ML components evolve through retraining/fine-tuning, causing previous test results to become invalid. Regression test prioritization is inherently directional (performance can improve for some cases while worsening others), but existing methods lack direction-aware capabilities.
AI.method: GBV-PD represents each test case as a gradient-based vector (GBV) via low-dimensional PCA projection of loss gradients under the original model. It then projects the evolved model's parameter update onto the same PCA basis and uses alignment to estimate loss increase/decrease directionality without running the evolved model on test cases during prioritization.
AI.result: GBV-PD consistently outperformed non-directional baselines across classification and regression tasks, remained competitive with full-gradient reference methods, and offered superior time/storage efficiency via reusable GBV caching for repeated updates.
