AI.conclusion: Behavior-space ideas can be operationalized into a practical, efficient mechanism for repeated-update regression testing of evolving ML-enabled systems.

title: Tessellating The Earth
comment: European Conference on Computer Vision -- ECCV 2026
summary: Geolocation encoders, which map geographic coordinates to learned representations, are emerging as an effective means of capturing visual and non-visual characteristics from a latitude-longitude pair alone. However, existing approaches project coordinates onto fixed bases (e.g., spherical harmonics), allocating representational capacity uniformly and devoting equal resources to the open ocean and to a developing city. We introduce Tessellating the Earth (TTE), a location encoder built from learnable Spherical Voronoi partitions that concentrates representational capacity where it is needed in a fully differentiable, end-to-end manner. Each Voronoi site carries its own embedding and migrates during training toward discriminative areas. To bridge the gap between local spatial structure and global semantic understanding, we introduce \emph{global semantic tokens}: a set of shared learnable concept tokens that distill semantic knowledge from the satellite imagery into a compact vocabulary the location encoder can reference at inference, enabling geographically distant sites covering similar environments to share semantics. TTE sets a new state of the art for location encoders across a suite of geospatial classification and regression tasks, and achieves the strongest results when used as a geographic prior for fine-grained species classification on iNaturalist-2018. Code, and weights are available at https://github.com/mvrl/TTE.

AI.tldr: TTE is a learnable location encoder using Spherical Voronoi partitions and global semantic tokens that concentrates representational capacity discriminatively, achieving state-of-the-art results on geospatial tasks and species classification.
AI.motivation: Existing geolocation encoders project coordinates onto fixed bases (e.g., spherical harmonics), allocating representational capacity uniformly and wasting resources on non-discriminative areas like open oceans versus developing cities.
AI.method: TTE uses learnable Spherical Voronoi partitions where each site carries an embedding that migrates during training toward discriminative areas. It also introduces global semantic tokens—shared learnable concept tokens that distill semantic knowledge from satellite imagery into a compact vocabulary for reference at inference.
AI.result: TTE sets a new state of the art across geospatial classification and regression tasks, and achieves the strongest results as a geographic prior for fine-grained species classification on iNaturalist-2018.
AI.conclusion: The proposed learnable Voronoi-based encoding with global semantic tokens effectively concentrates capacity where needed and bridges local spatial structure with global semantic understanding, demonstrating superior performance across multiple geospatial benchmarks.

title: Grounded Iterative Language Planning: How Parameterized World Models Reduce Hallucination Propagation in LLM Agents
summary: World models for language agents come in two useful forms. An agent-based world model calls an LLM API and reasons flexibly in language, but its errors appear as hallucinated state changes that are hard to score with ordinary regression losses. A parameterized world model is a trained transition predictor; its errors are easier to measure with quantities such as NodeMSE, delta accuracy, and validity accuracy, but it is usually weaker as a standalone planner. We compare these two families on four graph-structured planning benchmarks and introduce operational hallucination metrics for the agent-based case. The comparison motivates \textbf{Grounded Iterative Language Planning} (GILP), which trains only a small parameterized backbone and combines it with API-based agent reasoning. The backbone supplies valid actions, predicted state deltas, risk, and value; the LLM drafts an action and imagined delta; and a consistency gate asks for revision when the two disagree. On real GPT-4o-mini calls, GILP reduces hallucinated-state rate from 0.176 to 0.035. In calibrated simulator ablations, it raises success from 0.668 to 0.838 while adding only ~22% extra LLM calls.

AI.tldr: The paper compares agent-based (LLM-driven) and parameterized (trained) world models for language agents on graph planning tasks. It introduces GILP, which combines a small trained backbone with LLM reasoning via a consistency gate, significantly reducing hallucinated states and improving task success with modest extra LLM calls.
AI.motivation: Agent-based world models (LLM APIs) reason flexibly but produce hallucinated state changes hard to score; parameterized world models are easier to evaluate but weaker as standalone planners. The paper seeks to combine their strengths.
AI.method: Proposes Grounded Iterative Language Planning (GILP): a small parameterized backbone predicts valid actions, state deltas, risk, and value; an LLM drafts actions and imagined deltas; a consistency gate triggers revision when predictions disagree.
AI.result: On GPT-4o-mini, GILP reduces hallucinated-state rate from 0.176 to 0.035. In simulator ablations, success rate rises from 0.668 to 0.838 with ~22% extra LLM calls.
AI.conclusion: GILP effectively integrates trained world models with LLM reasoning, yielding more grounded planning with minimal overhead.

title: Productionized Fairness Measurement Under Privacy Constraints
summary: Fairness measurements in the form of disaggregated evaluations often rely on demographic signals that are legally constrained or culturally sensitive. Race and ethnicity signals are among the more difficult signals to curate and use for this task. This paper presents Privacy-Preserving Probabilistic Race/Ethnicity Estimation (PPRE) as a method for enabling fairness measurements with respect to race/ethnicity for U.S.\ LinkedIn members in a privacy-preserving manner. PPRE applies privacy technologies (specifically: secure two-party computation, differential privacy, and additive homomorphic encryption) on top of two race/ethnicity demographic signal sources (the Bayesian Improved Surname Geocoding estimator and a sparse golden survey set of self-reported demographics) to power a fairness measurement solution with respect to US-based race/ethnicity demographics. We detail its privacy guarantees and demonstrate its application on candidate- and viewer-side fairness measurements. We close with a transferable framework for institutions seeking to implement similar privacy-preserving measurement infrastructure.

AI.tldr: PPRE is a privacy-preserving method combining secure computation, differential privacy, and encryption to estimate race/ethnicity for fairness measurements on LinkedIn's U.S. members, using surname-geocoding and self-reported survey data.
AI.motivation: Fairness measurements require demographic signals like race/ethnicity, but these signals are legally constrained and culturally sensitive, making them difficult to curate and use while preserving privacy.
AI.method: PPRE combines Bayesian Improved Surname Geocoding (BISG) with a sparse golden survey set of self-reported demographics, layered with secure two-party computation, differential privacy, and additive homomorphic encryption to enable privacy-preserving race/ethnicity estimation.
AI.result: The method enables both candidate-side and viewer-side fairness measurements for U.S. LinkedIn members while providing formal privacy guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a transferable framework for institutions seeking to implement similar privacy-preserving measurement infrastructure for fairness evaluations.

title: SBridge: Identifying Source-to-Binary Function Similarity via Cross-Domain Control Block Matching
summary: We present SBridge, a precise approach for identifying functions in binaries that are similar to the given source code functions. Identifying reused code in binaries is critical for security, particularly for detecting propagated vulnerabilities. Although binary-to-binary comparison is feasible, leveraging source code as the reference is more practical because source code is easier to collect and analyze directly without compilation. However, significant gaps between source and binary representations, including function inlining, create challenges in cross-domain function detection. Existing approaches primarily rely on string literals or structural similarities between entire functions, failing to capture detailed code behavior and generating many false alarms. SBridge addresses these limitations through a key innovation: control block-based function matching, which encapsulates essential functional features by segmenting functions into meaningful units such as conditionals and loops. Leveraging control blocks as a cross-domain representation, SBridge enables precise measurement of function similarity between source and binary code, effectively overcoming challenges posed by function inlining and stripped binaries. For evaluation, we collected 3,904 real-world C/C++ binaries from BinKit. In experiments identifying binary functions identical to input source functions, despite approximately 40% of binary functions being inlined, SBridge achieved 75.13% recall@1 and 80.98% recall@5, outperforming existing approaches, which achieved up to 43.31% recall@1 and 50.2% recall@

AI.tldr: SBridge is a precise cross-domain (source-to-binary) function identification approach using control block-based matching, achieving high recall (75.13% recall@1, 80.98% recall@5) even with ~40% function inlining, significantly outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Identifying reused code in binaries is critical for security (e.g., detecting propagated vulnerabilities). Using source code as reference is more practical than binary-to-binary comparison, but significant gaps between source and binary representations (e.g., function inlining, stripped symbols) cause existing approaches to fail by relying on string literals or whole-function structural similarity, generating many false alarms.
AI.method: SBridge introduces control block-based function matching: segmenting functions into meaningful units (e.g., conditionals, loops) that encapsulate essential functional features. These control blocks serve as a cross-domain representation bridging source and binary, enabling precise similarity measurement while overcoming inlining and stripped binary challenges.
AI.result: Evaluated on 3,904 real-world C/C++ binaries from BinKit. Despite ~40% of binary functions being inlined, SBridge achieved 75.13% recall@1 and 80.98% recall@5, substantially outperforming existing approaches (best baseline: 43.31% recall@1, 50.2% recall@5).
AI.conclusion: SBridge demonstrates that control block-based matching is an effective cross-domain representation for source-to-binary function identification, significantly improving accuracy over prior methods even under heavy function inlining.

title: Large Language Model Teaches Visual Students: Cross-Modality Transfer of Fine-Grained Conceptual Knowledge
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) possess broad conceptual knowledge acquired through large-scale text pretraining, yet their potential to supervise models in other modalities remains underexplored. In this work, we propose LaViD--Language-to-Visual Knowledge Distillation--a simple and effective framework for transferring high-level semantic knowledge from a language-only teacher to a vision-only student model. Instead of relying on paired multimodal data, LaViD elicits conceptual signals from an LLM by prompting it to generate multiple-choice questions (MCQs) that probe semantic distinctions between visual classes. Each class is mapped to a soft label distribution over these MCQs, forming a rich conceptual signature that guides the student through an auxiliary distillation loss. Notably, despite using a language-only teacher without access to image data, LaViD consistently outperforms recent methods like MaKD that distill from vision-language models across multiple fine-grained benchmarks. It also achieves competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art visual distillation methods such as DKD and MLKD, with further gains when combined with logit standardization. On the Waterbirds dataset, LaViD substantially improves worst-group accuracy, demonstrating enhanced robustness to spurious correlations with distillation. Code is available at https://github.com/lliangthomas/lavid.

AI.tldr: LaViD is a framework for distilling high-level semantic knowledge from a language-only LLM teacher to a vision-only student model by generating multiple-choice questions that probe class distinctions, outperforming existing methods that use vision-language teachers.
AI.motivation: LLMs possess rich conceptual knowledge from text pretraining, but their potential to supervise other modalities (e.g., vision) is underexplored. Existing distillation methods rely on paired multimodal data or vision-language models, which may limit the transfer of high-level semantic understanding.
AI.method: LaViD elicits conceptual signals from an LLM by prompting it to generate multiple-choice questions (MCQs) that probe semantic distinctions between visual classes. Each class is mapped to a soft label distribution over these MCQs, forming a conceptual signature. An auxiliary distillation loss guides the vision-only student using these signatures.
AI.result: LaViD consistently outperforms recent methods like MaKD that distill from vision-language models across multiple fine-grained benchmarks. It achieves competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art visual distillation methods (DKD, MLKD). On Waterbirds, it substantially improves worst-group accuracy and robustness to spurious correlations.
AI.conclusion: Language-only LLMs can effectively supervise vision-only models via conceptual question-asking, without needing paired multimodal data, and this approach improves both accuracy and robustness.

title: ATOD: Annealed Turn-aware On-policy Distillation for Multi-turn Autonomous Agents
summary: Training small language-model agents for long-horizon interactive tasks requires both fast imitation and reward-driven improvement. On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense teacher guidance and typically improves rapidly in the early stage, but its gains saturate once the student approaches the teacher, limiting the final performance ceiling. Reinforcement learning (RL) directly optimizes environment rewards and encourages exploratory improvement toward a higher reward-defined ceiling, but sparse and delayed feedback makes early-stage learning much less efficient than OPD. In this paper, we propose ATOD (Annealed Turn-aware On-policy Distillation), a hybrid online distillation algorithm that explicitly exploits this complementarity. (1) ATOD uses an annealed OPD-RL schedule: OPD dominates early training to approach teacher-level behavior, while RL is gradually strengthened to drive reward-based exploration. (2) ATOD introduces Turn-level Disagreement-Uncertainty Reweighting (T-DUR), which softly amplifies high-utility turns and improves dense supervision in long trajectories. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA show that ATOD consistently outperforms competing post-training baselines: across the three student sizes, ATOD improves average success rate by 3.03 points over OPD and 23.62 points over GRPO, while surpassing the corresponding teacher models by 2.16 points.

AI.tldr: ATOD is a hybrid online distillation algorithm combining OPD and RL with an annealed schedule and turn-level reweighting, achieving superior performance over existing methods on long-horizon interactive tasks.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) provides fast early improvement but saturates near teacher performance, while reinforcement learning (RL) offers higher reward ceilings but suffers from sparse feedback and inefficient early learning. The paper aims to exploit the complementarity between OPD and RL for training small language-model agents on long-horizon interactive tasks.
AI.method: (1) An annealed OPD-RL schedule where OPD dominates early training for rapid approach to teacher behavior and RL is gradually strengthened to drive reward-based exploration. (2) Turn-level Disagreement-Uncertainty Reweighting (T-DUR) to amplify high-utility turns and improve dense supervision in long trajectories.
AI.result: On ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, ATOD improves average success rate by 3.03 points over OPD and 23.62 points over GRPO across three student sizes, and surpasses teacher models by 2.16 points.
AI.conclusion: ATOD consistently outperforms competing post-training baselines and even exceeds teacher-level performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of the hybrid OPD-RL approach with turn-level reweighting for training small language-model agents.

title: Quantum Generative Diffusion Model for Real-World Time Series
summary: Generative models have achieved remarkable success in data synthesis, though recent advances driven by increasing model scale have introduced challenges in computational cost and efficiency. Quantum machine learning offers a promising alternative, representing complex data distributions using compact, highly expressive models. Here, we propose QDiffusion-TS, the first quantum generative diffusion model for time series synthesis, and validate it on the IQM quantum processor. The framework extends a classical diffusion architecture by replacing feed-forward components within the denoising transformer with quantum neural networks, yielding a hybrid quantum transformer that reduces the number of trainable parameters in each replaced component by nearly three orders of magnitude. Evaluated on financial time series from Apple and Amazon, the model generates synthetic data that more accurately reproduces the real distributions, reducing Wasserstein distance by approximately 44% relative to its classical counterpart across both datasets. In a downstream forecasting task, augmentation with the generated data improves predictive performance by up to 71% in RMSE over a baseline trained solely on real data. These results show that quantum enhanced architectures can consistently match and frequently surpass classical performance with substantially fewer parameters, establishing a practical framework towards more efficient and scalable data-driven generative modelling.

AI.tldr: QDiffusion-TS is the first quantum generative diffusion model for time series synthesis, validated on a real quantum processor. It replaces feed-forward layers in a denoising transformer with quantum neural networks, reducing parameters by ~1000x per component, and achieves 44% lower Wasserstein distance and up to 71% RMSE improvement in downstream forecasting on financial datasets.
AI.motivation: Classical generative models suffer from high computational costs as scale increases; quantum machine learning offers more compact, expressive models as an alternative.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid quantum-classical diffusion model (QDiffusion-TS) where feed-forward components in a denoising transformer are replaced with quantum neural networks, creating a hybrid quantum transformer with drastically fewer trainable parameters.
AI.result: On Apple and Amazon financial time series, the model reduces Wasserstein distance by ~44% vs. classical counterpart, and augmentation improves downstream forecasting RMSE by up to 71% over baseline.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-enhanced architectures can match or surpass classical performance with far fewer parameters, establishing a practical path toward more efficient and scalable generative modeling.

title: The ARDoCo Tool Landscape: REST API, TraceView, and TraceViz for Architecture Traceability
comment: Accepted at ASE'26 Tools and Datasets
summary: Context and Problem. Software development produces interrelated artifacts like software architecture documentation (SAD), software architecture models (SAMs), and source code, whose relationships are essential for maintenance and consistency checking. However, automatically recovering links between these artifacts (traceability link recovery (TLR)) remains difficult to deploy in practice. Method and Aim. We present an accessible tool landscape for ARDoCo's TLR approaches: the ARDoCo REST API exposes four TLR pipelines (SAD-SAM, SAM-Code, SAD-Code, and SAD-SAM-Code) via HTTP endpoints with asynchronous execution and caching; TraceView is a browser-based frontend with a guided wizard and interactive multi-panel exploration of recovered links and inconsistencies; and TraceViz, which is a VS Code extension that overlays trace links directly onto documentation in the IDE. Results and Conclusion. All three components are publicly deployed and usable. A preliminary study for TraceViz's in-IDE visualization confirmed that it improves developer comprehension during software understanding tasks. The tool landscape makes state-of-the-art TLR accessible to architects, developers, and tool integrators. Video. We provide a screencast of our ARDoCo Tool Landscape and how it is used here: https://youtu.be/IOTEPZQ3tVs

AI.tldr: ARDoCo provides a tool landscape (REST API, TraceView browser frontend, TraceViz VS Code extension) for automated traceability link recovery between software architecture documentation, models, and source code, with public deployment and preliminary evidence of improved developer comprehension.
AI.motivation: Software development produces interrelated artifacts (SAD, SAMs, source code) whose relationships are essential for maintenance and consistency checking, but automatically recovering links between them (traceability link recovery, TLR) remains difficult to deploy in practice.
AI.method: The ARDoCo REST API exposes four TLR pipelines (SAD-SAM, SAM-Code, SAD-Code, SAD-SAM-Code) via HTTP endpoints with asynchronous execution and caching; TraceView is a browser-based frontend with a guided wizard and interactive multi-panel exploration; TraceViz is a VS Code extension overlaying trace links onto documentation in the IDE.
AI.result: All three components are publicly deployed and usable. A preliminary study for TraceViz confirmed that its in-IDE visualization improves developer comprehension during software understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: The tool landscape makes state-of-the-art TLR accessible to architects, developers, and tool integrators.

title: MemoBench: Benchmarking World Modeling in Dynamically Changing Environments
summary: Video generation models aspire to simulate dynamic environments, and several benchmarks now evaluate memory consistency across frames. However, most assess consistency only while the target remains in view, and the few that force objects out of view evaluate static scenes where nothing changes during occlusion. To bridge this gap, we introduce MemoBench, a diagnostic benchmark built around the disappear-and-reappear paradigm in dynamically changing environments: a target object undergoes a physical process, disappears from view, and must be correctly recovered in its updated state upon reappearance. We curate 360 ground-truth clips spanning synthetic and real-world scenes, and design an evaluation suite combining automated metrics with VQA-based assessment across four diagnostic pillars. Evaluation of eight state-of-the-art models reveals key insights and open challenges regarding memory consistency under the disappear-and-reappear paradigm.

AI.tldr: MemoBench is a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating video generation models' memory consistency under the disappear-and-reappear paradigm in dynamically changing environments, where objects undergo physical processes while occluded. It includes 360 clips and evaluates 8 models across 4 diagnostic pillars, revealing key challenges.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate memory consistency only while targets remain in view or in static scenes during occlusion. There's a gap in assessing whether models can correctly recover objects in their updated state after they disappear and reappear in dynamically changing environments.
AI.method: The paper curates 360 ground-truth clips spanning synthetic and real-world scenes, designed around the disappear-and-reappear paradigm. The evaluation suite combines automated metrics with VQA-based assessment across four diagnostic pillars. Eight state-of-the-art models are evaluated.
AI.result: Evaluation of eight state-of-the-art video generation models reveals key insights and open challenges regarding memory consistency under the disappear-and-reappear paradigm.
AI.conclusion: MemoBench exposes significant limitations in current video generation models' ability to maintain memory consistency when objects undergo physical processes while occluded, indicating this remains an open challenge.

title: NormAct: A Benchmark for Hidden Social Norm Compliance in Embodied Planning
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed as embodied planners in egocentric environments, where task success requires not only achieving instructed goals but also acting in socially appropriate ways. While explicit goals may render certain actions optimal, implicit social norms often impose hidden constraints. Existing evaluations typically focus on explicit goal achievement or direct norm knowledge, seldom assessing whether planners can infer and apply these hidden constraints within action sequences. We introduce NormAct, a benchmark for embodied social-norm interactions that evaluates plans on Goal Achievement, Norm Compliance, and overall Task Success. NormAct uniquely embeds hidden norms within ordinary tasks, testing whether models can realize them without explicit instruction. Experiments with state-of-the-art MLLMs (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) reveal a significant gap: models achieve explicit goals in 67.3\% of cases, but comply with hidden norms in only 26.4\%. Cue-condition experiments indicate that this gap stems not from a lack of general social knowledge, but from challenges in activating and grounding relevant norms in context. To address this, we propose NormPerceptor, a context-conditioned cue generator that infers scene-relevant norms prior to planning, increasing Task Success from 24.2\% to 46.7\%. Our results underscore the importance of enabling embodied agents to proactively detect hidden norms, ground them in visual evidence, and integrate them as action-planning constraints. Our benchmark is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Caleb196x/NormAct.

AI.tldr: NormAct is a benchmark for evaluating embodied social-norm interactions in MLLMs, revealing a significant gap between explicit goal achievement (67.3%) and hidden norm compliance (26.4%). The proposed NormPerceptor cue generator improves Task Success from 24.2% to 46.7% by proactively inferring and grounding relevant norms before planning.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of MLLMs as embodied planners focus on explicit goal achievement or direct norm knowledge, but rarely assess whether models can infer and apply hidden social norms embedded within ordinary tasks without explicit instruction.
AI.method: The authors introduce NormAct, a benchmark for embodied social-norm interactions that evaluates plans on Goal Achievement, Norm Compliance, and overall Task Success, embedding hidden norms in ordinary tasks. They also propose NormPerceptor, a context-conditioned cue generator that infers scene-relevant norms prior to planning.
AI.result: Models achieve explicit goals in 67.3% of cases but comply with hidden norms in only 26.4%. Cue-condition experiments show the gap stems from challenges in activating and grounding relevant norms, not lack of social knowledge. NormPerceptor increases Task Success from 24.2% to 46.7%.
AI.conclusion: Enabling embodied agents to proactively detect hidden norms, ground them in visual evidence, and integrate them as action-planning constraints is crucial for socially appropriate behavior in egocentric environments.

title: hia-gat: A Heterogeneous Interaction-Aware Graph Attention Network For Frame-Level Traffic Conflict Risk Prediction On Freeways
summary: This paper formulates frame-level freeway risk assessment as a multi-agent scene graph-level binary classification problem, where each video or trajectory frame is labeled risky if any TTC- or PET-based conflict violates a specified severity threshold. We construct a relation-aware graph per frame with vehicles as nodes and two interaction types as edges: same-lane (longitudinal) and adjacent-lane (lateral), augmented with physics-informed edge features aligned to rear-end and lane-change conflict mechanisms. Building on a structured benchmarking suite of non-graph models and graph baselines, we propose HIA-GAT, a dual-stream heterogeneous graph attention network that processes longitudinal and lateral interactions through dedicated attention pathways and fuses them via a conflict-type-aware gating mechanism with event-level gate supervision derived from SSM conflict attribution. Experiments on the NGSIM I-80 and US-101 freeway datasets across nine TTC and PET threshold configurations show that HIA-GAT achieves the best average risk-ranking performance (AUC 0.835 on I-80 and 0.867 on US-101), with the largest gains on PET-only (lane-change) settings where relational structure is essential. Beyond accuracy, the learned gate provides interpretable per-vehicle attribution of dominant conflict type, supporting actionable, real-time freeway safety monitoring. We show that graph structure is critical for modeling lateral conflict risk, while longitudinal risk can often be captured by non-relational aggregation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes HIA-GAT, a dual-stream heterogeneous graph attention network for frame-level freeway risk assessment, treating interactions as longitudinal (same-lane) and lateral (adjacent-lane) edges with physics-informed features. Evaluated on NGSIM data, it achieves superior AUC (0.835 I-80, 0.867 US-101) and provides interpretable per-vehicle conflict-type attribution via a gating mechanism.
AI.motivation: To improve freeway risk assessment by moving beyond simple threshold-based conflict detection (TTC/PET) to a relational, graph-level classification that captures vehicle-vehicle interaction structure, especially for lateral (lane-change) conflicts where relational context is critical.
AI.method: Formulates risk assessment as multi-agent scene graph binary classification; constructs per-frame graphs with vehicles as nodes, same-lane and adjacent-lane edges, and physics-informed edge features. Proposes HIA-GAT: a dual-stream heterogeneous graph attention network with separate pathways for longitudinal/lateral interactions and a conflict-type-aware gating mechanism supervised by SSM-based conflict attribution.
AI.result: HIA-GAT achieves the best average AUC (0.835 on I-80, 0.867 on US-101) across nine TTC/PET thresholds, with largest gains on PET-only (lane-change) settings. Graph structure is critical for lateral conflict; longitudinal risk can be captured by non-relational models.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based relational modeling is essential for lateral conflict risk assessment, while the proposed gating mechanism offers interpretable, real-time per-vehicle conflict-type attribution for practical freeway safety monitoring.

title: How Humans, Bots, and Agents Communicate About Vulnerabilities in Pull Requests
summary: Developers may reference vulnerabilities in pull request discussions through both explicit identifiers, such as CVEs or GHSAs, and implicit security-related language (e.g., "unauthorized access" or "SQL injection"). Prior work has primarily focused on explicit identifiers, potentially overlooking vulnerability discussions that lack formal references. Bots and coding agents are becoming more common in pull requests, raising new questions about how different accounts communicate about vulnerabilities. In this registered report, we describe our planned study of vulnerability communication in pull requests by humans, bots, and coding agents. Building on the AIDev-pop dataset, we analyze explicit vulnerability references and implicit security-related signals across pull request titles, descriptions, review comments, commit messages, and timeline discussions. We further investigate whether these references are associated with vulnerabilities introduced or fixed in the modified code and how they relate to pull request review activity and outcomes. This study contributes a large-scale empirical investigation of vulnerability communication practices in modern software development.

AI.tldr: This registered report plans to study how humans, bots, and coding agents communicate about vulnerabilities in pull requests, analyzing both explicit identifiers (CVEs/GHSAs) and implicit security-related language across multiple discussion channels, and investigating their relationship with code vulnerabilities and review outcomes.
AI.motivation: Prior work has focused primarily on explicit vulnerability identifiers (e.g., CVEs, GHSAs) in pull request discussions, potentially missing vulnerability discussions that lack formal references. With bots and coding agents becoming more common, there is a need to understand how different types of accounts communicate about vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Using the AIDev-pop dataset, the authors will analyze explicit vulnerability references and implicit security-related signals across pull request titles, descriptions, review comments, commit messages, and timeline discussions. They will also investigate associations with vulnerabilities introduced/fixed in code and links to review activity and outcomes.
AI.result: Not yet available (registered report); the study plans to contribute a large-scale empirical investigation of vulnerability communication practices.
AI.conclusion: This study will provide a comprehensive understanding of vulnerability communication in pull requests across humans, bots, and coding agents, expanding beyond explicit identifiers to include implicit security-related language.

title: Beyond MoCap: Scaling Motion Tokenizers with Synthetic Human Motion for Generative Modeling
summary: Human motion generation models are fundamentally constrained by the limited diversity of motion capture datasets, which predominantly contain common, repetitive actions and fail to cover the long tail of complex human movements, resulting in a restricted motion vocabulary in learned latent representations and poor generalization to rare, compositional, and highly dynamic motions. In this work, we propose a framework for expanding the motion representation space by leveraging large-scale synthetic human motion, introducing a data generation pipeline that produces diverse, physically plausible motion sequences beyond the distribution of existing datasets and integrating it with a redesigned VQ-VAE tokenizer that adapts to this expanded motion space. Unlike conventional tokenizers trained on narrow data distributions, our approach jointly scales both the training distribution and the discrete codebook, enabling the model to capture a significantly richer set of motion primitives. We demonstrate that training with synthetic motion substantially improves the coverage and compositionality of the learned motion vocabulary, leading to consistent gains across motion generation tasks such as text-to-motion and motion continuation, while remaining fully compatible with existing frameworks including MotionGPT. Our results suggest that the primary bottleneck lies in the limited support of the learned motion representation, rather than model architecture alone. Scaling synthetic motion in tandem with representation learning offers a principled path toward more expressive, controllable, and generalizable human motion synthesis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework that uses large-scale synthetic human motion data and a redesigned VQ-VAE tokenizer to expand the motion representation space, improving coverage and compositionality for tasks like text-to-motion and motion continuation.
AI.motivation: Existing motion capture datasets lack diversity, containing mostly common/repetitive actions and failing to cover long-tail complex movements, which limits the motion vocabulary of learned representations and harms generalization to rare, compositional, and dynamic motions.
AI.method: (1) A data generation pipeline producing diverse, physically plausible synthetic motion sequences beyond existing dataset distributions. (2) A redesigned VQ-VAE tokenizer that jointly scales both the training distribution and the discrete codebook to capture a richer set of motion primitives.
AI.result: Training with synthetic motion substantially improves coverage and compositionality of the learned motion vocabulary, yielding consistent gains on text-to-motion and motion continuation tasks, while remaining compatible with existing frameworks like MotionGPT.
AI.conclusion: The primary bottleneck in human motion generation is the limited support of the learned motion representation, not model architecture alone. Scaling synthetic motion alongside representation learning provides a principled path toward more expressive, controllable, and generalizable motion synthesis.

title: Verifiable Geometry Problem Solving: Solver-Driven Autoformalization and Theorem Proposing
summary: Geometry Problem Solving have increasingly adopt the neuro-symbolic paradigm, combining neural intuition with symbolic rigor. However, current frameworks suffer from severe bottlenecks in two core stages: autoformalization, which treats multimodal translation as a static task decoupled from downstream solver compatibility, and theorem prediction, where solvers frequently hit a deductive impasse due to fixed rule libraries. To address these, we propose SD-GPS, a solver-driven framework that treats the symbolic solver as an execution oracle throughout both formalization and deduction. First, Solver-Driven Autoformalization unifies supervised formal-language adaptation and solvability-guided reinforcement learning into a single module built on QwenVL3-2B, making executability the central training signal. Second, Verified Theorem Proposing introduces an impasse-aware agent that proposes local auxiliary lemmas from current proof states, ensuring soundness by filtering all proposals through symbolic verification. Empirical evaluations on Geometry3K and PGPS9K demonstrate that SD-GPS consistently outperforms existing MLLM, neural, and neuro-symbolic methods across standard completion, multiple-choice, and cross-modal reference regimes, proving that closing the loop between multimodal perception and symbolic execution significantly improves geometric reasoning, offering profound insights into how neural agents can be grounded by formal systems to achieve verifiable problem-solving capabilities.

AI.tldr: SD-GPS is a solver-driven neuro-symbolic framework for geometry problem solving that closes the loop between multimodal perception and symbolic execution by using the symbolic solver as an execution oracle for both autoformalization (via supervised adaptation + solvability-guided RL) and theorem proposing (via impasse-aware auxiliary lemma generation with symbolic verification), outperforming existing methods on Geometry3K and PGPS9K.
AI.motivation: Current neuro-symbolic geometry solvers suffer from two core bottlenecks: (1) autoformalization treats multimodal translation as a static task decoupled from downstream solver compatibility, and (2) theorem prediction faces deductive impasse due to fixed rule libraries.
AI.method: SD-GPS introduces two key components: (1) Solver-Driven Autoformalization, which combines supervised formal-language adaptation with solvability-guided reinforcement learning (built on QwenVL3-2B) using executability as the central training signal; (2) Verified Theorem Proposing, an impasse-aware agent that proposes local auxiliary lemmas from current proof states, filtered through symbolic verification for soundness.
AI.result: SD-GPS consistently outperforms existing MLLM, neural, and neuro-symbolic methods across standard completion, multiple-choice, and cross-modal reference regimes on Geometry3K and PGPS9K benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Closing the loop between multimodal perception and symbolic execution significantly improves geometric reasoning, demonstrating how neural agents can be grounded by formal systems to achieve verifiable problem-solving capabilities.

title: PEBS: Per-rater Empirical-Bayes Shrinkage for RLHF Reward-Model Calibration
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Pluralistic Alignment. Code: https://github.com/deadsmash07/pebs-pluralistic
summary: Reward models for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) pool preferences across thousands of annotators and fit one global affine calibrator, collapsing raters with systematically different rating-scale offsets and slopes into a single average-rater fit that does not match any individual annotator. PEBS is a per-rater empirical-Bayes shrinkage estimator: it fits per-rater affine calibrators on a held-out slice of each annotator's ratings and applies Morris-James-Stein empirical-Bayes shrinkage toward the population mean, in closed form and without retraining the reward model. On PRISM, PEBS reduces within-user held-out RMSE by 8.58% over the pooled population-slope baseline. The procedure replicates on PluriHarms harm ratings (Qwen-2.5 base, in-family) with a +9.66% RMSE reduction over the same population-slope baseline. PEBS is a closed-form post-hoc estimator for annotator-specific affine calibration in RLHF reward modeling; it leaves the reward base model unchanged and estimates only the rater-level map used at inference time for new ratings.

AI.tldr: PEBS is a per-rater empirical-Bayes shrinkage estimator that improves RLHF reward modeling by fitting individual affine calibrators for each annotator and applying James-Stein shrinkage toward the population mean, achieving 8-10% RMSE reduction without retraining the reward model.
AI.motivation: Standard RLHF reward models pool preferences across thousands of annotators with different rating-scale biases (offsets and slopes) into a single global affine calibrator, which does not match any individual annotator's judgment patterns.
AI.method: PEBS fits per-rater affine calibrators on held-out slices of each annotator's ratings, then applies Morris-James-Stein empirical-Bayes shrinkage toward the population mean in closed form, without retraining the reward model. It operates as a post-hoc estimator that only modifies the rater-level map at inference time.
AI.result: On PRISM, PEBS reduces within-user held-out RMSE by 8.58% over the pooled population-slope baseline. On PluriHarms harm ratings (Qwen-2.5 base, in-family), it achieves a 9.66% RMSE reduction over the same baseline.
AI.conclusion: PEBS provides an effective, closed-form post-hoc solution for annotator-specific affine calibration in RLHF reward modeling, leaving the base reward model unchanged while improving prediction accuracy for individual raters.

title: CrossLangFuzzer: Differential Testing of Cross-Language JVM Compilers
summary: Modern JVM software increasingly integrates multiple programming languages, such as Java, Kotlin, Groovy, and Scala, within a single application. Supporting such interoperability requires JVM compilers to perform cross-language compilation while reconciling subtle semantic differences across language boundaries. Errors in this process can lead to critical miscompilations, yet existing compiler testing techniques focus exclusively on isolated, singlelanguage compilation. To address this gap, we present CrossLangFuzzer, the first differential testing framework for cross-language JVM compilation. CrossLangFuzzer leverages the Kotlin compiler's unified intermediate representation (IR) to synthesize cross-language test programs. It further applies seven mutation operators to diversify generated test programs and improve bug-finding capability. Evaluated on the latest versions of five major JVM compilers, CrossLangFuzzer uncovered 32 confirmed bugs, including 15 in Kotlin, 4 in Groovy, 7 in Scala 3, 2 in Scala 2, and 4 in Java. CrossLangFuzzer is open-source at https://github.com/XYZboom/CrossLangFuzzer

AI.tldr: CrossLangFuzzer is a differential testing framework for cross-language JVM compilation that synthesizes multi-language test programs using Kotlin's IR and applies mutation operators, uncovering 32 confirmed bugs across five major JVM compilers.
AI.motivation: Existing compiler testing techniques focus exclusively on single-language compilation, ignoring the critical interoperability challenges when multiple JVM languages (Java, Kotlin, Groovy, Scala) are combined in one application, where semantic differences can lead to miscompilations.
AI.method: CrossLangFuzzer uses Kotlin compiler's unified intermediate representation (IR) to synthesize cross-language test programs and applies seven mutation operators to diversify test cases, enabling differential testing across JVM compilers.
AI.result: Evaluated on five major JVM compilers, CrossLangFuzzer found 32 confirmed bugs: 15 in Kotlin, 4 in Groovy, 7 in Scala 3, 2 in Scala 2, and 4 in Java.
AI.conclusion: Cross-language compilation bugs are prevalent across JVM compilers, and the proposed differential testing framework effectively detects them, filling a crucial gap in compiler testing.

title: Understanding Cross-Rig Generalization in Automotive Perception: a Multi-Rig Benchmark and Rig Variation Metrics
summary: Camera-based perception systems for autonomous driving are typically developed and evaluated using fixed sensor rigs, while real-world vehicle fleets exhibit substantial variation in camera placement, orientation, field of view, and camera count. This mismatch introduces a cross-rig domain gap in which only the geometric observation process changes. To study this effect under controlled conditions, we introduce Plentiful CARLA Camera Rigs, a benchmark that renders identical driving scenes under 14 systematically designed camera rigs. This setup enables direct analysis of cross-rig generalization without confounding changes in scene content or appearance. Using the benchmark, we analyze cross-rig transfer behavior of representative multi-view perception architectures and observe substantial performance shifts induced by geometric rig variation. To facilitate structured analysis, we further introduce two calibration-based descriptors derived from rig metadata: Rig Variance, capturing internal rig diversity, and Rig Contrastive Distance, measuring geometric discrepancy between rigs. Our experiments show that geometric rig differences strongly correlate with relative cross-rig performance shifts and that Rig Contrastive Distance provides a reliable proxy for ranking transfer difficulty between sensor rigs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Plentiful CARLA Camera Rigs, a benchmark with 14 systematically designed camera rigs to study cross-rig generalization in autonomous driving perception. It finds that geometric rig variations cause substantial performance shifts, and proposes two calibration-based descriptors (Rig Variance and Rig Contrastive Distance) that reliably predict cross-rig transfer difficulty.
AI.motivation: Real-world autonomous driving fleets exhibit substantial variation in camera placement, orientation, FOV, and camera count, but perception systems are typically developed and evaluated using fixed sensor rigs. This mismatch introduces a cross-rig domain gap that is poorly understood, motivating a controlled study of how geometric rig variation alone affects perception performance.
AI.method: The authors create the Plentiful CARLA Camera Rigs benchmark, rendering identical driving scenes under 14 systematically designed camera rigs. They analyze cross-rig transfer behavior of representative multi-view perception architectures and introduce two calibration-based descriptors: Rig Variance (capturing internal rig diversity) and Rig Contrastive Distance (measuring geometric discrepancy between rigs).
AI.result: Substantial performance shifts are induced by geometric rig variation. Geometric rig differences strongly correlate with relative cross-rig performance shifts, and Rig Contrastive Distance provides a reliable proxy for ranking transfer difficulty between sensor rigs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed benchmark and descriptors enable structured analysis of cross-rig generalization. Rig Contrastive Distance can serve as a predictive tool for estimating transfer difficulty between different camera rig configurations.

title: RelBall: Relation Ball with Quaternion Rotation for Knowledge Graph Completion
summary: Real-world knowledge graphs are often incomplete, lacking many valid facts. Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) aims to predict missing links using known triples, thereby enhancing graph coverage. A key challenge is modeling diverse relational patterns such as symmetry, antisymmetry, inversion, composition and semantic hierarchy. Existing models such as RotatE can capture symmetric, antisymmetric, inverse, and commutative composition patterns, yet struggle with non-commutative composition. Rotate3D addresses this by introducing non-commutativity via three-dimensional rotations, but still fails to capture the semantic hierarchies prevalent in knowledge graphs. Moreover, both models cannot effectively model one-to-many relations. To overcome these limitations, we propose RelBall, which extends Rotate3D with two innovations. First, our model introduces modulus transformation to model hierarchies, driving abstract concepts toward smaller moduli and concrete instances toward larger ones. Second, it introduces a tail-centric relation ball to model one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations. RelBall offers the following advantages: (1) coverage of all relational patterns, including the ones mentioned above; (2) an interpretable hierarchical representation where the modulus directly reflect semantic levels; (3) support for one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate RelBall's competitive link prediction performance against various baselines.

AI.tldr: RelBall is a Knowledge Graph Completion model that extends Rotate3D by adding modulus transformation for modeling semantic hierarchies and a tail-centric relation ball for handling one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations, achieving competitive link prediction performance.
AI.motivation: Existing KGC models like RotatE and Rotate3D fail to capture semantic hierarchies and cannot effectively model one-to-many relations, limiting their ability to cover all relational patterns in knowledge graphs.
AI.method: RelBall extends Rotate3D with two innovations: (1) modulus transformation to model hierarchies, where abstract concepts have smaller moduli and concrete instances have larger moduli; (2) a tail-centric relation ball to model one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations.
AI.result: RelBall achieves competitive link prediction performance on multiple datasets against various baselines.
AI.conclusion: RelBall covers all relational patterns (symmetry, antisymmetry, inversion, composition, hierarchy, and cardinality relations), provides interpretable hierarchical representations, and supports diverse relation types.

title: Retroactive Advantage Correction: Closed-Form V-Trace Bias Correction for Delay-Aware RLHF
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback (RLxF). Code: https://github.com/deadsmash07/rac-rlxf-code
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) in production does not always have a synchronous reward signal. Code-execution verifiers, slow judge ensembles, and queued human review can return several gradient steps after the rollout that produced them, breaking the synchronous-reward assumption underlying standard PPO. We address this gap with Retroactive Advantage Correction (RAC): each pending slow completion is queued, aged through a non-negative kernel, and reinjected as a clipped residual into the next optimiser step's advantage. We prove that under an unbiased clipped importance ratio, the cumulative RAC correction is exactly unbiased when the effective delay kernel reinjects all of its mass, and carries a bias linear in the unreinjected fraction otherwise; at the no-delay identity kernel it reduces to V-trace. On a tabular Markov decision process (MDP) proof-of-concept, RAC reduces the closed-form policy bias by up to 47.9x at the two-slow-channel configuration, beating wait-for-slow at lower wall-clock cost. RAC integrates with PPO and GRPO through a two-line reward-manager patch.

AI.tldr: RAC enables PPO/GRPO to handle asynchronous (slow) reward signals from code-execution verifiers, judge ensembles, or human review by queuing, aging, and reinjecting delayed rewards as clipped residual advantages, achieving unbiased updates and up to 47.9x lower policy bias in tabular MDPs.
AI.motivation: Production RLHF often encounters asynchronous reward signals (e.g., slow code-execution verifiers, judge ensembles, queued human review) that break the synchronous-reward assumption of standard PPO, creating a gap in existing online RL algorithms.
AI.method: Retroactive Advantage Correction (RAC): queue each pending slow completion, age it through a non-negative kernel, and reinject it as a clipped residual into the next optimizer step's advantage. Proves unbiasedness under unbiased clipped importance ratio with full kernel mass reinjection, and linear bias otherwise; reduces to V-trace at identity kernel. Integrates via two-line reward-manager patch.
AI.result: In a tabular MDP proof-of-concept, RAC reduces closed-form policy bias by up to 47.9x at the two-slow-channel configuration, outperforming wait-for-slow with lower wall-clock cost.
AI.conclusion: RAC effectively addresses asynchronous reward delays in RLHF, providing theoretically grounded bias guarantees and practical integration with PPO/GRPO.

title: Humanizing Automatically Generated Unit Test Suites with LLM-Based Refactoring
summary: Search-based test generation tools such as EvoSuite produce compilable and high-coverage unit tests at scale, but their suites are often hard to read and maintain. LLMs can generate more natural tests, yet direct generation remains brittle, with compilation rates of only 51-78% in our study. We introduce TestHumanizer, a hybrid SBST+LLM approach that uses LLMs as controlled refactoring layers over compilable SBST suites to improve naming, structure, and developer-oriented clarity while preserving behavior and compilation validity. We evaluate TestHumanizer on 350 classes from Defects4J and SF110. EvoSuite generates 15 suites per class, and each suite is refactored under three context configurations using gpt-4o and mistral-large-2407, yielding 31,500 refactorings. TestHumanizer reaches 88-98% compilation rates, close to EvoSuite's 100% baseline and clearly above direct LLM generation. Structural coverage is largely preserved, typically within 1-2 percentage points, and 86-95% of refactorings satisfy a composite faithful-refactoring threshold. Refactored suites also improve predicted readability, reduce control-flow and cognitive complexity, and mitigate structural smells. The summary-based setting offers the most robust trade-off, while long code-centric prompts are more prone to hallucination-induced failures. A developer study on 30 classes and 444 test methods confirms significant gains in perceived readability and willingness to adopt, with Wilcoxon p less than 0.01 and substantial inter-rater agreement. Overall, LLMs are most effective not as standalone generators but as validation-gated refinement layers over robust SBST outputs.

AI.tldr: TestHumanizer combines SBST (EvoSuite) with LLMs as a controlled refactoring layer to improve test readability and naturalness while preserving compilation validity and coverage, outperforming direct LLM generation on 31,500 refactorings across 350 classes.
AI.motivation: Search-based test generation (SBST) tools produce high-coverage tests that are hard to read/maintain, while LLM-generated tests are more natural but brittle (51-78% compilation). The goal is to combine both approaches to get readable AND compilable tests.
AI.method: TestHumanizer uses LLMs (gpt-4o, mistral-large-2407) as controlled refactoring layers over EvoSuite-generated test suites, modifying naming, structure, and clarity under three context configurations (summary-based, code-centric, etc.) while preserving behavior and compilation validity.
AI.result: 88-98% compilation rates (vs 100% EvoSuite baseline), coverage preserved within 1-2 percentage points, 86-95% faithful-refactoring threshold satisfaction. Developer study on 444 test methods shows significant gains in readability and willingness to adopt (p<0.01, substantial inter-rater agreement).
AI.conclusion: LLMs are most effective not as standalone generators but as validation-gated refinement layers on top of robust SBST outputs; summary-based context offers the best trade-off, while long code-centric prompts are more prone to hallucination failures.

title: Radar Guided Camera Verification for Automatic Emergency Braking Rethinking Object Detection in Radar Camera Fusion
summary: Radar camera fusion is widely used in Automatic Emergency Braking AEB systems because radar provides reliable range and velocity measurements while cameras provide a proper visual confirmation of the objects . Most of the deployed systems perform this confirmation using computationally intensive object detectors. However, if the radar has already localized a target, the camera may only need to verify the obstacles presence rather than solving a full problem by identifying the object. Our work proposes a radar scoped edge density gate that performs obstacle verification within radar guided image regions of interest. This method requires no training data, model weights, or GPU acceleration and was integrated into a complete radar camera fusion AEB system with brake by wire actuation. Evaluated on a real instrumented vehicle across 72 driving sessions and 131,603 camera frames, the proposed approach reduced the camera search space by up to 98.7 percentage, achieved a mean processing latency of 0.121 ms per ROI, an AUC of 0.898, and a recall of 0.994. Across 33 staged threat scenarios, the complete AEB system recorded zero missed brake events.

AI.tldr: Proposes a radar-scoped edge density gate for low-latency obstacle verification in radar-camera fusion AEB systems, requiring no training data or GPU, reducing camera search space by 98.7% and achieving zero missed braking events across 33 staged scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing radar-camera fusion AEB systems rely on computationally intensive object detectors for visual confirmation. Since radar already localizes targets, the camera only needs to verify presence rather than full identification — current approaches are unnecessarily heavy.
AI.method: Proposes a radar-scoped edge density gate that performs obstacle verification within radar-guided image regions of interest (ROIs). The method requires no training data, model weights, or GPU acceleration. Integrated into a complete radar-camera fusion AEB system with brake-by-wire actuation.
AI.result: Evaluated on a real instrumented vehicle: 72 driving sessions, 131,603 camera frames. Reduced camera search space by 98.7%, mean processing latency 0.121 ms per ROI, AUC 0.898, recall 0.994. Across 33 staged threat scenarios, zero missed brake events.
AI.conclusion: The radar-scoped edge density gate is an efficient, training-free alternative for obstacle verification in AEB systems, drastically reducing computational cost and latency while maintaining high reliability and safety performance.

title: Lifted Causal Inference
comment: Accepted to the Annals of Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence journal
summary: Lifted inference exploits indistinguishabilities in probabilistic graphical models by using a representative for indistinguishable objects, thereby speeding up query answering while maintaining exact answers. In this article, we show how lifting can be applied to efficiently compute causal effects in relational domains. More specifically, we introduce parametric causal factor graphs (PCFGs) to incorporate causal knowledge in lifted models and give a formal semantics of interventions therein. We further present the Lifted Causal Inference (LCI) algorithm to compute causal effects on a lifted level, thereby drastically speeding up causal inference compared to propositional inference, e.g., in causal Bayesian networks. In addition, we present partially directed parametric causal factor graphs (PD-PCFGs) as a generalisation of PCFGs to handle partial causal knowledge and extend LCI to perform lifted causal inference in a PD-PCFG, thereby extending the applicability of lifted causal inference to a broader range of models requiring less prior knowledge about causal relationships.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces parametric causal factor graphs (PCFGs) and the Lifted Causal Inference (LCI) algorithm to perform efficient causal inference on lifted (aggregated) representations in relational probabilistic models, along with a partial-knowledge extension (PD-PCFGs).
AI.motivation: Efficiently computing causal effects in relational domains is challenging due to the combinatorial explosion of propositional representations. Lifted inference, which uses representatives for indistinguishable objects, has been successful for probabilistic query answering but not yet applied to causal inference. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The authors introduce Parametric Causal Factor Graphs (PCFGs) to incorporate causal knowledge into lifted models and provide a formal semantics for interventions. They propose the Lifted Causal Inference (LCI) algorithm that computes causal effects at the lifted level. They further extend this with Partially Directed PCFGs (PD-PCFGs) to handle partial causal knowledge, and adapt LCI accordingly.
AI.result: LCI drastically speeds up causal inference compared to propositional causal Bayesian networks. PD-PCFGs extend applicability to models with incomplete causal knowledge, requiring less prior information about causal relationships.
AI.conclusion: Lifted causal inference is feasible and significantly more efficient than propositional approaches, and the partial-knowledge extension broadens the range of real-world models where this technique can be applied.

title: Global Explanations for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting Models via $K$-Order Markov Approximations
comment: Accepted at the Workshop on Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2026)
summary: While many explainable AI (XAI) methods have been proposed, most are not designed for time-series forecasting models and often rely on the implicit assumption that timestamp features are independent. This assumption ignores the fundamental property of temporal dependence and can lead to explanations that violate the sequential and causal structure of the data. We introduce \textsc{KARMA}, a method for explaining time-series predictors by constructing a Markov surrogate model that captures the temporal dependencies learned by the predictor. Our approach revolves around three main aspects: identifying the minimal history length $K$ that is predictively sufficient for the model, estimating the best-fitting $K$-order Markov transition kernel from the discretized history space, and a five-level global explanation hierarchy that can be derived from the Markov transition kernel, which we illustrate using real-world weather data (Beijing PM 2.5). We also certify using complex synthetic data with known true causal edges that KARMA (i) recovers the data causal structure as learned by the model via a controlled experiment and (ii) identifies temporal dependencies better than established attribution methods such as TimeSHAP.

AI.tldr: KARMA is an XAI method for time-series forecasting that builds a Markov surrogate model to explain temporal dependencies, outperforming methods like TimeSHAP on synthetic and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Existing XAI methods assume timestamp features are independent, ignoring temporal dependence and causal structure in time-series data, leading to explanations that violate sequential and causal properties.
AI.method: KARMA constructs a K-order Markov surrogate model by (1) identifying the minimal sufficient history length K, (2) estimating the best-fitting K-order Markov transition kernel from discretized history space, and (3) deriving a five-level global explanation hierarchy from the kernel.
AI.result: On synthetic data with known causal edges, KARMA recovers the causal structure learned by the model and identifies temporal dependencies better than TimeSHAP. It is also demonstrated on Beijing PM2.5 weather data.
AI.conclusion: KARMA effectively explains time-series predictors by capturing temporal dependencies through a Markov surrogate, addressing a key limitation of prior XAI methods.

title: Govern the Repository, Not the Agent: Measuring Ecosystem-Level Risk in AI-Native Software
summary: Autonomous coding agents now open and merge pull requests in shared repositories at scale, and the field evaluates them the way it has always evaluated components, one agent at a time, on isolated benchmark tasks. Yet agents that each pass their own tests still leave repositories that accumulate problems no single contribution accounts for. We ask whether this problem belongs to the individual agent or to the repository where it accumulates. We study integration friction, the cost of integrating a contribution into a codebase that other contributors are concurrently changing. Across more than 930,000 agent-authored pull requests, we measure how much of the variation in friction stays with the repository after the contribution, its author, its size, and its agent are accounted for. About half does, and it survives full controls. In the same repositories, agent-authored contributions concentrate this repository-level friction roughly twice as much as human ones (intraclass correlation 0.30 versus 0.16), a gap that holds after controlling for codebase size, age, task shape, process maturity, and merge path. The risk is a property of the ecosystem, not the agent. AI-native software is therefore better measured and governed at the ecosystem level than one agent at a time.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes integration friction across 930,000+ agent-authored pull requests and finds that about half of the variation in friction is attributable to the repository, not the individual agent, and that agent contributions concentrate repository-level friction roughly twice as much as human contributions (ICC 0.30 vs 0.16). The authors argue that risks in AI-native software development are ecosystem-level properties, not agent-level ones.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation practices assess autonomous coding agents one at a time on isolated benchmarks, but repositories accumulate problems that no single contribution accounts for. The paper asks whether this problem belongs to the individual agent or to the repository where it accumulates.
AI.method: The authors study "integration friction" (cost of integrating a contribution into a concurrently changing codebase) across over 930,000 agent-authored PRs, measuring how much variation in friction stays with the repository after controlling for contribution, author, size, and agent.
AI.result: About half of the variation in integration friction is attributable to the repository level and survives full controls. Agent-authored contributions concentrate repository-level friction roughly twice as much as human ones (intraclass correlation 0.30 vs 0.16), holding across multiple control variables.
AI.conclusion: The risk is a property of the ecosystem, not the agent. AI-native software should be measured and governed at the ecosystem level rather than one agent at a time.

title: Perceptual 3D Simulation With Physical World Modeling
comment: Published as a conference paper at CVPR 2026
summary: Predicting how a scene will evolve after a desired 3D transformation from images is a central goal in vision, graphics, and robotics. Yet unlike ideal simulators with full access to 3D geometry and dynamics, real world systems must rely on perceptual inputs and local actions that are inherently partial and incomplete. In this work, we present P3Sim, a physical world modeling system that simulates future scene states under both partial observations and incomplete 3D transformation signals. P3Sim is composed of three interacting components: a learned physical world model, a geometric conditioning module, and a persistent scene memory. The world model interprets perception as probabilistic inference over multimodal scene variables, providing predictions of the distributions of any scene variable conditioned on any combination of others. The geometric conditioning module provides a partial 3D transform signal for conditioning the world model at inference time. The persistent scene memory integrates predictions over time, enabling online updates and consistency under uncertainty. By combining learned inference with explicit geometric structure, P3Sim balances data-driven flexibility with built-in inductive bias. This design yields a flexible perceptual simulator that generalizes across diverse 3D transformation tasks, such as novel view synthesis, object manipulation, and dynamic scene prediction, advancing toward general purpose 3D scene understanding and transformation.

AI.tldr: P3Sim is a physical world modeling system that simulates future scene states under partial observations and incomplete 3D transformation signals, using a learned world model, geometric conditioning, and persistent scene memory to predict scene evolutions for tasks like view synthesis, object manipulation, and dynamic prediction.
AI.motivation: Real-world systems must rely on perceptual inputs and local actions that are inherently partial and incomplete, unlike ideal simulators with full access to 3D geometry and dynamics. The goal is to predict scene evolution after 3D transformations from images despite these limitations.
AI.method: P3Sim consists of three components: (1) a learned physical world model that performs probabilistic inference over multimodal scene variables, (2) a geometric conditioning module that provides partial 3D transform signals at inference time, and (3) a persistent scene memory that integrates predictions over time for online updates and consistency.
AI.result: The system generalizes across diverse 3D transformation tasks including novel view synthesis, object manipulation, and dynamic scene prediction.
AI.conclusion: Combining learned inference with explicit geometric structure balances data-driven flexibility with built-in inductive bias, advancing toward general-purpose 3D scene understanding and transformation.

title: JD Oxygen AI Item Center (Oxygen AIIC) V1: An Industrial-Scale LLM/VLM-Centric Solution for Item Understanding, Management, and Applications
summary: JD.com, one of the world's largest e-commerce platforms, serves over 700 million active users and millions of merchants, with a catalog of tens of billions of SKUs. At this scale, high-quality, structured item knowledge underpins a better consumer experience, lower management costs, and higher operational efficiency-yet producing and serving it poses three industrial-scale challenges: fast-emerging concepts, high-quality knowledge production for massive SKUs, and diverse downstream requirements. To address these challenges, we present the JD Oxygen AI Item Center (Oxygen AIIC), an industrial-scale platform built on LLMs/VLMs for item-knowledge production and service. Oxygen AIIC is built around four core pillars: (i) ontology engineering driven by efficient human-AI collaboration, which supports the dynamic evolution and agile expansion of an ontology with millions of entries; (ii) a "Semantic Search then Discrimination"(S2D) knowledge identification architecture that, combined with throughput improvement strategies, enables scalable, extensible, and high-throughput AI Item Library production for tens of billions of SKUs; (iii) self-evolving item-understanding LLMs/VLMs that improve in a stable and controllable manner, enabling knowledge production with 94.2% precision and 82.8% recall; and (iv) a unified item tunnel that serves as the data and service hub. Oxygen AIIC now covers tens of thousands of JD categories and processes hundreds of millions of item updates per day on Huawei Ascend NPUs. It has accumulated hundreds of billions of item-knowledge assets. Deployed across core business scenarios-including search, recommendation, operations, category planning-Oxygen AIIC has delivered measurable gains at scale. Search-traffic coverage reaches 80.4%, item-information quality issues drop by 37%, the automated fill rate of core attributes during item listing exceeds 80%.

AI.tldr: JD.com presents Oxygen AIIC, an industrial-scale platform using LLMs/VLMs on Huawei Ascend NPUs for item-knowledge production and service, covering tens of thousands of categories, processing hundreds of millions of daily item updates, and achieving 94.2% precision, 82.8% recall, 80.4% search-traffic coverage, 37% reduction in quality issues, and 80%+ automated attribute fill rate.
AI.motivation: To address three industrial-scale challenges at JD.com: fast-emerging concepts, high-quality knowledge production for massive SKUs (tens of billions), and diverse downstream requirements.
AI.method: Four core pillars: (i) ontology engineering with human-AI collaboration for dynamic evolution; (ii) Semantic Search then Discrimination (S2D) knowledge identification with throughput optimization; (iii) self-evolving item-understanding LLMs/VLMs for stable improvement; (iv) unified item tunnel as data/service hub. Deployed on Huawei Ascend NPUs.
AI.result: 94.2% precision, 82.8% recall, 80.4% search-traffic coverage, 37% reduction in item-information quality issues, 80%+ automated fill rate of core attributes during item listing, covering tens of thousands of JD categories with hundreds of billions of item-knowledge assets.
AI.conclusion: Oxygen AIIC successfully delivers measurable gains across search, recommendation, operations, and category planning at industrial scale, demonstrating the effectiveness of LLM/VLM-based knowledge production for e-commerce.

title: Training Observable Control Policies to Expose Agent State Through Actions
summary: Physical or operational constraints often impose communications limitations on autonomous agents. Such limitations complicate monitoring or multiagent coordination. Even when strong communications are absent, some information may still be available. The remainder of the relevant agent state may be reconstructed via estimation. The actions taken by an agent are a potential source of information -- as the agent interacts with the environment, these actions may be observed even in the absence of explicit communication. We investigate using actions to estimate the state of an agent, using reinforcement learning to develop policies which make the estimation problem more tractable. Policy observability is encouraged through the training reward and is analyzed using simulation of the trained agent. In an aircraft tracking problem a policy with enhanced observability is found that has minimal impact on nominal task performance.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how an agent's actions can serve as implicit communication signals for state estimation in constrained environments. Using reinforcement learning, the authors develop policies that enhance "policy observability" — making state estimation easier from observed actions — and demonstrate in an aircraft tracking problem that this can be achieved with minimal degradation to primary task performance.
AI.motivation: Physical or operational constraints often limit communications between autonomous agents, complicating monitoring and multi-agent coordination. Even without explicit communication, some information may still be available, and the authors aim to leverage this to reconstruct agent state via estimation.
AI.method: The authors use reinforcement learning to develop policies that make the state estimation problem more tractable by encouraging policy observability through the training reward. The observability is then analyzed via simulation of the trained agent.
AI.result: In an aircraft tracking problem, the authors find a policy with enhanced observability that has minimal impact on nominal task performance.
AI.conclusion: Actions taken by an agent are a potential source of information for state estimation in the absence of explicit communication, and reinforcement learning can be used to develop policies that enhance observability without significantly degrading primary task performance.

title: DeLux: Cross-Modal Local Artifact Restoration in Video Using Neuromorphic Data
summary: Conventional RGB cameras suffer from lighting artifacts such as flare, glare, flicker, and overexposure, leading to irrecoverable information loss that necessitates computational restoration. However, existing approaches treat these problems in isolation, failing to recover structural details completely obscured by complex spatially discrete image degradations. In this paper, we propose a novel cross-modal restoration paradigm and present DeLux, a modular proof-of-concept pipeline that leverages neuromorphic event streams as a structural prior to guide the targeted detection and inpainting of lighting artifacts in RGB video. Validation on synthetic benchmarks and real-world automotive footage demonstrates that DeLux effectively suppresses local artifacts and restores affected regions. The proposed approach outperforms existing RGB-only baselines and event-guided HDR models, achieving an average MS-SSIM of over 0.99 across all artifact types and demonstrating up to an 88% reduction in artifact severity in real-world automotive footage. The synthetic artifact generation tools and curated real-world evaluation datasets are made publicly available to foster future research on cross-modal restoration.

AI.tldr: DeLux is a neuromorphic event-guided pipeline that uses event streams as structural priors to detect and inpaint lighting artifacts (flare, glare, flicker, overexposure) in RGB video, achieving >0.99 MS-SSIM and up to 88% artifact reduction on real-world automotive footage.
AI.motivation: Conventional RGB cameras suffer from lighting artifacts (flare, glare, flicker, overexposure) causing irrecoverable information loss. Existing methods treat these problems in isolation and fail to recover structural details completely obscured by complex spatially discrete degradations.
AI.method: Proposes a cross-modal restoration paradigm called DeLux, a modular proof-of-concept pipeline that leverages neuromorphic event streams as a structural prior to guide targeted detection and inpainting of lighting artifacts in RGB video.
