AI.method: TRUST introduces three core modules: (1) Cross-Frequency Collaborative Adapter (CFCA) for discriminative spatial feature extraction under speckle corruption via mutual constraints between low/high-frequency components; (2) Multi-Granularity Motion-Aware (MGMA) module combining local temporal convolutions with motion-prior-guided global self-attention to capture intra-view patterns and inter-view transitions; (3) Visual Query Semantic Aggregation (VQSA) for adaptive visual-textual alignment via dynamically generated text prototypes conditioned on visual features.
AI.result: On in-house ultrasound trauma datasets, TRUST outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 9.63% with superior computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: TRUST demonstrates that explicitly modeling fine-grained spatiotemporal variations through scan-aware PEIVTL can significantly improve the reliability and generalizability of ultrasound video understanding, offering a promising direction for automated trauma triage.

title: WattLayer: Get Layers Right to Estimate Inference Energy of Neural Networks
summary: The widespread adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has led to increasing concerns about energy consumption, yet there is a lack of standardized methodologies to accurately estimate AI inference energy consumption, particularly across various tasks and architectures. In this study, we propose a task independent, layer-wise energy estimation model for AI architectures. Our model is evaluated on a large dataset of more than 100,000 layers for 295 neural network architectures across 3 widely-used tasks and 3 distinct hardware platforms. Our approach achieves a median error of 19.6%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. We further show that layer-wise decomposition generalize to new tasks without complete retraining, by leveraging shared layers across architectures. It offer tools, insights and a precise methodology to empower stakeholders in designing energy-efficient AI systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a task-independent, layer-wise energy estimation model for AI inference, achieving 19.6% median error across 295 architectures on 3 tasks and 3 hardware platforms.
AI.motivation: Lack of standardized methodologies to accurately estimate AI inference energy consumption across diverse tasks and architectures.
AI.method: A task-independent, layer-wise energy estimation model evaluated on over 100,000 layers from 295 neural network architectures across 3 tasks and 3 hardware platforms.
AI.result: Achieves median error of 19.6%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods; layer-wise decomposition generalizes to new tasks without complete retraining via shared layers.
AI.conclusion: Provides tools, insights, and precise methodology to empower stakeholders in designing energy-efficient AI systems.

title: MindFlow: Harmonizing Cognitive Semantics and Acoustic Dynamics for Facial Animation Generation in Dyadic Conversations
summary: Generating lifelike facial animation for dyadic conversations requires reconciling high-level cognitive intent with precise low-level motor reflexes, yet existing methods fall short in the semantic understanding of dialogue context and in precise dynamic control. In this paper, we propose MindFlow, a dual-pathway generative framework inspired by the Ventral-Dorsal pathway model in neuroscience, which decouples generation into two collaborative streams, thereby harmonizing deep semantic reasoning with fine-grained control. In the Ventral module, we transform the conventional Sentence-Action approach into a novel Chunk-State approach that models raw acoustic streams as a context-aware, evolving emotional state chain, capturing subtle paralinguistic nuances and mid-utterance emotional shifts missed by sentence-level modeling. The Dorsal module features a conditional autoregressive flow matching network for high-fidelity facial motion, driven by high-frequency acoustic cues and modulated by emotion states, plus a Selective Acoustic Injector for adaptive audio gating to ensure robustness in talking-and-listening dynamics without interference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MindFlow achieves superior semantic appropriateness and motion naturalness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: MindFlow is a dual-pathway generative framework inspired by the Ventral-Dorsal pathway model in neuroscience for dyadic conversational facial animation. It decouples generation into two streams: a Ventral module using a Chunk-State approach for context-aware emotional state modeling, and a Dorsal module with conditional autoregressive flow matching and a Selective Acoustic Injector for high-fidelity facial motion control. The method achieves superior semantic appropriateness and motion naturalness over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for generating lifelike facial animation in dyadic conversations fall short in both semantic understanding of dialogue context and precise dynamic control, failing to reconcile high-level cognitive intent with low-level motor reflexes.
AI.method: MindFlow employs a dual-pathway generative framework inspired by the Ventral-Dorsal pathway model from neuroscience. The Ventral module uses a Chunk-State approach (vs. conventional Sentence-Action) to model raw acoustic streams as a context-aware emotional state chain. The Dorsal module features a conditional autoregressive flow matching network for high-fidelity facial motion driven by high-frequency acoustic cues and modulated by emotion states, plus a Selective Acoustic Injector for adaptive audio gating in talking-and-listening dynamics.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that MindFlow achieves superior semantic appropriateness and motion naturalness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MindFlow framework effectively harmonizes deep semantic reasoning with fine-grained dynamic control for facial animation in dyadic conversations, outperforming existing methods.

title: Applicability of memorization indicators for early spotting of overfitting while recalibrating sEMG-decoders on low sample sizes
summary: Deep learning models for surface electromyography (sEMG) can benefit substantially from subject-specific (re-)calibration, since no sufficiently large and diverse datasets are available to train fully generic decoders. However, for user acceptance, the number of repetitions that can realistically be collected during calibration is severely limited, which increases the risk of overfitting and, in extreme cases, can even degrade performance compared to the uncalibrated model. Classical overfitting indicators such as validation performance and regularization with early stopping are difficult to apply in this low-sample regime, as they require additional held-out data that is rarely available in practical calibration scenarios. In this work, we investigate a recently proposed class of memorization indicators based solely on the activation statistics of rectified linear units (ReLU) in deep neural networks, which can be computed directly from training data without any extra validation set. We conduct a transferlearning experiment on a benchmark sEMG dataset, where a convolutional neural network is first pre-trained on multiple subjects and subsequently fine-tuned on individual users using only a small number of repetitions. During calibration, we monitor both decoding performance and the activation behaviour of the last hidden layer. Our results provide first evidence that decreases in test accuracy during fine-tuning are ac companied by characteristic changes in activation rates, indicating that activation-based memorization indicators are a promising tool for early spotting of unsuccessful learning in low-sample sEMG calibration settings.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates activation-based memorization indicators from ReLU statistics to detect unsuccessful fine-tuning in low-sample sEMG calibration, showing that drops in test accuracy correlate with characteristic changes in activation rates.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models for sEMG need subject-specific calibration but suffer from limited data, making overfitting detection difficult since validation sets are impractical in low-sample regimes.
AI.method: Transfer learning experiment: CNN pre-trained on multiple subjects, fine-tuned on individual users with few repetitions. Activation statistics of ReLU in the last hidden layer are monitored during calibration without needing a validation set.
AI.result: Decreases in test accuracy during fine-tuning are accompanied by characteristic changes in activation rates, suggesting memorization indicators correlate with overfitting.
AI.conclusion: Activation-based memorization indicators are a promising tool for early detection of unsuccessful learning in low-sample sEMG calibration scenarios.

title: Improving Adversarial Robustness via Activation Amplification and Attenuation
summary: The existence of adversarial attacks is often attributed to the presence of non-robust features in neural networks. While prior defenses reduce their impact via pruning, masking, or feature recalibration, we instead propose to jointly learn to amplify and attenuate these signals through a simple activation scaling mechanism. To this end, we introduce Activation Amplification and Attenuation (A3), a lightweight plug-in module that enhances adversarial robustness with minimal modifications of the activations. A3 dynamically rescales the activations using a learnable mask and a scaling factor derived from the original activation magnitudes. The influence of adversarial perturbations can be amplified or attenuated using the same learnable parameters by simply flipping the sign of the scaling operation. The amplified signals serve as negative references to construct novel contrastive and ranking loss functions. Experimental analysis shows that learning to degrade the predictions in amplification mode simultaneously improves adversarial robustness in attenuation mode. Moreover, A3 relies on only a small number of learnable parameters, with most of its behavior being determined by the scaling mechanism rather than additional network capacity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that integrating A3 into different backbones, datasets, and training methods consistently improves adversarial robustness while introducing negligible computational and memory overhead compared to existing plug-in modules. Code is available at: https://github.com/tgoncalv/A3.

AI.tldr: A3 is a lightweight plug-in module that enhances adversarial robustness by learning to both amplify and attenuate activation signals through a simple scaling mechanism, using amplified signals as negative references for contrastive/ranking losses while using attenuated signals for robust predictions, achieving consistent robustness improvements across backbones, datasets, and training methods with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing defenses against adversarial attacks (pruning, masking, feature recalibration) only reduce the impact of non-robust features. The authors propose a different approach: jointly learning to both amplify and attenuate these signals to better leverage the relationship between them.
AI.method: A3 introduces a learnable mask and scaling factor derived from activation magnitudes to dynamically rescale activations. By flipping the sign of the scaling operation, the same parameters can either amplify or attenuate adversarial perturbations. Amplified signals are used as negative references in contrastive and ranking loss functions.
AI.result: Learning to degrade predictions in amplification mode simultaneously improves robustness in attenuation mode. A3 consistently improves adversarial robustness across different backbones, datasets, and training methods, with negligible computational and memory overhead compared to existing plug-in modules.
AI.conclusion: The A3 module demonstrates that jointly learning to amplify and attenuate adversarial signals is an effective and efficient approach to improving adversarial robustness, requiring only minimal modifications and additional parameters.

title: GNBAN: Graph Neural Basis Attention Networks for Long-Horizon Forecasting over Large Entity Sets
summary: Demand forecasting at the bottom of a retail hierarchy requires predicting tens of thousands of correlated long-horizon series across products, stores, and regions. Modern systems must scale across massive catalogs, capture shared demand dynamics, and remain interpretable enough to be trusted. Classical statistical methods need a separate model per series and are hard to manage at scale; deep autoregressive models struggle as the joint state grows to tens of thousands of dimensions; and recent graph-based forecasters, while capturing cross-entity dependencies, often produce opaque long-horizon forecasts. We propose GNBAN (Graph Neural Basis Attention Network), an end-to-end architecture combining heterogeneous graph representation learning with an interpretable basis-decomposition head. Retail data are represented directly as a heterogeneous graph derived from the relational schema, so a single model serves the entire catalog. Rather than predicting the horizon directly, GNBAN decomposes each forecast into trend, seasonal, and generic components. Its key innovation is a per-basis attention mechanism: each basis function keeps its own learnable query and retrieves information independently from the entity's historical neighborhood, letting different bases specialize to distinct temporal patterns while preserving interpretability. On two large-scale benchmarks, M5 Walmart and Favorita Grocery Sales, evaluated under matched protocols, GNBAN improves volume-weighted WRMSSE by roughly 4-5% over a matched graph baseline. Qualitative analysis shows the learned decomposition exposes trend, seasonal, and residual demand drivers without post-hoc explanation methods. These results demonstrate that scalable relational forecasting and interpretable forecast decomposition can be achieved together in a unified graph-based framework.

AI.tldr: GNBAN is an end-to-end graph neural architecture for retail demand forecasting that combines heterogeneous graph representation learning with an interpretable basis-decomposition head, achieving ~4-5% WRMSSE improvement over graph baselines on M5 and Favorita benchmarks while providing transparent trend/seasonal/residual decomposition.
AI.motivation: Retail demand forecasting at scale requires modeling tens of thousands of correlated time series across products, stores, and regions. Classical statistical methods (separate models per series) and deep autoregressive models (joint state explosion) struggle with scalability, while graph-based forecasters often produce opaque long-horizon forecasts. There is a need for a unified framework that is both scalable and interpretable.
AI.method: GNBAN represents retail data as a heterogeneous graph derived directly from the relational schema, using a single model for the entire catalog. Instead of predicting the horizon directly, it decomposes forecasts into trend, seasonal, and generic components via a per-basis attention mechanism. Each basis function maintains its own learnable query and retrieves information independently from the entity's historical neighborhood, enabling specialization to distinct temporal patterns while preserving interpretability.
AI.result: On M5 Walmart and Favorita Grocery Sales benchmarks under matched protocols, GNBAN improves volume-weighted WRMSSE by roughly 4-5% over a matched graph baseline. Qualitative analysis confirms that learned decomposition exposes trend, seasonal, and residual demand drivers without requiring post-hoc explanation methods.
AI.conclusion: Scalable relational forecasting and interpretable forecast decomposition can be achieved together in a unified graph-based framework, demonstrating that graph neural architectures with basis-decomposition heads offer a practical solution for large-scale retail demand forecasting.

title: Text as Illumination: Spatial Contrastive Retinex Learning for Language-guided Medical Image Segmentation
comment: Aceepted by MICCAI2026. More modifications may be performed
summary: Language-guided Medical Image Segmentation (LMIS) has shown great potential to improve the delineation of anatomical structures and lesions by integrating clinical textual information. Existing methods generally rely on either implicit interaction between textual and visual features or auxiliary coarse-grained supervision for cross-modal alignment. However, these methods lack explicit and fine-grained constraints to ensure semantic consistency, causing a mismatch between language and the segmentation outputs. To address this issue, we propose Text-as-Illumination Retinex Network (TIRNet), a novel Retinex-inspired framework that treats text embeddings as semantic illumination for feature modulation, thereby improving semantic consistency in LMIS. TIRNet introduces two key blocks integrated at each decoder stage: (1) the Retinex-inspired Text Modulation Block (RTMB), which employs positive and negative illumination maps to enhance text-relevant foreground features and suppress background interference; and (2) the Consistent Detail Compensation Block (CDCB), which selectively recovers high-frequency details via a consistency-gated mechanism conditioned on illumination reliability. Furthermore, we propose a Multi-Scale Illumination Supervision Loss (MSIS-Loss), comprising a Region-Grounded Contrastive Loss (RGC-Loss) that enforces cross-modal similarity to be concentrated in text-relevant foreground regions and suppressed in background regions, and a Background Suppression Loss (BS-Loss) that provides pixel-level supervision for negative illumination maps, jointly ensuring a precise cross-modal alignment at each decoder stage. Extensive experiments on the MosMedData+ and QaTa-COV19 datasets demonstrate that TIRNet achieves state-of-the-art performance in LMIS. The code is available at: https://github.com/anaanaa/TIRNet.

AI.tldr: TIRNet is a Retinex-inspired framework for Language-guided Medical Image Segmentation that uses text embeddings as semantic illumination to modulate features, achieving state-of-the-art performance on MosMedData+ and QaTa-COV19 datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing LMIS methods lack explicit and fine-grained constraints to ensure semantic consistency between text and segmentation outputs, causing a mismatch between language guidance and segmentation results.
AI.method: Proposes TIRNet with: (1) Retinex-inspired Text Modulation Block (RTMB) using positive/negative illumination maps for foreground enhancement and background suppression; (2) Consistent Detail Compensation Block (CDCB) for high-frequency detail recovery via consistency-gated mechanism; (3) Multi-Scale Illumination Supervision Loss including Region-Grounded Contrastive Loss and Background Suppression Loss for precise cross-modal alignment.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on MosMedData+ and QaTa-COV19 datasets for LMIS tasks.
AI.conclusion: Treating text embeddings as semantic illumination effectively ensures semantic consistency and improves cross-modal alignment in language-guided medical image segmentation.

title: FlexMoE: One-for-All Nested Intra-Expert Pruning for MoE Language Models
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models scale model ability with sparsely activated experts, making this architecture a standard recipe for modern large models. However, sparse activation does not remove the deployment burden of storing and serving all experts, and the available deployment budget can vary substantially across devices, users, and workloads. Existing MoE compression methods are still largely fixed-budget, typically optimizing one compressed endpoint at each chosen target budget. We study a different setting: converting a large pretrained MoE LLM into a nested family of deployable subnetworks across budgets. Our method first ranks expert FFN channels by their importance, then lets each expert learn a discrete action to prune its channels. By gradually increasing cost pressure, a single action-training run exports a series of action masks from high to low budgets, each of which identifies a reliable smaller subnetwork nested in the ranked base model. Moreover, we use a single recovery fine-tune at a mid pruning budget (40%) to recover degraded model quality and transfer the recovered model to other unseen budgets. Overall, our framework surpasses recent MoE compression baselines. Specifically, on Qwen2-57B-A14B, our method retains ~99.8% of base performance while pruning 50% of routed expert parameters even without fine-tuning. For deployment, our pruned subnetworks deliver real memory reduction and throughput gains, and further support realtime online budget switching with kernel-level co-design.

AI.tldr: A method to convert pretrained MoE LLMs into a nested family of deployable subnetworks across varying budgets by ranking expert FFN channels, learning discrete pruning actions per expert, and using a single recovery fine-tune at a mid budget to transfer across budgets, achieving ~99.8% performance at 50% pruning on Qwen2-57B-A14B without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing MoE compression methods are fixed-budget and cannot adapt to varying deployment budgets across devices, users, and workloads; there is a need for a single framework that yields nested subnetworks across multiple budgets from one pretrained MoE LLM.
AI.method: (1) Rank expert FFN channels by importance; (2) Each expert learns a discrete action (mask) to prune its channels via action-training with gradually increasing cost pressure, producing a series of nested action masks from high to low budgets; (3) A single recovery fine-tune at a mid pruning budget (40%) recovers degraded quality and transfers to unseen budgets.
AI.result: Surpasses recent MoE compression baselines; on Qwen2-57B-A14B, retains ~99.8% base performance while pruning 50% of routed expert parameters without fine-tuning; delivers real memory reduction and throughput gains; supports realtime online budget switching with kernel-level co-design.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables flexible, multi-budget deployment of compressed MoE LLMs from a single pretrained model, achieving strong performance retention and practical deployment benefits across varying resource constraints.

title: Scalable and Differentiable Point-Cloud Registration Using Maximum Mean Discrepancy
summary: We present MMD-Reg, a novel correspondence-free approach to point-cloud registration that is differentiable and has linear computational complexity in the number of points. We model registration as a nonlinear least-squares problem based on the Maximum Mean Discrepancy, approximated using random Fourier features. The resulting objective can be solved efficiently with standard methods such as Levenberg-Marquardt, and the solution is differentiable via the implicit function theorem. This allows MMD-Reg to be used as a differentiable optimization layer within end-to-end trainable models, supporting registration under challenging conditions such as poor initial alignment and partial overlap. We demonstrate this Neural MMD-Reg formulation by integrating the layer with a set transformer, training the resulting model in supervised and unsupervised settings, and comparing its performance against recent learning-based methods. We also evaluate standalone MMD-Reg, comparing its accuracy and scalability against widely used non-learning-based registration methods.

AI.tldr: MMD-Reg is a differentiable, linear-complexity, correspondence-free point-cloud registration method based on Maximum Mean Discrepancy with random Fourier features. It can be used as a standalone optimizer or as a differentiable layer in end-to-end learning, showing strong performance under poor initial alignment and partial overlap.
AI.motivation: To develop a correspondence-free point-cloud registration method that is both differentiable (enabling integration into end-to-end learning pipelines) and computationally efficient (linear complexity), while handling challenging conditions like poor initialization and partial overlap.
AI.method: Registration is formulated as a nonlinear least-squares problem using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) approximated via random Fourier features. Optimization is performed with Levenberg-Marquardt, and differentiability is achieved via the implicit function theorem. The method is integrated with a set transformer in a Neural MMD-Reg formulation for supervised/unsupervised training.
AI.result: Standalone MMD-Reg demonstrates competitive accuracy and scalability against non-learning-based methods. Neural MMD-Reg, when used as a differentiable layer with a set transformer, shows strong performance compared to recent learning-based methods in both supervised and unsupervised settings, especially under poor initial alignment and partial overlap.
AI.conclusion: MMD-Reg provides a principled, differentiable, and scalable solution for point-cloud registration that bridges optimization-based and learning-based approaches, offering robustness to challenging registration conditions.

title: A Comparison of Fusion Techniques for Multi-Modal Human Activity Recognition on the HARMES Dataset
summary: Recent advances in Human Activity Recognition (HAR) from wearable sensors have shown that multi-modal deep learning models consistently outperform their uni-modal counterparts. Modalities can include IMUs, RGB cameras, audio signals, and others. One important aspect of multi-modal deep learning is the sensor fusion approach we apply. Over recent years, multiple fusion paradigms have been proposed for multi-modal HAR. However, to the best of our knowledge, no head-to-head comparison of these paradigms exists on a common multi-modal HAR benchmark dataset. To address this research gap, we systematically compare seven state-of-the-art sensor fusion methods on the recently released HARMES dataset, which comprises 61 hours of fully labeled IMU, audio, and ambient humidity data. The chosen dataset focuses on 15 household and personal hygiene activities of daily living (ADLs). By applying the seven different fusion techniques to a state-of-the-art multi-modal model architecture, we show that Gated Multi-modal Fusion achieves the highest macro F1-score (0.82), surpassing the concatenation-based late fusion HARMES paper baseline of 0.76 by +6pp under leave-one-participant-out evaluation. All code used in our experiments is made publicly available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a systematic head-to-head comparison of seven sensor fusion methods for multi-modal Human Activity Recognition (HAR) on the HARMES dataset (61 hours, 15 ADLs). Gated Multi-modal Fusion achieves the best macro F1-score (0.82), surpassing the late fusion baseline (0.76) by +6pp.
AI.motivation: Despite many proposed fusion paradigms for multi-modal HAR, no head-to-head comparison exists on a common benchmark dataset. This paper addresses that research gap.
AI.method: Systematically compare seven state-of-the-art sensor fusion techniques on the HARMES dataset (IMU, audio, humidity) using a common state-of-the-art multi-modal model architecture with leave-one-participant-out evaluation.
AI.result: Gated Multi-modal Fusion achieves the highest macro F1-score (0.82), outperforming the concatenation-based late fusion baseline (0.76) by +6pp.
AI.conclusion: Gated Multi-modal Fusion is the most effective sensor fusion paradigm among the seven compared methods for multi-modal HAR on this dataset. All code is publicly available.

title: Video-MME-Logical: A Controlled Diagnostic Benchmark for Video Temporal-Logical Reasoning
summary: Recent interest in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) raises a central question: can they reason over dynamic visual evidence rather than merely recognize objects or events in individual frames? This ability, which we refer to as video temporal-logical reasoning, requires models to maintain, update, and compose evidence as visual states evolve across frames. Existing video benchmarks often conflate this capability with scene complexity, static recognition, or uncontrolled temporal variation. To isolate this capability, we introduce Video-MME-Logical, a controlled benchmark organized around five temporal-logical operations: state tracking, sequential counting, temporal ordering, dynamic spatiality, and structural composition. The benchmark contains 25 fine-grained task categories generated with controlled object states, transitions, temporal dependencies, and logical compositions. It enables difficulty-controlled final-answer evaluation by varying temporal horizon and reasoning complexity, and supports intermediate-state diagnostics by verifying whether models recover the required logical reasoning trace before producing the final answer. Experiments with state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal a substantial human-model gap, especially as temporal-logical complexity increases. Supervised fine-tuning on up to 500K generated samples improves performance but remains insufficient to close the reasoning gap, positioning Video-MME-Logical as a scalable testbed for analyzing and improving temporal-logical reasoning in MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Video-MME-Logical is a controlled benchmark isolating video temporal-logical reasoning in MLLMs across five operations (state tracking, sequential counting, temporal ordering, dynamic spatiality, structural composition). It reveals a large human-model gap that supervised fine-tuning on 500K samples cannot close.
AI.motivation: Existing video benchmarks conflate temporal-logical reasoning with scene complexity, static recognition, or uncontrolled temporal variation. The paper aims to isolate and systematically evaluate whether MLLMs can maintain, update, and compose evidence as visual states evolve across frames.
AI.method: The authors introduce Video-MME-Logical, a benchmark with 25 fine-grained task categories spanning five temporal-logical operations. It uses controlled object states, transitions, temporal dependencies, and logical compositions, enabling difficulty-controlled evaluation by varying temporal horizon and reasoning complexity, plus intermediate-state diagnostics.
AI.result: State-of-the-art MLLMs show a substantial human-model gap that widens as temporal-logical complexity increases. Supervised fine-tuning on up to 500K generated samples improves performance but does not close the reasoning gap.
AI.conclusion: Video-MME-Logical serves as a scalable testbed for analyzing and improving temporal-logical reasoning in MLLMs, highlighting that current models lack robust reasoning over dynamic visual evidence.

title: Hippocampus-DETR: An Explicit Memory Object Detection Framework Based on Hippocampus Modeling
summary: This paper addresses the lack of explicit memory mechanisms in current object detection models and proposes Hippocampus-DETR, a novel detection framework based on biological hippocampal memory modeling. This framework integrates a hippocampal memory network module, HipNet, into the DETR architecture and systematically simulates the anatomical structure and functional organization of hippocampal subregions, including the entorhinal cortex, dentate gyrus, CA3, CA1, and subiculum. Through this design, Hippocampus-DETR realizes pattern separation, pattern completion, importance filtering, and information integration of visual encoding features. During training, different memory submodules are optimized using a layer-wise training strategy, ultimately forming a memory system with memory retrieval and completion capabilities. Experimental results demonstrate that Hippocampus-DETR achieves higher detection accuracy than current mainstream models. More importantly, models equipped with this framework also exhibit excellent generalization ability and data efficiency in tasks such as few-shot image classification, multimodal feature construction, and image restoration. Subsequent experiments further validate the functional necessity and internal interpretability of each memory submodule. This study not only provides a novel object detection framework, but also offers a feasible technical pathway for integrating neurocognitive mechanisms with deep learning models, highlighting its significant value in improving model learning efficiency and task robustness. The project is available at https://github.com/2186cloud/hipnet.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hippocampus-DETR, a novel object detection framework that integrates a biologically-inspired hippocampal memory module (HipNet) into the DETR architecture. It simulates hippocampal subregions (entorhinal cortex, dentate gyrus, CA3, CA1, subiculum) to achieve pattern separation, completion, importance filtering, and information integration. The model achieves higher detection accuracy, better generalization, and data efficiency across multiple tasks, with validated interpretability of memory submodules.
AI.motivation: Current object detection models lack explicit memory mechanisms, limiting their ability to retain and retrieve visual information effectively. This paper aims to address this gap by introducing a biologically plausible memory system inspired by the hippocampus.
AI.method: The approach integrates a hippocampal memory network module (HipNet) into the DETR framework, simulating five hippocampal subregions (entorhinal cortex, dentate gyrus, CA3, CA1, subiculum). It employs a layer-wise training strategy to optimize different memory submodules, enabling pattern separation, pattern completion, importance filtering, and information integration of visual features.
AI.result: Hippocampus-DETR achieves higher detection accuracy than current mainstream models. It also demonstrates excellent generalization ability and data efficiency in few-shot image classification, multimodal feature construction, and image restoration. Experiments validate the functional necessity and internal interpretability of each memory submodule.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a novel object detection framework that bridges neurocognitive mechanisms with deep learning, offering a feasible technical pathway to improve model learning efficiency and task robustness.

title: TA-SparseMG: Trend-Aware Sparse Forecasting via Multi-Scale Gating for Long-Term Time Series
summary: Long-term time series forecasting finds extensive applications in domains such as power demand, traffic flow, meteorological observation, and renewable energy dispatch. Forecasting dynamically varying long-term time series poses inherent challenges, including statistical nonstationarity, local high-frequency disturbances, and coupled cross-period dependencies, which make it difficult for lightweight models to balance parameter efficiency and forecasting performance. To address this issue, this study presents TA-SparseMG, a lightweight cross-period forecasting model built on SparseTSF's sparse cross-period modeling framework. It incorporates three key modules: a trend-aware reversible instance normalization module, a scale-adaptive gated denoising module, and a multiscale gated-attention MLP forecasting module. The trend-aware normalization module captures input-window statistics and calibrates forecast-window distributions, effectively mitigating distribution shift. The scale-adaptive gated denoising module performs feature smoothing and residual suppression before period rearrangement, thereby reducing interference from high-frequency perturbations. The multiscale gated attention prediction module strengthens the prediction head's adaptive representational capacity via conditional gating and feature modulation. Extensive experiments across multiple LTSF benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed TA-SparseMG consistently achieves superior, stable performance. Ablation studies confirm that each module independently improves distribution adaptation, input robustness, and cross-period feature mapping capability.

AI.tldr: TA-SparseMG is a lightweight cross-period time series forecasting model that uses trend-aware normalization, scale-adaptive gated denoising, and multiscale gated-attention MLP to handle nonstationarity, high-frequency noise, and cross-period dependencies, achieving superior performance on LTSF benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-term time series forecasting faces challenges of statistical nonstationarity, local high-frequency disturbances, and coupled cross-period dependencies, making it difficult for lightweight models to balance parameter efficiency and forecasting performance.
AI.method: TA-SparseMG builds on SparseTSF's sparse cross-period modeling framework and incorporates three modules: (1) a trend-aware reversible instance normalization module for distribution shift mitigation, (2) a scale-adaptive gated denoising module for feature smoothing and residual suppression, and (3) a multiscale gated-attention MLP forecasting module for adaptive representational capacity via conditional gating and feature modulation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple LTSF benchmarks demonstrate that TA-SparseMG consistently achieves superior, stable performance. Ablation studies confirm each module independently improves distribution adaptation, input robustness, and cross-period feature mapping.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TA-SparseMG effectively addresses the trade-off between parameter efficiency and forecasting accuracy in long-term time series forecasting through its three specialized modules.

title: CSD: Content-aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Image Generation
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) has emerged as a key solution to accelerate the inference of autoregressive models. However, in the field of image generation, it faces the challenge of low acceptance rates, and directly relaxing its criteria leads to degradation in image quality. In this paper, we propose a novel content-aware speculative decoding algorithm, termed CSD, which integrates an entropy-based probability relaxation mechanism with an optimal resampling strategy to enhance the inference efficiency for autoregressive image generation. By leveraging the informational uncertainty inherent in different regions of an image, CSD dynamically adjusts the acceptance probability of candidate tokens, increasing the acceptance rate in low-detail areas to accelerate generation. Moreover, a distribution alignment filter is introduced to ensure the output distribution to be aligned with the target model, which significantly improves the generative quality. Experiments conducted on Lumina-mGPT and Janus-Pro demonstrate that the superiority of the proposed CSD. Our source code is available at https://github.com/aderfebr/CSD.

AI.tldr: CSD is a content-aware speculative decoding algorithm that uses entropy-based probability relaxation and optimal resampling to accelerate autoregressive image generation while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding faces low acceptance rates in image generation, and simply relaxing criteria degrades image quality.
AI.method: Entropy-based probability relaxation mechanism dynamically adjusts acceptance probability based on regional informational uncertainty; optimal resampling strategy; distribution alignment filter to maintain output distribution alignment with the target model.
AI.result: Experiments on Lumina-mGPT and Janus-Pro demonstrate superiority of CSD over baseline speculative decoding methods.
AI.conclusion: CSD effectively accelerates autoregressive image generation inference by content-aware acceptance rate adjustment without sacrificing generative quality.

title: Graph Dimensionality Reduction for Contextual Bandits: Structure-Specific Regret Bounds under Approximate Smoothness and Noisy Eigenspaces
summary: Contextual bandits with graph-structured arms arise in recommendation, citation retrieval, and social advertising, where arms connected on a graph tend to share reward signal. Standard dimensionality reduction ignores this structure, inflating exploration cost by a factor of $d/k$. We propose GraphDR-LinUCB, which projects arm features onto the graph's low-frequency spectral subspace and runs linear UCB in the resulting $k$-dimensional space. We prove the first $\wtO(k\sqrt{T})$ regret bound for spectral-projection-based contextual bandits, reducing dimension dependence from $d$ to $k$; a perturbation argument extends this to noisy graphs, with an explicit penalty for reward-smoothness mismatch and graph-estimation error. Our central theoretical finding is that the high-frequency reward component need not incur a worst-case linear-in-$T$ penalty: its actual cost depends on its realized impact along the played path, not on its total energy. A simple spectral comparison between subspaces ($Γ_k$) predicts which reducer wins on a given dataset, correctly calling five of six real-dataset outcomes without any fitted threshold. Across a synthetic benchmark and six real datasets (MovieLens, Amazon, LastFM, ogbn-arxiv, MIND), GraphDR-LinUCB reduces cumulative regret by $15\times$ over full-dimensional LinUCB and outperforms competing graph-aware methods on five of six; the single failure is precisely where the graph's spectral subspace is misaligned with the reward.

AI.tldr: GraphDR-LinUCB projects arm features onto a low-frequency spectral subspace of the graph to reduce contextual bandit dimensionality from d to k, achieving Õ(k√T) regret and 15× regret reduction over full-dimensional LinUCB across multiple real datasets.
AI.motivation: Standard contextual bandits with graph-structured arms ignore the graph structure, leading to inflated exploration cost by a factor of d/k. Existing dimensionality reduction fails to leverage the shared reward signal among connected arms on the graph.
AI.method: GraphDR-LinUCB projects arm features onto the graph's low-frequency spectral subspace and runs linear UCB in the resulting k-dimensional space. A perturbation argument handles noisy graphs with explicit penalties for reward-smoothness mismatch and graph-estimation error.
AI.result: First Õ(k√T) regret bound for spectral-projection-based contextual bandits, reducing dimension dependence from d to k. A spectral comparison metric (Γ_k) correctly predicts which reducer wins on a given dataset without any fitted threshold. GraphDR-LinUCB reduces cumulative regret by 15× over full-dimensional LinUCB and outperforms competing graph-aware methods on five of six real datasets.
AI.conclusion: High-frequency reward components do not incur worst-case linear-in-T penalty; their actual cost depends on realized impact along the played path. The method fails only when the graph's spectral subspace is misaligned with the reward structure.

title: Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Protein Sequence Generation with Targeted Amino-Acid Composition
summary: Protein language models are standard priors for biological sequence generation, but steering them toward explicit distributional design targets remains largely unexplored. We study a constrained protein generation problem in which sequences must match a desired amino-acid (AA) composition profile while preserving plausible sequence statistics and diversity. The motivating application is synthetic feed protein design, where the AA composition of dietary proteins directly determines their nutritional value. We propose a two-stage pipeline in which domain-adaptive fine-tuning (FT) on an in-domain protein dataset is followed by iterative reward-weighted FT via reinforcement learning (RL) anchored against the FT model as a frozen reference. We evaluate the pipeline on two AA compositions and find that FT brings the average composition close to the target, while the subsequent RL enforces specific sequence constraints that FT alone cannot satisfy. We additionally evaluate the design choices of the proposed composition reward term against two baselines and an ablated variant, isolate the contribution of each training stage, and verify that AA composition alignment is achieved without degrading sequence quality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a two-stage pipeline (domain-adaptive fine-tuning + iterative reward-weighted RL) for steering protein language models to generate sequences matching target amino-acid composition profiles, with application in synthetic feed protein design.
AI.motivation: Constrained protein generation where sequences must match a desired amino-acid composition profile while preserving plausible sequence statistics and diversity—a problem motivated by synthetic feed protein design where AA composition determines nutritional value.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) domain-adaptive fine-tuning on in-domain protein dataset, followed by (2) iterative reward-weighted fine-tuning via reinforcement learning anchored against the FT model as a frozen reference.
AI.result: FT brings average composition close to target; subsequent RL enforces specific sequence constraints that FT alone cannot satisfy. Additional evaluation against baselines and an ablated variant isolates each training stage's contribution. AA composition alignment is achieved without degrading sequence quality.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage FT+RL pipeline effectively steers protein language models toward explicit distributional design targets (AA composition) while maintaining sequence quality and diversity, with demonstrated application in synthetic feed protein design.

title: ScaLe-INR: Scale and Learn Implicit Neural Representations
comment: Submitted as a conference paper to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) parameterized by multilayer perceptrons excel at modeling continuous signals. However, a key challenge persists as INRs fundamentally suffer from spectral bias and information cross-talk. When a single network attempts to capture multi-scale phenomena, high-frequency weight updates destructively interfere with the underlying low-frequency structural approximation. We introduce Scale and Learn INR (ScaLe-INR), a novel multi-branch architecture that resolves these limitations by explicitly matching the signal's frequency spectrum with the optimal operating region of the INR. Drawing upon the Fourier inverse scaling theorem we demonstrate that applying directional coordinate scaling expands a network's representational bandwidth along specific spatial axes. To mathematically enforce functional disentanglement and minimize task-specific information leakage between branches, we propose a Directional Edge Guidance Loss, a spatially-conditioned sparsity prior derived from ground-truth gradients. By constraining the high-frequency branches to act as strict, localized edge-filters, ScaLe-INR eliminates spectral cross-talk, accelerates convergence, and achieves high-fidelity signal reconstruction on complex multi-scale topologies. We evaluate ScaLe-INR across diverse reconstruction and inverse tasks, demonstrating substantial performance gains over existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods. The proposed architecture improves upon the nearest baselines by +5.16 dB in image reconstruction and +0.65 dB in image denoising. Furthermore, it achieve an impressive figure of 50.02 dB on audio reconstruction and 0.999 IOU(Intersection Over Union) on 3D reconstruction which beats the all SOTA models.

AI.tldr: ScaLe-INR is a multi-branch architecture that uses directional coordinate scaling and a Directional Edge Guidance Loss to resolve spectral bias and information cross-talk in Implicit Neural Representations, achieving SOTA results across image (+5.16 dB), audio (50.02 dB), and 3D (0.999 IoU) reconstruction tasks.
AI.motivation: INRs suffer from spectral bias and information cross-talk when modeling multi-scale signals, where high-frequency updates destructively interfere with low-frequency structural approximations.
AI.method: A multi-branch architecture that (1) matches signal frequency spectrum with optimal INR operating regions via directional coordinate scaling (Fourier inverse scaling theorem), (2) enforces functional disentanglement using a Directional Edge Guidance Loss — a spatially-conditioned sparsity prior from ground-truth gradients that constrains high-frequency branches to act as localized edge-filters.
AI.result: +5.16 dB in image reconstruction, +0.65 dB in image denoising, 50.02 dB on audio reconstruction, 0.999 IoU on 3D reconstruction, outperforming all SOTA models.
AI.conclusion: ScaLe-INR eliminates spectral cross-talk, accelerates convergence, and achieves high-fidelity reconstruction on complex multi-scale topologies.

title: RECAST: Model Reconstruction via Counterfactual-Aware Wasserstein Geometry under Limited Data
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Counterfactual explanations (CFs) help understand machine learning models by identifying minimal input changes that would lead to alternative model outcomes. Recent work demonstrates their utility for reconstructing black-box models, enabling third-party auditing of opaque decision systems for fairness and accountability. Still, CF-based reconstruction may suffer from decision boundary shifts, overfitting, and restrictive assumptions requiring online query access to target platforms. We propose REconstruction via Counterfactual-Aware waSserstein opTimization (RECAST) under limited data and restricted access, a behavioral surrogate model based on Wasserstein barycentric prototypes. Our approach addresses decision boundary shifts by incorporating CFs as informative, though less representative, samples for both classes, maintaining high surrogate fidelity in low-sample regimes without requiring online access during reconstruction. To enhance fairness auditing, our method enables systematic group fairness diagnostics. Experiments on real-world datasets and various setups show that RECAST effectively achieves high fidelity and query efficiency, as well as stable results even when the access is limited and noisy.

AI.tldr: RECAST is a Wasserstein barycentric prototype-based surrogate model that reconstructs black-box ML models using counterfactual explanations under limited data and restricted access, achieving high fidelity and enabling fairness auditing without requiring online query access.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual explanations can be used for model reconstruction to enable third-party auditing of opaque decision systems, but existing CF-based reconstruction suffers from decision boundary shifts, overfitting, and restrictive assumptions requiring online query access to target platforms.
AI.method: RECAST uses Wasserstein barycentric prototypes to construct a behavioral surrogate model from counterfactual explanations. It incorporates CFs as informative (though less representative) samples for both classes, operates under limited data and restricted access, and does not require online query access during reconstruction. The method also enables systematic group fairness diagnostics.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that RECAST achieves high fidelity and query efficiency, with stable results even when access is limited and noisy.
AI.conclusion: RECAST effectively addresses decision boundary shifts and overfitting issues in CF-based model reconstruction, enabling reliable surrogate modeling and fairness auditing under constrained access scenarios.

title: A Unified Framework for Vision Transformers Equivariant to Discrete Subgroups of $\mathrm{O}(2)$
summary: Vision transformers have become a dominant architecture for visual recognition. However, standard models do not explicitly encode the planar symmetries that arise in many vision domains. We introduce a family of vision transformers equivariant to arbitrary discrete subgroups of $\mathrm{O}(2)$, providing a unified framework that generalizes prior flipping- and $D_4$-equivariant transformer architectures. Our construction yields equivariant analogues of the core transformer components, together with expressivity guarantees for the resulting layers. In particular, we show that whenever $H \le G$, the class of $G$-equivariant ViTs embeds naturally into the class of $H$-equivariant ViTs. We also prove that, in the single-head setting, the corresponding equivariant self-attention layer realizes every $G$-equivariant self-attention map representable by ordinary self-attention. We further construct a $D_6$-equivariant model based on hexagonal patches, making the architecture compatible with six-fold rotational symmetries. We evaluate the resulting models on the PatternNet aerial image dataset in artificially data-scarce regimes across subgroups of $D_4$ and $D_6$. Our experiments compare two equivariant attention mechanisms and analyze how the choice of homogeneous-space configurations used in the nonlinearities affects performance. Preliminary results under matched parameter budgets indicate that equivariance can improve recognition accuracy, motivating further study of how discrete symmetry groups shape transformer-based visual recognition models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a family of vision transformers equivariant to arbitrary discrete subgroups of O(2), providing a unified framework that generalizes prior flipping- and D4-equivariant transformer architectures. It constructs equivariant analogues of core transformer components, proves expressivity guarantees (including embedding relations between symmetry groups and full realizability of equivariant self-attention maps), builds a D6-equivariant model with hexagonal patches, and evaluates on PatternNet under data-scarce regimes, showing equivariance can improve recognition accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard vision transformers do not explicitly encode planar symmetries (rotational and reflectional) that naturally arise in many vision tasks. The authors aim to incorporate these inductive biases directly into the architecture.
AI.method: The authors construct equivariant analogues of core transformer components (self-attention, MLPs, etc.) for any discrete subgroup of O(2). They prove theoretical guarantees: (1) a nesting property where G-equivariant ViTs embed into H-equivariant ViTs for H ≤ G; (2) in single-head setting, equivariant self-attention realizes every G-equivariant self-attention map representable by ordinary self-attention. They also build a D6-equivariant model using hexagonal patches for six-fold rotational symmetry.
AI.result: Experiments on PatternNet aerial image dataset in data-scarce regimes across subgroups of D4 and D6 show that equivariance can improve recognition accuracy under matched parameter budgets. The paper compares two equivariant attention mechanisms and analyzes how homogeneous-space configurations in nonlinearities affect performance.
AI.conclusion: Equivariant vision transformers with discrete planar symmetry groups show promise for improving recognition, especially in data-scarce settings. The work provides a unified theoretical framework and motivates further study of how discrete symmetry groups shape transformer-based visual recognition models.

title: SpatialUAV: Benchmarking Spatial Intelligence for Low-Altitude UAV Perception, Collaboration, and Motion
summary: Spatial intelligence is essential for low-altitude unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) perception, collaboration, and navigation. However, existing UAV benchmarks often emphasize image-level recognition, single-view understanding, or narrow answer formats, leaving 3D spatial inference, multi-view collaboration, scene dynamics, and diverse task formulations insufficiently evaluated. To address these gaps, we introduce SpatialUAV, a real low-altitude UAV benchmark comprising 4,331 curated instances across 14 fine-grained task types, covering semantic discrimination, spatial relation, aerial--aerial collaboration, aerial--ground collaboration, and motion understanding. SpatialUAV organizes all samples into a unified visual-input--question--answer schema, while supporting seven input configurations and nine answer formats, including option labels, region identifiers, geometric values, cross-view correspondences, and free-form motion descriptions. To ensure reliable and grounded evaluation, our data construction pipeline integrates detector-assisted regions, depth supervision, metadata-derived rules, extensive manual annotation, blind filtering, and multi-turn human validation, together with task-specific metrics for heterogeneous outputs. Evaluating representative vision-language models across three categories, we show that current models remain far from human-level performance, with pronounced bottlenecks in cross-view association, structured grounding, geometric reasoning, and temporal viewpoint understanding. These results offer empirical guidance for advancing low-altitude UAV spatial intelligence. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Hyu-Zhang/SpatialUAV.

AI.tldr: SpatialUAV is a real low-altitude UAV benchmark with 4,331 curated instances across 14 fine-grained task types, covering semantic discrimination, spatial relations, aerial-aerial/ground collaboration, and motion understanding. It supports 7 input configurations and 9 answer formats. Evaluations show current vision-language models significantly underperform humans, particularly in cross-view association, structured grounding, geometric reasoning, and temporal viewpoint understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing UAV benchmarks emphasize image-level recognition, single-view understanding, or narrow answer formats, leaving 3D spatial inference, multi-view collaboration, scene dynamics, and diverse task formulations insufficiently evaluated.
AI.method: Data construction pipeline integrates detector-assisted regions, depth supervision, metadata-derived rules, extensive manual annotation, blind filtering, and multi-turn human validation, with task-specific metrics for heterogeneous outputs. The benchmark organizes samples into a unified visual-input-question-answer schema supporting 7 input configurations and 9 answer formats.
AI.result: Current vision-language models remain far from human-level performance, with pronounced bottlenecks in cross-view association, structured grounding, geometric reasoning, and temporal viewpoint understanding.
AI.conclusion: SpatialUAV offers empirical guidance for advancing low-altitude UAV spatial intelligence. Code and data are publicly available.

title: Dual-Learning based Penalized Multi-Align Clustering for Multi-View Incomplete and Disorderly Data
summary:
Multimodal feature fusion can effectively capture complex patterns in real-world data by integrating complementary information from different modalities. However, in many applications, such as boiler combustion monitoring, equipment failure, inconsistent sensor sampling frequencies, and network delays often cause missing modalities and temporal asynchrony. These issues lead to incomplete and disorderly multimodal data. To address them, previous studies have proposed several data fusion methods that align cluster centers before fusion. However, these methods have two key limitations. First, they cannot guarantee accurate sample-level alignment of data pairs. Second, they do not address significant discrepancies in data sizes across different classes, which may affect subsequent fusion performance.
  To address these problems, we propose a dual-learning based penalized multi-align clustering model, named DLPMAC. The dual-learning mechanism enables the model to learn prior knowledge from each modality, including semantic and structural information. This helps preserve semantic consistency and structural similarity across modalities at both local and global levels. In addition, the penalized multi-align module performs multi-to-multi data alignment through a penalty mechanism. It allows one sample to form data pairs with different samples from other modalities, thereby improving data-pair alignment accuracy. The penalty mechanism also prevents data aggregation, avoiding the case where excessive samples are linked to a single sample. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of DLPMAC in addressing data alignment and fusion challenges from both sampling and clustering perspectives.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DLPMAC, a dual-learning based penalized multi-align clustering model, to address missing modalities and temporal asynchrony in multimodal data fusion by enabling multi-to-multi alignment with a penalty mechanism and preserving semantic/structural consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal fusion methods cannot guarantee accurate sample-level alignment of data pairs and fail to address significant discrepancies in data sizes across different classes, especially in applications like boiler combustion monitoring where missing modalities and temporal asynchrony occur due to sensor failures and network delays.
AI.method: DLPMAC uses a dual-learning mechanism to learn prior knowledge (semantic and structural information) from each modality, preserving cross-modal consistency. A penalized multi-align module performs multi-to-multi data alignment via a penalty mechanism, allowing one sample to form pairs with different samples from other modalities while preventing over-aggregation.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate DLPMAC's effectiveness in addressing data alignment and fusion challenges from both sampling and clustering perspectives.
AI.conclusion: DLPMAC effectively handles incomplete and asynchronous multimodal data by improving alignment accuracy and preventing data aggregation issues, outperforming prior cluster-center alignment methods.

title: Every Step of the Way: Video-based Parkinsonian Turning Step Counting
summary: As a prominent symptom of Parkinson's disease (PD), turning impairment is evaluated through parameters such as turning angle, duration, and particularly, the number of steps required to complete a turn, which directly reflects motor dysfunction. Accurate step counting is challenging due to variability in real-world turning movements and atypical shuffling patterns in parkinsonian gait. Existing methods are predominantly wearable-based, requiring users to wear and manage dedicated devices, which can be inconvenient for continuous daily use. To address this, we propose a passive, video-based framework that estimates step count in a coarse-to-fine manner using diverse motion representations. Specifically, an initial step count is estimated from foot movement signals derived from 3D human mesh recovery, providing high-level motion structures. To incorporate fine-grained motion details, a motion encoder learns complementary gait dynamics from mesh and optical flow to refine the initial estimate. In this process, coarse foot movement signals query the pixel-level motion cues via cross attention to capture subtle parkinsonian gait dynamics. To handle varying video lengths, we partition each video into clips and integrate clip-wise motion embeddings via multiple instance learning (MIL) for step count residual prediction. Extensive experiments show our method consistently outperforms existing step counting methods on real-world PD turning datasets.

AI.tldr: A passive video-based framework estimates step count during turning in Parkinson's disease patients by combining coarse 3D human mesh-derived foot movement signals with fine-grained optical flow cues via cross-attention and multiple instance learning, outperforming existing wearable-based methods.
AI.motivation: Turning impairment is a key PD symptom, and step count during turns directly reflects motor dysfunction. Existing wearable-based methods are inconvenient for continuous daily use; a passive video-based approach is needed to improve usability while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: 1) Coarse initial step count from foot movement signals derived via 3D human mesh recovery; 2) Motion encoder learning complementary gait dynamics from mesh and optical flow; 3) Coarse foot signals query pixel-level motion cues via cross-attention to capture subtle parkinsonian gait; 4) Video partitioned into clips, clip-wise embeddings aggregated via multiple instance learning (MIL) for step count residual prediction.
AI.result: The method consistently outperforms existing step counting methods on real-world PD turning datasets.
AI.conclusion: A passive video-based coarse-to-fine framework with cross-attention and MIL effectively estimates step count during PD turns, offering a convenient alternative to wearable-based approaches.

title: Benchmarking on Tasks That Matter: Dataset Selection for Preserving Model Rankings
comment: Accepted to the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary:
Benchmarks of machine learning models often include many datasets, making evaluation expensive. For efficiency, it is preferable to perform evaluations on small, representative datasets instead. The selection of such subsets typically relies on heuristics and is rarely analyzed for the robustness of the resulting model rankings.
  We introduce a framework to perform the task of selecting datasets subsets with an evaluation of how different selection strategies preserve the global model rankings. Our framework includes bootstrap aggregation, which provides valid confidence intervals, allowing a principled comparison of selection strategies. We consider clustering, design criteria (A/D-optimality), random baselines, and greedy farthest-first (FAFI). For the latter, we derive upper bounds on selection quality in terms of ranking errors as a function of the number of selected datasets.
  Empirically, in time series classification (TSC, 112 datasets) and in a supplementary natural language processing benchmark derived from MTEB (57 tasks), several selection strategies improve rank preservation compared with random subsets, including simple FAFI. In contrast, in recommender systems (30 datasets), the improvement of strategies over random selection is small and typically statistically insignificant. For TSC, our best-performing strategy achieves a Spearman correlation of 0.95 with the full benchmark model rankings using only five selected datasets. Additional experiments indicate that the effectiveness of selection approaches depends on both the quality of dataset representations and the scale of the benchmarking regime.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a principled framework for selecting small, representative subsets of datasets from large benchmarks to efficiently evaluate machine learning models, demonstrating that simple strategies like farthest-first selection can preserve model rankings with high correlation (e.g., Spearman 0.95 with only 5 datasets in TSC), though effectiveness varies across domains.
AI.motivation: Evaluating ML models on large benchmarks with many datasets is computationally expensive. The goal is to identify small, representative dataset subsets that can preserve global model rankings, enabling efficient evaluation without running on all datasets.
AI.method: The framework uses bootstrap aggregation to provide valid confidence intervals for comparing selection strategies. Strategies evaluated include clustering, A/D-optimality design criteria, random baselines, and greedy farthest-first (FAFI). Upper bounds on selection quality in terms of ranking errors are derived for FAFI.
AI.result: On time series classification (112 datasets), strategies like FAFI significantly improve rank preservation over random selection, achieving Spearman correlation of 0.95 with full rankings using only 5 datasets. In NLP (57 tasks from MTEB), similar improvements hold. In recommender systems (30 datasets), improvements over random are small and rarely statistically significant.
AI.conclusion: The effectiveness of dataset selection strategies depends on both the quality of dataset representations and the scale of the benchmarking regime. Simple FAFI is competitive, but not all domains benefit equally from principled selection.

title: OrthoTryOn: Geometric Orthogonalization for Conflict-Free Unified Fashion Generation
summary: Unified fashion generation integrates tasks like virtual try-on and garment reconstruction into a single model to reduce task-specific adaptation costs. However, naive parameter sharing across semantically distinct tasks induces negative transfer through severe inter-task gradient conflict. We propose OrthoTryOn, a unified framework mitigating this interference within a shared Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) module. Its Orthogonal Subspace Projection (OSP) applies task-specific orthogonal rotations to bottleneck features, mapping them into decorrelated coordinate frames. To address residual semantic coupling at inference time, we further propose Fisher-guided Negative Guidance (FNG), a parameter-free strategy that utilizes diagonal Fisher information to quantify inter-task sensitivity overlap and explicitly repels generation trajectories from the most confusable task via Classifier-Free Guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OrthoTryOn avoids the severe performance degradation typical of naive unified training and even surpasses independently trained task-specific models, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks while generalizing robustly across diverse diffusion backbones. Code is available at https://github.com/NJU-PCALab/OrthoTryOn.

AI.tldr: OrthoTryOn is a unified fashion generation framework that uses Orthogonal Subspace Projection (OSP) within a shared LoRA module to mitigate inter-task gradient conflict, plus Fisher-guided Negative Guidance (FNG) to reduce residual semantic coupling at inference, achieving SOTA results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Naive parameter sharing across semantically distinct fashion generation tasks (e.g., virtual try-on, garment reconstruction) causes negative transfer due to severe inter-task gradient conflict, leading to performance degradation in unified models.
AI.method: (1) Orthogonal Subspace Projection (OSP): applies task-specific orthogonal rotations to LoRA bottleneck features, mapping them into decorrelated coordinate frames to reduce gradient conflict during training. (2) Fisher-guided Negative Guidance (FNG): a parameter-free inference strategy using diagonal Fisher information to quantify inter-task sensitivity overlap and explicitly repel generation trajectories from the most confusable task via Classifier-Free Guidance.
AI.result: OrthoTryOn avoids the severe performance degradation of naive unified training, surpasses independently trained task-specific models, and achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks with robust generalization across diverse diffusion backbones.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific orthogonal subspace projection and Fisher-guided negative guidance effectively resolve inter-task interference in unified fashion generation, enabling a single model to outperform individual task-specific models.

title: Reflect-R1: Evidence-Driven Reflection for Self-Correction in Long Video Understanding
summary: Current multimodal reflection mechanisms for long video understanding predominantly rely on closed-loop self-reflection within internal parameters. Lacking objective external evidence, models are frequently trapped in blind confidence and often fail to correct errors. Furthermore, applying reinforcement learning to multi-stage reflection pipelines introduces severe policy coupling, which is exacerbated by a critical scarcity of dedicated training data. To address these limitations, this work proposes Reflect-R1, the first Evidence-Driven self-correction framework for long video understanding. The framework constructs a three-stage pipeline consisting of intuition, verification, and arbitration. By dynamically retrieving objective visual evidence to verify initial intuitions and autonomously executing multiple temporal searches to resolve conflicts, it completely breaks the hallucination loop. To overcome policy coupling, we design a stage-decoupled reinforcement learning algorithm named SD-GRPO that independently computes advantage functions across different reasoning stages. Concurrently, we construct a dataset of 120K samples to bridge the training data gap. Extensive experiments on benchmarks such as VideoMME and LongVideoBench demonstrate that Reflect-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our method significantly improves the genuine rectification rate and enables authentic self-correction strictly grounded in objective evidence.

AI.tldr: Reflect-R1 is an evidence-driven self-correction framework for long video understanding that uses a three-stage pipeline (intuition, verification, arbitration) with stage-decoupled reinforcement learning (SD-GRPO) and a 120K training dataset to overcome blind confidence and policy coupling, achieving SOTA on VideoMME and LongVideoBench.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal reflection mechanisms for long video understanding rely on closed-loop self-reflection within internal parameters, lacking objective external evidence, which leads to blind confidence and failure to correct errors. Additionally, applying reinforcement learning to multi-stage reflection pipelines causes severe policy coupling, exacerbated by a scarcity of dedicated training data.
AI.method: Proposes Reflect-R1 with a three-stage pipeline: intuition, verification, and arbitration. It dynamically retrieves objective visual evidence to verify initial intuitions and autonomously executes multiple temporal searches to resolve conflicts. Introduces SD-GRPO, a stage-decoupled reinforcement learning algorithm that independently computes advantage functions across reasoning stages. Also constructs a 120K-sample training dataset.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks such as VideoMME and LongVideoBench. Significantly improves the genuine rectification rate and enables authentic self-correction strictly grounded in objective evidence.
AI.conclusion: The evidence-driven design and stage-decoupled RL effectively break the hallucination loop and overcome policy coupling, enabling reliable self-correction for long video understanding tasks.

title: OperatorSHAP: Fast and Accurate Shapley Value Estimation for Neural Operators
summary: Understanding model predictions is essential for physical applications, where outputs often inform safety-critical decisions, such as structural load assessment, weather warnings, and clinical diagnosis. Shapley values satisfy many desirable properties as an attribution method, but their computational cost during inference hinders their practical use. Current amortized explainers, such as FastSHAP, are limited to homogeneous inputs, which is problematic for physical applications where data often comes from irregular grids and geometries. We introduce OperatorSHAP, a grid-agnostic attribution method and training procedure that allows us to train FastSHAP-like explainers for neural operators. We establish a theoretical framework for attributions in function space, connecting to Aumann-Shapley values. We further show that OperatorSHAP's explanations are consistent with state-of-the-art discrete Shapley values across resolutions and transfer across grid sizes without retraining.

AI.tldr: OperatorSHAP is a grid-agnostic attribution method for neural operators that enables efficient, amortized Shapley value computation for physical applications with irregular grids and geometries, establishing theoretical connections to Aumann-Shapley values in function space.
AI.motivation: Shapley values are desirable for attribution in safety-critical physical applications but are computationally expensive during inference. Current amortized explainers like FastSHAP are limited to homogeneous inputs, which fails for physical data on irregular grids and geometries.
AI.method: OperatorSHAP introduces a grid-agnostic attribution method and training procedure for neural operators, extending FastSHAP-like explainers. It establishes a theoretical framework for attributions in function space, connecting to Aumann-Shapley values.
AI.result: OperatorSHAP's explanations are consistent with state-of-the-art discrete Shapley values across different resolutions and transfer across grid sizes without requiring retraining.
AI.conclusion: OperatorSHAP enables practical, grid-agnostic Shapley value-based explanations for neural operators in physical applications, overcoming the homogeneity limitations of prior amortized explainers.

title: A Multi-Attribute Latent Space for Visual Analysis of Watches
summary: We present a design rationale, embedding model, and interactive visual-analysis system for exploring large wristwatch collections through heterogeneous visual and semantic attributes. The system addresses a common limitation of catalog and e-commerce interfaces: users can filter by metadata, but they receive little support for open-ended exploration of visual similarity, stylistic alternatives, and mixed aesthetic-functional criteria. We therefore represent watches with separate attribute graphs for dial color and dial design, while using watch type as an explicit semantic organizer. Dials are segmented with a U-Net, watch types are predicted with a Vision Transformer, colors are represented through a shared CIELAB reference palette, and dial structure is described with a gradient-based image descriptor. We extend UMAP by combining attribute-specific neighborhood graphs in a unified probabilistic objective and by adding a class-aware layout term that separates global type structure from local visual neighborhoods. The resulting map is exposed in an interactive interface with spatial navigation, metadata filtering, detail inspection, and search-by-example insertion. We evaluate the approach through parameter analysis, runtime measurements, and a qualitative pilot study with watch experts and novices. The results suggest that the system supports discovery and comparison, while also revealing limitations in scalability assessment, search-by-example validation, and the need for broader domain studies. We explicitly discuss these limitations and derive design implications for multi-attribute latent-space visualization across heterogeneous visual collections.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a visual-analytics system for exploring large wristwatch collections using heterogeneous visual attributes (dial color, dial design) and semantic metadata (watch type). It employs U-Net for dial segmentation, a Vision Transformer for type classification, CIELAB color representation, and a gradient-based dial descriptor, then extends UMAP with attribute-specific neighborhood graphs and class-aware layout. An interactive interface supports navigation, filtering, and search-by-example. Evaluation includes parameter analysis, runtime, and a pilot study with experts and novices.
AI.motivation: Existing catalog and e-commerce interfaces allow filtering by metadata but lack support for open-ended exploration of visual similarity, stylistic alternatives, and mixed aesthetic-functional criteria in large heterogeneous visual collections such as wristwatches.
AI.method: The system uses separate attribute graphs for dial color and dial design, U-Net for dial segmentation, Vision Transformer for watch type prediction, CIELAB reference palette for color representation, and a gradient-based image descriptor for dial structure. UMAP is extended by combining attribute-specific neighborhood graphs via a unified probabilistic objective and adding a class-aware layout term.
