AI.result: Parameter analysis, runtime measurements, and a qualitative pilot study with watch experts and novices indicate the system supports discovery and comparison. However, limitations were identified in scalability assessment, search-by-example validation, and the need for broader domain studies.
AI.conclusion: The system effectively enables exploration of wristwatch collections through multi-attribute latent-space visualization. Limitations are explicitly discussed, yielding design implications for future work on heterogeneous visual collections.

title: Home3D 1.0: A High-Fidelity Image-to-3D Asset Generation System for Interior Design
summary: We present Home3D 1.0, a modular image-to-3D generation system that produces high-quality 3D assets from a single reference image, targeting interior design and e-commerce applications. Given a photograph of a furniture or decor item, the system outputs a mesh with physically-based rendering (PBR) materials, and the mesh can be decomposed into material-specific components. The pipeline is organized into four tightly coupled modules: Geometry reconstructs a watertight mesh through latent SDF modelling with a geometry VAE and a coarse-to-fine flow-matching DiT; Texture predicts multiview albedo observations, reprojects them onto the mesh, and completes unseen surface regions with a 3D texture field; Material uses MatWeaver to obtain component masks through video-based segmentation and UV-space voting, then retrieves and bakes PBR maps from a curated material library through hierarchical multi-modal matching; and Parts generates material-editable semantic part meshes with a PartVAE and PartDiT, decoding multi-head part-specific SDF fields in one pass. Each module is evaluated independently with dedicated metrics, highlighting both the current system capability and the remaining gaps toward broader deployment.

AI.tldr: Home3D 1.0 is a modular image-to-3D generation system that produces high-quality 3D assets with PBR materials, decomposable meshes, and material-editable semantic parts from a single reference image, targeting interior design and e-commerce.
AI.motivation: To enable practical 3D content creation for interior design and e-commerce by generating production-ready 3D assets with PBR materials and editable component parts from a single photograph.
AI.method: A four-module pipeline: (1) Geometry module uses latent SDF modeling with a VAE and coarse-to-fine flow-matching DiT; (2) Texture module predicts multiview albedo, reprojects to mesh, and completes unseen regions via 3D texture field; (3) Material module uses MatWeaver for segmentation and hierarchical multi-modal matching to retrieve/bake PBR maps; (4) Parts module uses PartVAE/PartDiT for multi-head part-specific SDF decoding.
AI.result: Each module is evaluated independently with dedicated metrics, demonstrating current system capability and identifying remaining gaps for broader deployment.
AI.conclusion: Home3D 1.0 achieves high-quality, decomposable 3D asset generation with PBR materials and semantic parts, though further improvements are needed for broader deployment.

title: Fair Classification with Efficient and Post-hoc Controllable Fairness-Accuracy Trade-off
summary: Post-hoc controllability of fair machine learning models, the ability to control the trade-off between fairness and accuracy after training, is valuable for practical deployment. Existing post-processing methods provide such post-hoc controllability but often suffer from significant accuracy degradation, whereas in-processing methods achieve efficient trade-offs but require computationally expensive retraining for each change in trade-off ratio. To achieve both post-hoc controllability and efficient trade-offs, we propose a novel fair classification algorithm that learns effective feature representations to improve the trade-off efficiency of post-processing fair classifiers, by a gradient-based optimization approach. Experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method achieves trade-off efficiency comparable to, or even surpassing, in-processing methods, without requiring any retraining.

AI.tldr: Proposes a gradient-based fair classification algorithm that learns effective feature representations, enabling post-hoc controllability of the fairness-accuracy trade-off without retraining, achieving performance comparable to in-processing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing post-processing methods offer post-hoc controllability but suffer from accuracy degradation, while in-processing methods achieve efficient trade-offs but require expensive retraining for each trade-off ratio change.
AI.method: A gradient-based optimization approach that learns effective feature representations to improve the trade-off efficiency of post-processing fair classifiers.
AI.result: The method achieves trade-off efficiency comparable to or even surpassing in-processing methods on real-world datasets without requiring retraining.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm successfully combines post-hoc controllability with efficient fairness-accuracy trade-offs, eliminating the need for retraining while maintaining competitive performance.

title: Long-Term Prediction of Local and Global Human Motion with Occlusion Recovery
summary: Human motion describes the three-dimensional full-body movement of a person. Anticipating such motion holds significant relevance across a wide range of application domains such as human-robot interaction, autonomous driving, animation, and healthcare. In recent research, spatial and temporal dependencies are modeled by bidirectional attention mechanisms. These typically anticipate human motion in an autoregressive manner which could cause an accumulation of errors over time. As a consequence, they solely focus on local pose forecasting. To address these limitations, we propose a non-autoregressive transformer based on spatio-temporal attention, and train it not only for local pose anticipation, but also for global motion prediction in space. Furthermore, to enhance its applicability in real-world scenarios, our model is also trained to recover missing joints due to occlusions, and is capable of processing varying lengths of history observations. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Q-Y-Yang/Prediction-of-Local-and-Global-Human-Motion.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a non-autoregressive transformer with spatio-temporal attention for both local pose forecasting and global motion prediction, capable of handling occlusions and varying observation lengths.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive methods for human motion prediction suffer from error accumulation over time and focus only on local pose forecasting, limiting their applicability.
AI.method: A non-autoregressive transformer based on spatio-temporal attention, trained for both local pose anticipation and global motion prediction, with the ability to recover missing joints from occlusions and process varying history lengths.
AI.result: The model addresses error accumulation through non-autoregressive decoding, achieves joint local-global prediction, handles occluded joints, and accommodates variable-length input sequences.
AI.conclusion: The proposed non-autoregressive spatio-temporal transformer improves human motion prediction by enabling global motion forecasting, robustness to occlusions, and flexible input length processing, with code publicly available.

title: When One Adapter Speaks for Many: Discovering Low-Rank Redundancy in Continual Fine-Tuning
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become the standard tool for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large pretrained models. When applied sequentially across tasks in Continual Learning (CL), the standard assumption is that each new task requires a dedicated low-rank adapter. In this work, we challenge this assumption empirically and structurally. We show that task-specific LoRA adapters in CL exhibit significant low-rank redundancy: the subspaces spanned by adapters trained on different tasks substantially overlap, and in many cases earlier adapters can faithfully represent later tasks. Building on this observation, we propose LiteLoRA, a plug-and-play gating mechanism that learns at train time whether to recruit a new adapter or reuse existing low-rank representations. Our method reduces the number of active adapters by 20-70% while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance on standard CL benchmarks, revealing that structural redundancy is pervasive and that selective learning is sufficient to achieve stability without sacrificing plasticity.

AI.tldr: LiteLoRA is a gating mechanism that reduces redundant LoRA adapters in Continual Learning by 20-70% while maintaining or improving performance, by learning when to reuse existing low-rank representations instead of allocating new ones per task.
AI.motivation: To challenge and address the redundancy of task-specific LoRA adapters in Continual Learning, where adapters trained on different tasks exhibit significant subspace overlap and earlier adapters can often represent later tasks.
AI.method: Proposes LiteLoRA, a plug-and-play gating mechanism that learns during training whether to activate a new adapter or reuse existing low-rank representations from previous tasks.
AI.result: Reduces active adapters by 20-70% while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance on standard CL benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Structural redundancy is pervasive in CL with LoRA, and selective learning through gating is sufficient to achieve stability without sacrificing plasticity.

title: There and Back Again: A Flexible-Frame Transformer for Multi-Exposure Fusion
summary: Multi-exposure fusion (MEF) brings the dynamic range of conventional cameras closer to that of human vision, producing images with rich scene content. Given the large variability in scene luminance, exposure strategies often require different numbers of frames to capture the full radiance range faithfully. However, conventional MEF techniques are typically designed for a fixed number of inputs, forcing deployment systems to maintain separate models for different frame-count requirements, which undermines deployment efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose FreeMEF, the first flexible-frame transformer for MEF that seamlessly accommodates varying numbers of input exposures without retraining or architectural changes. The proposed approach consists of two key modules. First, we introduce a recurrent state space module (RSSM) that sequentially fuses features from arbitrary sequences via adaptive alignment and state-space recurrent modeling, thereby providing global information guidance for the subsequent restoration. Second, we devise a global feature guided block (GFGB) incorporating an extremity-aware hybrid attention (EAHA) and an affine-injection feed-forward network (AFFN), which effectively resolves the similarity paradox while simultaneously optimizing contrast and brightness regulation. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, which performs favorably against state-of-the-art methods both quantitatively and qualitatively.

AI.tldr: FreeMEF proposes the first flexible-frame transformer for multi-exposure fusion that handles varying numbers of input exposures without retraining, using a recurrent state space module and a global feature guided block with extremity-aware hybrid attention.
AI.motivation: Conventional MEF techniques are designed for a fixed number of input frames, forcing deployment systems to maintain separate models for different frame-count requirements, which undermines deployment efficiency.
AI.method: FreeMEF consists of two key modules: (1) Recurrent State Space Module (RSSM) that sequentially fuses features from arbitrary sequences via adaptive alignment and state-space recurrent modeling; (2) Global Feature Guided Block (GFGB) incorporating Extremity-Aware Hybrid Attention (EAHA) and Affine-Injection Feed-Forward Network (AFFN) to resolve the similarity paradox and optimize contrast/brightness regulation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate favorable quantitative and qualitative performance against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: FreeMEF is the first flexible-frame transformer for MEF that seamlessly handles varying numbers of input exposures without architectural changes or retraining, improving deployment efficiency.

title: Dangerous Liaisons of Convex Learning and Non-Affine Aggregation
summary: Last-iterate convergence and generalization guarantees in first-order convex learning hinge on the monotonicity of the update operator. While linear averaging preserves the monotonicity of gradient updates, this property is often violated when gradients are aggregated non-affinely, as in modern pipelines enforcing constraints like adaptivity, privacy, robustness or fairness. Whether it is possible to design non-affine aggregation rules that maintain monotonicity has remained an open question. We answer this question negatively: we prove that the monotonicity of aggregated gradients is preserved if and only if the aggregation rule is positively affine. Consequently, non-affine aggregation prevents steady convergence and substantially degrade algorithmic stability. We quantify these drawbacks and propose a path forward by identifying sufficient conditions under which monotonicity can be restored. Our results provide a unified theoretical framework explaining the disparate failure modes observed in modern learning systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that non-affine gradient aggregation rules (e.g., for adaptivity, privacy, robustness, fairness) break monotonicity, which is necessary for last-iterate convergence and generalization in convex learning. The authors show monotonicity is preserved iff the aggregation rule is positively affine, quantify the resulting degradation, and propose sufficient conditions to restore monotonicity.
AI.motivation: Modern machine learning pipelines use non-affine gradient aggregation for adaptivity, privacy, robustness, or fairness, which can violate the monotonicity property essential for convergence guarantees. Whether monotonicity can be preserved under such non-affine rules was an open question.
AI.method: The authors prove a negative result: monotonicity of aggregated gradients holds iff the aggregation rule is positively affine. They then quantify the performance degradation caused by non-affine aggregation and identify sufficient conditions to restore monotonicity.
AI.result: Non-affine aggregation rules unavoidably break monotonicity, leading to unstable convergence and degraded generalization. The degradation is quantified theoretically, and sufficient conditions for restoring monotonicity are identified.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a unified theoretical explanation for disparate failure modes in modern learning systems and suggest a path forward by designing aggregation rules that satisfy sufficient conditions for monotonicity.

title: ProMSA:Progressive Multimodal Search Agents for Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering
summary: Knowledge-based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires models to combine image understanding with external knowledge. Most prior methods use a fixed retrieve-then-generate pipeline with a pre-selected retriever and a static top-k setting, which is not adaptive during reasoning. We propose ProMSA, a progressive multimodal search agent for KB-VQA. Given an image-question pair, the agent iteratively chooses image search, text search, or stop, under explicit tool-call budgets and with deduplication to avoid redundant retrieval. For training, we first use rejection-sampling SFT to learn valid tool-use formats, then optimize the agent with TN-GSPO, a sequence-level RL objective that normalizes updates by both generation length and tool-interaction depth. Experiments on E-VQA and InfoSeek show consistent gains over strong RAG and agent baselines, and improved retrieval and end-to-end accuracy. The code is available at https://github.com/DingWu1021/Promsa.

AI.tldr: ProMSA is a progressive multimodal search agent for KB-VQA that iteratively chooses image search, text search, or stop under explicit budgets with deduplication, trained via rejection-sampling SFT and sequence-level RL (TN-GSPO), achieving consistent gains on E-VQA and InfoSeek.
AI.motivation: Prior KB-VQA methods use a fixed retrieve-then-generate pipeline with a pre-selected retriever and static top-k setting, lacking adaptability during reasoning.
AI.method: ProMSA iteratively selects image search, text search, or stop under explicit tool-call budgets with deduplication; trained via rejection-sampling SFT followed by TN-GSPO (sequence-level RL that normalizes by generation length and tool-interaction depth).
AI.result: Consistent gains over strong RAG and agent baselines on E-VQA and InfoSeek, with improved retrieval and end-to-end accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive iterative tool-use with budget control and RL optimization improves KB-VQA over fixed retrieve-then-generate pipelines.

title: Beyond Sparse Supervision: Diffusion-Guided Learning for Few-Shot Graph Fraud Detection
summary: Graph-based fraud detection is essential for safeguarding large-scale transaction systems, where undetected anomalies may lead to substantial financial losses and security risks. Real-world fraud graphs pose two coupled challenges: sparse and imbalanced supervision, where verified fraudulent labels are scarce and heavily skewed toward benign accounts, and representation dilution, where spatial message passing may oversmooth camouflaged anomalies while spectral filters may suppress fraud-relevant mid- and high-frequency irregularities. To address these challenges, we propose ADC-GNN, short for Attention-guided Diffusion-Contrastive Graph Neural Network, a unified framework that combines diffusion-guided feature augmentation, contrastive representation learning, and multi-hop spectral attention for few-shot graph fraud detection. The diffusion component is formulated as a feature-space denoising augmentation mechanism rather than a full topology-generative graph diffusion model: it constructs noise-perturbed node-feature views under a cosine schedule and uses contrastive learning to stabilize node representations across perturbations. The spectral attention module further adaptively emphasizes fraud-relevant hop-level and relation-level cues. We evaluate ADC-GNN primarily on three public benchmarks and additionally report a proprietary real-world telecom transaction dataset with approximately 60,000 records as a private case study. Under the 1% training setting, ADC-GNN achieves consistent improvements over original graph fraud baselines and four protocol-consistent recent graph anomaly/fraud baselines on the public benchmarks. Additional analyses on split stability, training ratios, oversampling alternatives, module-level ablations, diffusion schedules, and runtime and memory-consumption comparisons further characterize the effective operating regime of ADC-GNN.

AI.tldr: ADC-GNN is a unified framework combining diffusion-guided feature augmentation, contrastive learning, and multi-hop spectral attention to address sparse/imbalanced supervision and representation dilution in graph-based fraud detection, achieving consistent improvements under 1% training settings on public benchmarks and a private telecom dataset.
AI.motivation: Real-world fraud graphs face two coupled challenges: (1) sparse and imbalanced supervision, where fraudulent labels are scarce and heavily skewed toward benign accounts, and (2) representation dilution, where spatial message passing oversmooths camouflaged anomalies and spectral filters suppress mid-/high-frequency fraud-relevant irregularities.
AI.method: ADC-GNN integrates three components: (a) diffusion-guided feature augmentation using noise-perturbed node-feature views under a cosine schedule, (b) contrastive representation learning to stabilize representations across perturbations, and (c) multi-hop spectral attention to adaptively emphasize fraud-relevant hop-level and relation-level cues.
AI.result: Under the 1% training setting, ADC-GNN achieves consistent improvements over original graph fraud baselines and four recent graph anomaly/fraud baselines on three public benchmarks. Additional analyses on split stability, training ratios, oversampling, ablations, diffusion schedules, and runtime/memory comparisons characterize its effective operating regime.
AI.conclusion: ADC-GNN effectively addresses few-shot graph fraud detection by combining denoising diffusion augmentation, contrastive stabilization, and spectral attention, offering a principled solution to sparse supervision and representation dilution in fraud graphs.

title: Parallel Rollout Approximation for Pixel-Space Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Pixel-space continuous-token autoregressive (AR) generation directly models images as sequences of raw pixel patches, avoiding discrete tokenization or a separately pretrained tokenizer. However, it faces coupled challenges: high-dimensional patch generation causes large single-step errors, and teacher-forced training creates a train--inference gap that makes these errors accumulate across AR steps. Existing fixes such as $x$-prediction and input noise injection only partially mitigate these issues. Exact rollout training better matches inference-time conditions, but is impractical due to prohibitively slow sequential sampling. We propose \emph{Parallel Rollout Approximation} (PRA), a scalable framework that addresses both challenges jointly. PRA generates low-dimensional intermediate states instead of high-dimensional pixel patches, then maps them back to pixel-space tokens with a pixel decoder, preserving a pixel-in, pixel-out AR interface. It also constructs inference-like pixel inputs through the same intermediate-state-to-pixel path used at inference, independently across positions, approximating the pixel-feedback interface encountered during inference-time rollout while retaining parallel teacher-forced training. On class-conditional ImageNet-1K generation at $256\times256$ resolution, PRA-S with 135M parameters achieves an FID of 2.58, surpassing the previous billion-scale pixel-space AR result of 3.60. Scaling to PRA-L with 511M parameters further improves FID to 1.94, establishing a new state of the art among pixel-space AR models. Beyond generation, PRA achieves higher ImageNet classification probing accuracy than other AR and diffusion baselines, suggesting its potential for unified pixel-space image generation and understanding.

AI.tldr: Pixel-space autoregressive generation suffers from high-dimensional patch errors and train-inference gap. PRA introduces low-dimensional intermediate states with a pixel decoder to reduce error magnitude and approximates inference-time pixel feedback during parallel training. Achieves SOTA FID of 1.94 on ImageNet-1K 256×256 with 511M parameters, surpassing prior billion-scale results, and shows strong classification probing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Pixel-space continuous-token AR generation faces two coupled challenges: (1) high-dimensional patch generation leads to large single-step errors, and (2) teacher-forced training creates a train-inference gap causing error accumulation during AR rollout. Existing fixes (x-prediction, noise injection, exact rollout) are only partial or impractical due to slow sequential sampling.
AI.method: PRA generates low-dimensional intermediate states instead of high-dimensional pixel patches, then maps them back to pixel-space tokens via a pixel decoder, preserving a pixel-in, pixel-out interface. It constructs inference-like pixel inputs through the same intermediate-state-to-pixel path used at inference, independently across positions, approximating pixel-feedback during rollout while retaining parallel teacher-forced training.
AI.result: PRA-S (135M): FID 2.58 (surpasses prior billion-scale pixel-space AR result 3.60). PRA-L (511M): FID 1.94 (new SOTA among pixel-space AR models). Also achieves higher ImageNet classification probing accuracy than other AR and diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: PRA effectively addresses the high-dimensional error and train-inference gap challenges in pixel-space AR generation, achieving scalable SOTA results with relatively small models, and shows potential for unified pixel-space image generation and understanding.

title: MixTTA: Low-Rank Cross-Channel Mixing for Reliable Test-Time Adaptation
comment: To be published in the 19th European Conference on Computer Vision -- ECCV 2026
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) methods commonly update the affine parameters of normalization layers to adapt deployed models under distribution shifts. However, per-channel affine parameters perform axis-aligned scaling and shifting, making them geometrically incapable of correcting cross-channel structural changes induced by distribution shift. To address this limitation, we propose MixTTA, a lightweight plug-in module that equips normalization layers with a low-rank cross-channel transformation, enabling inter-channel mixing at each layer. To ensure that the low-rank branch captures only cross-channel interactions, we also propose Decoupling Projection that enforces strict separation from the diagonal affine path, along with Spectral Projection that prevents rank-1 collapse under non-stationary test streams. MixTTA can be seamlessly integrated into any existing normalization-based TTA method. Experiments in both standard and wild TTA settings show consistent improvements over strong baselines while mitigating adaptation failure under challenging conditions. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/delta6189/MixTTA.

AI.tldr: MixTTA introduces a low-rank cross-channel transformation module for normalization layers in Test-Time Adaptation, addressing the geometric limitations of traditional per-channel affine parameters under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Standard TTA methods update per-channel affine parameters of normalization layers, but these axis-aligned scaling/shifting operations cannot correct cross-channel structural changes caused by distribution shifts.
AI.method: MixTTA is a plug-in module adding low-rank cross-channel transformation to normalization layers, with Decoupling Projection to separate it from diagonal affine paths and Spectral Projection to prevent rank-1 collapse under non-stationary test streams.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong baselines in both standard and wild TTA settings, with mitigated adaptation failure under challenging conditions.
AI.conclusion: MixTTA effectively enables inter-channel mixing at each layer, overcoming the geometric limitations of axis-aligned affine parameters, and integrates seamlessly into existing normalization-based TTA methods.

title: Mind the Gap: Quantifying the Domain Gap in Cross-Sensor Diffusion Super-Resolution
summary: Demand for high-resolution satellite imagery has increased interest in super-resolution (SR) to bridge the spatial resolution gap between freely available missions such as Sentinel-2 and commercial systems like PlanetScope. Because no sensor provides true paired low- and high-resolution observations, SR models are usually trained on synthetically degraded data, creating a domain gap on real cross-sensor imagery. In this work, we provide the first systematic study of how this synthetic-to-real mismatch affects the performance of modern diffusion-based SR models. Using a large, geometrically and temporally aligned dataset of Sentinel-2 and PlanetScope imagery, we evaluate five state-of-the-art diffusion architectures under controlled experimental settings. We also introduce LPIPS-Sat, a domain-adapted perceptual metric based on Sentinel-2 self-supervised features. Our results show two persistent challenges: synthetically trained models degrade sharply on real pairs, while models trained on real cross-sensor data exhibit optimisation difficulties and struggle to adapt to the physical and radiometric diversity. These findings highlight a key limitation of current SR and motivate methods that disentangle super-resolution from domain adaptation.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies how synthetic-to-real domain mismatch affects diffusion-based super-resolution models for satellite imagery, using aligned Sentinel-2/PlanetScope data and introduces LPIPS-Sat metric.
AI.motivation: The lack of truly paired low/high-resolution satellite observations forces SR models to train on synthetic degraded data, creating a domain gap that degrades performance on real cross-sensor imagery. No prior work had systematically studied this mismatch for diffusion-based SR models.
AI.method: The authors construct a large, geometrically and temporally aligned dataset of Sentinel-2 (low-res) and PlanetScope (high-res) imagery. They evaluate five state-of-the-art diffusion architectures under controlled settings and introduce LPIPS-Sat, a domain-adapted perceptual metric based on Sentinel-2 self-supervised features.
AI.result: Two persistent challenges identified: (1) synthetically trained models degrade sharply on real cross-sensor pairs, and (2) models trained on real data exhibit optimisation difficulties and struggle with physical/radiometric diversity.
AI.conclusion: Current super-resolution approaches face a key limitation due to the synthetic-to-real gap, motivating future methods that explicitly disentangle super-resolution from domain adaptation.

title: Autoencoder Architectures for Athlete Performance Scoring from Wearable Telemetry
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, submitted to SPA 2026 Conference https://spaconference.org.pl/
summary: Wearable devices produce large, high dimensional training logs for everyday runners, and interpretation rather than data collection is now the limiting step. This paper evaluates five dimensionality reduction models, three autoencoder variants, PCA, and a Variational Autoencoder, on their ability to compress nine sensor runner profiles into a single scalar performance indicator, the latent score. Because the setting is fully unsupervised, model quality is assessed along two complementary axes: reconstruction error (Mean Squared Error) and latent score interpretability, measured via Spearman and Kendall rank correlations, Mutual Information, and Permutation Importance. These are combined into a composite selection criterion that prevents selecting models on reconstruction accuracy alone. Feature rankings from the four metrics are aggregated via a modified Borda count, and their stability is confirmed by bootstrap validation. A two feature linear baseline is included to anchor the comparison. Deep autoencoder achieved the lowest reconstruction error and the highest composite score. Once the PCA hidden layers were widened, the deeper variants became closely competitive with Deep AE on the composite criterion, indicating that the limiting factor was hidden layer capacity rather than the one dimensional bottleneck. Running pace, aerobic decoupling, and average heart rate emerged as the dominant latent score drivers across all models and resampling runs, consistent with established physiology.

AI.tldr: This paper compares five dimensionality reduction models (autoencoder variants, PCA, VAE) for compressing nine wearable sensor features into a single latent performance score for runners. Evaluation combines reconstruction error (MSE) with interpretability metrics (rank correlations, mutual information, permutation importance). Deep autoencoder performs best overall, though wider PCA layers narrow the gap. Pace, aerobic decoupling, and average heart rate are the most important features across all models.
AI.motivation: Wearable devices generate large, high-dimensional training logs for everyday runners. Data collection is no longer the bottleneck—interpretation is. The authors aim to find an unsupervised method that compresses nine sensor-derived features into a single interpretable performance indicator.
AI.method: Five models are evaluated: three autoencoder variants, PCA, and a Variational Autoencoder. Quality is assessed via reconstruction error (MSE) and latent score interpretability (Spearman/Kendall rank correlations, Mutual Information, Permutation Importance). A composite criterion prevents selection based solely on reconstruction accuracy. Feature rankings are aggregated via modified Borda count, with bootstrap validation for stability. A two-feature linear baseline anchors comparison.
AI.result: Deep autoencoder achieved the lowest reconstruction error and highest composite score. Widening PCA hidden layers made deeper PCA variants closely competitive with Deep AE, suggesting hidden layer capacity (not the one-dimensional bottleneck) was the limiting factor. Running pace, aerobic decoupling, and average heart rate emerged as the dominant latent score drivers across all models and resampling runs.
AI.conclusion: Deep autoencoders are effective for unsupervised compression of runner sensor data into a single interpretable score. Model capacity matters more than bottleneck dimensionality. The dominant features align with established exercise physiology, validating the approach.

title: Controllable Histopathology Image Synthesis with Training-free Structural Initialization and Textural Modulation
summary:
Deep learning has demonstrated remarkable success in high-throughput histopathology image analysis. However, the performance of learning-based models critically depends on the quality and size of annotations by expert pathologists, which is a resource-intensive and time-consuming process. To address the limitations of data scarcity and annotation burden, several methods have been proposed to synthesize paired histopathology data. Nevertheless, these frameworks typically still require annotation data, albeit in reduced quantities, to impose structural constraints during training.
  In this work, we present CHIS, a plug-in framework that guides the sampling trajectory of a pretrained diffusion model through two key stages: structural initialization at the start and textural modulation during generation. The initial noise state is refined by fusing the phase information from a prior mask with the amplitude of Gaussian noise in the frequency domain, yielding a structurally informed starting point. During the reverse diffusion process, we adaptively modulate both coarse-grained and fine-grained textures at different wavelet decomposition levels. This enables a diffusion model pretrained solely on unlabeled images to generate outputs that align with prior structural masks while preserving the reference tissue style.
  We conducted extensive experiments demonstrating the superiority of CHIS in generation fidelity and its substantial benefits for downstream segmentation tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/IBIL-Code/CHIS.

AI.tldr: CHIS is a plug-in framework that guides pretrained diffusion models to generate histopathology images conditioned on structural masks without requiring labeled training data, using frequency-domain phase initialization and wavelet-based texture modulation.
AI.motivation: Deep learning in histopathology requires large expert-annotated datasets, which are resource-intensive. Existing synthesis methods still need annotation data for structural constraints. CHIS aims to eliminate the need for any annotated training data while enabling conditional generation from structural masks.
AI.method: Two-stage guidance for pretrained diffusion models: (1) Structural initialization by fusing phase information from a prior mask with amplitude of Gaussian noise in the frequency domain; (2) Textural modulation during reverse diffusion using adaptive control of coarse-grained and fine-grained textures at different wavelet decomposition levels.
AI.result: CHIS achieves superior generation fidelity and provides substantial benefits for downstream segmentation tasks, as demonstrated through extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: CHIS enables a diffusion model pretrained solely on unlabeled images to generate outputs aligned with prior structural masks while preserving reference tissue style, effectively addressing data scarcity and annotation burden in histopathology.

title: Regularized Reward-Punishment Reinforcement Learning
summary: We propose KL-Coupled Policy Regularization (KCPR), a policy coordination framework for Reward-Punishment Reinforcement Learning (RPRL). Based on KCPR, we derive KL-Coupled Soft Optimality (KCSO) and develop its deep realization, klDMP. Unlike existing RPRL approaches that optimize reward-seeking and punishment-related policies largely independently, KCPR enables direct interactions between companion policies by treating each as a dynamically learned prior for the other. KCSO yields coupled soft-optimal policies and KL-regularized Bellman operators, allowing reward and punishment information to jointly influence value propagation. To improve learning stability, we introduce a companion-prior softening mechanism and evaluate separate replay-buffer designs for balancing reward- and punishment-related experience. Experiments in grid-world and Gazebo robotic navigation tasks demonstrate that klDMP improves safety and learning stability while maintaining competitive task performance compared with DQN, SQL and softDMP. These results suggest that policy-level coordination provides an effective mechanism for integrating multiple behavioral objectives and may serve as a useful design principle for reinforcement learning systems with interacting motivational processes.

AI.tldr: KL-Coupled Policy Regularization (KCPR) is a framework for Reward-Punishment RL that coordinates reward-seeking and punishment-avoiding policies through mutual KL regularization, leading to coupled soft-optimal policies (KCSO) and a deep algorithm (klDMP) that improves safety and stability in navigation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing RPRL approaches optimize reward-seeking and punishment-related policies independently, lacking direct interaction between companion policies. This separation limits the ability to jointly integrate multiple behavioral objectives.
AI.method: KCPR treats each companion policy as a dynamically learned prior for the other via KL regularization. KCSO yields coupled soft-optimal policies and KL-regularized Bellman operators for joint value propagation. A companion-prior softening mechanism and separate replay-buffer designs are introduced for stability.
AI.result: In grid-world and Gazebo robotic navigation tasks, klDMP improves safety and learning stability while maintaining competitive task performance compared to DQN, SQL, and softDMP baselines.
AI.conclusion: Policy-level coordination via KL coupling provides an effective mechanism for integrating multiple behavioral objectives and may serve as a useful design principle for RL systems with interacting motivational processes.

title: Understanding How MLLMs Describe Artworks Using Token Activation Maps
comment: Accepted at PRESTIGE workshop at ICPR 2026
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) describe artworks with remarkable fluency, yet the visual reasoning behind their outputs remains opaque. When an MLLM names a style, identifies a subject, or recognizes an iconographic symbol, does it ground each claim in the relevant region of the canvas, draw on an undifferentiated visual signal, or rely primarily on textual priors? We study this using the Token Activation Map (TAM), which produces, for each generated token, a heatmap isolating the visual evidence specific to that token from prior-context interference. Applying TAM to a curated set of paintings spanning multiple periods and genres, we analyze grounding patterns across five semantically distinct token categories: common visual objects, style descriptors, metadata, iconographic tokens, and affective expressions. We find that visual grounding varies substantially with token semantics. We further show that MLLMs attempt to identify artworks and artists, achieving higher accuracy in artist attribution than in title prediction, where hallucinations are more frequent. Finally, we compare TAM with SAM~3 open-vocabulary segmentation. To ensure reproducibility, we release our code, experimental configurations, prompts, and qualitative results on the project page at https://nicolafan.github.io/tamart/.

AI.tldr: This paper uses Token Activation Maps (TAM) to analyze visual grounding in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) when describing artworks, finding that grounding varies by token semantics, and that MLLMs achieve higher accuracy in artist attribution than title prediction, with hallucinations more common in the latter.
AI.motivation: To understand whether MLLMs ground their descriptions of artworks in specific visual regions of the canvas, rely on undifferentiated visual signals, or depend primarily on textual priors—addressing the opacity of visual reasoning in these models.
AI.method: The authors apply Token Activation Map (TAM) to generate per-token heatmaps isolating visual evidence for each generated token. They analyze grounding patterns across five token categories: common visual objects, style descriptors, metadata, iconographic tokens, and affective expressions. They also compare TAM with SAM-3 open-vocabulary segmentation.
AI.result: Visual grounding varies substantially with token semantics. MLLMs show higher accuracy in artist attribution than in title prediction. Hallucinations are more frequent in title prediction than artist attribution.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs' visual grounding is not uniform—it depends on the semantic type of the generated token. The models perform better on artist identification than artwork title prediction, where hallucinations are more common.

title: Recovering Sharp Conductivity Features in the Finite-Data Calderón Problem with Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have recently emerged as a promising framework for addressing the Calderón inverse problem from limited boundary data. In this work, we revisit neural Calderón inversion by introducing multiscale boundary excitations based on randomized wavelet functions and investigating the role of Fourier-feature encoding (FFE) for representing sharp conductivity variations. We propose a physics-informed reconstruction framework that represents the unknown conductivity and the associated family of electric potentials with separate neural networks conditioned on the applied boundary excitations. The governing elliptic PDE is enforced through physics-informed residuals, while finite Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) data are incorporated through boundary losses. Using synthetic data from a finite-difference forward solver, we evaluate the method on conductivity fields with inclusions, sharp interfaces, smooth profiles, and heterogeneous media. Results show that the framework recovers dominant conductivity structures from finite boundary measurements with relative errors between $3\%-12\%$ approximately. We show that FFE improves the reconstruction of localized sharp features, particularly for inclusions and interfaces, but are not universally optimal, with raw-coordinate networks performing competitively for smoother fields. These results highlight coordinate representations and boundary excitation design as key factors in neural Calderón inversion.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a physics-informed neural network framework for the Calderón inverse problem, using multiscale wavelet boundary excitations and Fourier-feature encoding (FFE) to reconstruct conductivity fields from limited boundary data. The method achieves 3-12% relative errors, with FFE improving sharp feature recovery but raw-coordinate networks performing better for smoother fields.
AI.motivation: To improve neural network-based solutions for the Calderón inverse problem (recovering internal conductivity from boundary measurements) by introducing multiscale randomized wavelet boundary excitations and investigating the role of Fourier-feature encoding in representing sharp conductivity variations.
AI.method: Separate neural networks are used to represent unknown conductivity and electric potentials, conditioned on boundary excitations. The elliptic PDE is enforced via physics-informed residuals, and finite Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) data are incorporated through boundary losses. Multiscale random wavelet functions are used as boundary excitations, and Fourier-feature encoding (FFE) is tested alongside raw-coordinate networks.
AI.result: The framework recovers dominant conductivity structures with 3-12% relative errors. FFE improves reconstruction of sharp features (inclusions, interfaces) but is not universally optimal; raw-coordinate networks perform competitively for smoother conductivity fields.
AI.conclusion: Coordinate representations (FFE vs. raw coordinates) and boundary excitation design are key factors in neural Calderón inversion; FFE is beneficial for sharp features but not universally superior.

title: Directing the World: Fast Autoregressive Video Generation with Compositional Human-Camera Control
summary: Building interactive world models requires generating realistic videos while maintaining controllable dynamics over long horizons. Autoregressive video generation offers a scalable foundation, but suffers from error accumulation and temporal degradation during extended rollouts. This issue is further amplified under heterogeneous controls such as human motion and camera trajectories, which may interfere and destabilize a pretrained video prior, while existing methods often trade off controllability and visual quality. We propose "Directing the World", a fast autoregressive framework for controllable world-model video generation with compositional human-motion and camera-trajectory control. Our key idea is to decouple control learning while preserving a unified autoregressive video prior. We introduce a Fast-Slow Memory training strategy to stabilize long-horizon rollout learning and improve convergence. For human motion control, we design a t-guided Dynamic Projection mechanism and a refined Motion-CFG strategy, enabling temporally smooth and accurate motion alignment without degrading visual fidelity, and supporting multi-person control.After learning a robust motion prior, we introduce a second-stage camera-trajectory control module to compose human dynamics with viewpoint changes for coherent world exploration. We further construct a large-scale dataset with synchronized video, text, human-motion, and camera-trajectory annotations, organized into motion-centric and camera-centric subsets for decoupled training. Extensive experiments show stable long-horizon generation with precise controllability and high visual quality. See more at https://whydahuzi.github.io/Directing-the-World.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Directing the World is a fast autoregressive framework for controllable world-model video generation that decouples human-motion and camera-trajectory control while preserving a unified video prior, using Fast-Slow Memory training, t-guided Dynamic Projection, and Motion-CFG to achieve stable long-horizon generation with precise controllability and high visual quality.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive video generation suffers from error accumulation and temporal degradation during long rollouts, especially under heterogeneous controls (e.g., human motion, camera trajectories) that can interfere with the pretrained video prior, and current methods often trade off controllability for visual quality.
AI.method: (1) Fast-Slow Memory training strategy to stabilize long-horizon rollout learning; (2) t-guided Dynamic Projection mechanism and refined Motion-CFG for temporally smooth, accurate human motion alignment; (3) Second-stage camera-trajectory control module composing human dynamics with viewpoint changes; (4) Large-scale dataset with synchronized video, text, human-motion, and camera-trajectory annotations for decoupled training.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate stable long-horizon generation with precise controllability and high visual quality, supporting multi-person control and coherent world exploration.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling control learning while preserving a unified autoregressive video prior enables effective compositional human-motion and camera-trajectory control for long-horizon world-model video generation without degrading visual fidelity.

title: CPAgents: Agentic Composite Phenotype Generation for Cardiac Disease Association
summary: Identifying robust associations between cardiac imaging phenotypes and clinical diseases is fundamental to population-scale cardiovascular research and reliable risk stratification. However, current phenome-wide association studies rely on pre-defined, single-variable phenotypes or expert-crafted features, which limits their ability to capture clinically meaningful non-linear effects and cross-phenotype interactions. To address this, we propose CPAgents, an iterative phenotype-Composition framework for cardiovascular Phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) that automatically constructs and validates interpretable composite phenotypes (e.g., polynomial, ratio, and interaction forms) from base imaging features. Specifically, our system coordinates three agents: (i) an Analyst that identifies statistical pathologies and nominates candidate transformations; (ii) a Proposer that generates constrained, medically and statistically motivated expressions under numerical safety rules; and (iii) a Verifier that evaluates candidates using multi-stage criteria and produces transparent evidence trails for accepted phenotypes. Evaluated on a population-scale cardiac imaging cohort, the discovered composite phenotypes markedly improve disease discrimination: across 72 classifier-disease-metric combinations, our variants achieve the top rank in 56 cases versus 18 for baselines, with gains observed across all nine clinical disease categories. Our framework yields compact, clinically interpretable phenotype formulas with transparent evidence trails, enabling scalable discovery of stronger phenotype-disease associations beyond expert-driven feature selection.

AI.tldr: CPAgents is an iterative phenotype-composition framework for cardiovascular PheWAS that automatically constructs and validates interpretable composite phenotypes (polynomial, ratio, interaction forms) from base imaging features via three coordinated agents (Analyst, Proposer, Verifier), achieving top-rank disease discrimination in 56/72 classifier-disease-metric combinations across nine clinical disease categories.
AI.motivation: Current PheWAS rely on pre-defined single-variable phenotypes or expert-crafted features, limiting their ability to capture clinically meaningful non-linear effects and cross-phenotype interactions for robust cardiovascular risk stratification.
AI.method: Proposes CPAgents with three coordinated agents: (i) Analyst identifies statistical pathologies and nominates candidate transformations; (ii) Proposer generates constrained, medically and statistically motivated expressions under numerical safety rules; (iii) Verifier evaluates candidates using multi-stage criteria and produces transparent evidence trails.
AI.result: Evaluated on a population-scale cardiac imaging cohort; across 72 classifier-disease-metric combinations, CPAgents variants achieve top rank in 56 cases versus 18 for baselines, with gains across all nine clinical disease categories.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields compact, clinically interpretable phenotype formulas with transparent evidence trails, enabling scalable discovery of stronger phenotype-disease associations beyond expert-driven feature selection.

title: OSOR: One-Step Diffusion Inpainting for Effect-Aware Object Removal
comment: Code and resources are available at https://github.com/Zhouqm-Git/osor
summary: Real-world object removal is challenging due to two key difficulties: the target object's non-local effects, such as shadows and reflections, which are difficult to model, and the fact that user-provided masks are often inaccurate or incomplete. With billions of parameters and tens of denoising steps, diffusion-based models achieve strong removal performance at the expense of substantial computational cost, limiting their use in interactive applications and on edge devices. To address these challenges, we present OSOR (One-Step Object Removal), which simultaneously achieves efficient, effect-aware, and mask-robust object removal. Concretely, OSOR introduces: (1) an occupancy-guided discriminator for precise boundary supervision, enabling stable single-step diffusion training; (2) an alpha head that leverages knowledge from pretrained diffusion models to predict appropriate removal regions with minimal overhead, thereby handling imperfect masks; and (3) a semantic-anchored verification pipeline (SAVP) that filters noisy instruction-based triplets to produce effect-aware supervision at scale. Using SAVP, we curate CORNE, which contains 280K verified removal pairs, and further annotate AnimeEraseBench and TextEraseBench to evaluate performance on more complex removal tasks. Experiments show that OSOR surpasses strong multi-step diffusion baselines in perceptual quality while achieving $4\times$ to $30\times$ faster inference.

AI.tldr: OSOR (One-Step Object Removal) is a diffusion-based model that achieves efficient, effect-aware, and mask-robust object removal in a single step, using an occupancy-guided discriminator, an alpha head for handling imperfect masks, and a semantic-anchored verification pipeline (SAVP) for effect-aware supervision, outperforming multi-step diffusion baselines in perceptual quality while being 4× to 30× faster.
AI.motivation: Real-world object removal faces two key challenges: the target object's non-local effects (shadows, reflections) are difficult to model, and user-provided masks are often inaccurate or incomplete. Existing diffusion-based models achieve strong removal but require billions of parameters and tens of denoising steps, incurring substantial computational cost that limits interactive and edge-device applications.
AI.method: OSOR introduces three components: (1) an occupancy-guided discriminator for precise boundary supervision enabling stable single-step diffusion training; (2) an alpha head leveraging pretrained diffusion knowledge to predict appropriate removal regions with minimal overhead, handling imperfect masks; (3) a semantic-anchored verification pipeline (SAVP) that filters noisy instruction-based triplets to produce effect-aware supervision at scale. The authors curated CORNE (280K verified pairs) and annotated AnimeEraseBench and TextEraseBench for evaluation.
AI.result: OSOR surpasses strong multi-step diffusion baselines in perceptual quality while achieving 4× to 30× faster inference.
AI.conclusion: OSOR simultaneously achieves efficiency, effect-awareness, and mask-robustness for object removal in a single-step diffusion framework, making it suitable for interactive applications and edge devices.

title: LLawCo: Learning Laws of Cooperation for Modeling Embodied Multi-Agent Behavior
summary: Embodied agents operating in decentralized and partially observable environments have attracted growing attention in recent years. However, existing large language model (LLM)-based agents often exhibit behaviors that are misaligned with their partners or inconsistent with the environment state, leading to inefficient cooperation and poor task success. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework, Learning Laws of Cooperation (LLawCo), that enables embodied agents to autonomously align with both their partners and task objectives. Our framework allows agents to reflect on past failures to extract misaligned behavioral patterns, which are used to derive high-level behavioral laws, such as "Talk when necessary" and "Wait for partner." These laws are explicitly incorporated into the agents' chains of thought via supervised fine-tuning, aligning their reasoning with task requirements and the behavior of other agents. To evaluate our approach, we introduce PARTNR-Dialog, a large-scale multi-agent communicative and cooperative planning benchmark built on the PARTNR environment. Experiments on existing tasks and our new benchmark demonstrate significant improvements in cooperative efficiency and task success rates. Across four backbone LLMs, our method achieves average success rate improvements of 4.5% on the PARTNR-Dialog benchmark and 6.8% on the TDW-MAT benchmark over state-of-the-art open-source communicative agent frameworks. See the LLawCo project page for details: https://www.merl.com/research/highlights/LLawCo

AI.tldr: LLawCo is a framework enabling embodied agents to autonomously align with partners and task objectives by extracting misaligned behavioral patterns from past failures, deriving high-level behavioral laws (e.g., "Talk when necessary," "Wait for partner"), and incorporating them into LLM chains of thought via supervised fine-tuning. It introduces PARTNR-Dialog benchmark and shows 4.5% and 6.8% average success rate improvements over SOTA on two benchmarks across four backbone LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based embodied agents in decentralized partially observable environments often exhibit misaligned behaviors with partners or inconsistent with environment state, leading to inefficient cooperation and poor task success.
AI.method: LLawCo enables agents to reflect on past failures to extract misaligned behavioral patterns, derive high-level behavioral laws (e.g., "Talk when necessary," "Wait for partner"), and incorporate these laws into agents' chains of thought via supervised fine-tuning to align reasoning with task requirements and partner behavior.
AI.result: Across four backbone LLMs, LLawCo achieves average success rate improvements of 4.5% on the PARTNR-Dialog benchmark and 6.8% on the TDW-MAT benchmark over state-of-the-art open-source communicative agent frameworks.
AI.conclusion: LLawCo significantly improves cooperative efficiency and task success rates in multi-agent embodied settings by autonomously learning and applying high-level behavioral laws distilled from past failures.

title: BiDeMem: Bidirectional Degradation Memory for Explainable Image Restoration
summary: Degradation-aware prompts, conditions, and latent priors are increasingly used in image restoration, yet they are usually judged by a single endpoint: whether the restored image obtains higher PSNR. This is a weak test of semantics. A condition can help by adding capacity, acting as a global correction bias, or exploiting dataset shortcuts, without becoming an interpretable degradation prior. We propose BiDeMem, a bidirectional degradation memory for explainable image restoration. A query built from restoration features and input statistics retrieves a compact top-k subset of memory slots. The same selected slot identity supports the restoration path at inference time and a training-only forward-degradation explanation path. The study centers on verifiability in a controlled multi-degradation NAFNet setting. New controls separate the gain from a correction head alone, a dense query prior, and a static global prior: these variants are 0.2588, 0.2586, and 0.2839 dB below BiRank, respectively. Strong residual supervision and a wider degradation head also remain below the full bidirectional memory model. Intervention probes show that BiRank preserves restoration quality while increasing wrong-prior and native-prior sensitivity, framing degradation memory as both a restoration module and a falsifiable explanation mechanism.

AI.tldr: BiDeMem introduces a bidirectional degradation memory mechanism for explainable image restoration, using a top-k query from restoration features to retrieve interpretable degradation priors. It outperforms variants (correction head, dense query, static prior) and provides falsifiable explanations while maintaining restoration quality.
AI.motivation: Existing image restoration methods rely on degradation-aware prompts/conditions but only evaluate via PSNR, which is a weak semantic test. These conditions often act as capacity-adding biases or exploit dataset shortcuts without being interpretable degradation priors.
AI.method: BiDeMem uses a query built from restoration features and input statistics to retrieve a compact top-k subset of memory slots. The same selected slot identity supports both the restoration path (inference) and a forward-degradation explanation path (training-only). Controlled variants (correction head, dense query prior, static global prior) are compared.
AI.result: Variants are 0.2588 dB (correction head), 0.2586 dB (dense query prior), and 0.2839 dB (static global prior) below BiRank. Intervention probes show BiRank preserves restoration quality while increasing wrong-prior and native-prior sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: The bidirectional memory model frames degradation memory as both a restoration module and a falsifiable explanation mechanism, outperforming non-interpretable alternatives.

title: The Remittance Blueprint: Data-driven Intelligence for Sri Lanka
summary: This study analyzes Sri Lankan migration and remittances over 32 years (1994-2025). Using a 384-month harmonized dataset, we apply exploratory data analysis, stationarity corrected time-series modeling (ADF, Johansen, VAR/VECM), and supervised learning. Results reveal remittance inflows are primarily driven by external macroeconomic variables, specifically exchange rate dynamics and global oil prices, rather than domestic indicators. Impulse response analysis confirms the asymmetric impact of currency depreciation and oil price shocks. Predictively, multivariate machine learning models outperform traditional univariate approaches; Ridge Regression achieves a 73.8% accuracy improvement over SARIMA (Annualized RMSE: USD 494.8 Mn). The optimized framework projects 2026 remittances at USD 9,001 million under stable conditions. These findings highlight the structural dependence of remittances on global economies, emphasizing the need for robust exchange rate policies, skilled migration, and formal financial channels to enhance long-term economic resilience.

AI.tldr: Over 32 years (1994-2025) of Sri Lankan migration data, this study finds remittance inflows are driven by external factors (exchange rates, oil prices) rather than domestic indicators, with Ridge Regression achieving 73.8% accuracy improvement over SARIMA, projecting 2026 remittances at USD 9,001 million.
AI.motivation: To understand the structural drivers of Sri Lankan remittance inflows over a long temporal horizon and assess whether domestic or external macroeconomic factors govern remittance behavior.
AI.method: Analysis of a 384-month harmonized dataset (1994-2025) using exploratory data analysis, stationarity-corrected time-series modeling (ADF, Johansen cointegration, VAR/VECM), impulse response analysis, and supervised learning including SARIMA and Ridge Regression.
AI.result: Remittance inflows are driven by exchange rate dynamics and global oil prices, not domestic indicators. Ridge Regression outperforms SARIMA with 73.8% improvement in Annualized RMSE (USD 494.8 Mn). 2026 projection: USD 9,001 million under stable conditions.
AI.conclusion: Sri Lankan remittances are structurally dependent on global economies, requiring robust exchange rate policies, skilled migration promotion, and formal financial channels for long-term economic resilience.

title: Latent Visual Diffusion Reasoning with Monte Carlo Tree Search
summary: Analyzing fine-grained skill activities (e.g., sports, surgery) requires not only recognizing visual patterns but also performing step-by-step visual reasoning that leads to the final judgment. While recent advances in action quality assessment have achieved remarkable progress in evaluating performance, existing models remain black boxes, where they lack the ability to explicitly reveal the reasoning processes underlying their judgments. To address this limitation, we propose Latent Visual Diffusion Reasoning (LVDR), a novel framework that integrates keypoint-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to model and visualize the latent visual reasoning process. LVDR not only produces more accurate skill assessments but also uncovers the critical visual reasoning sequences that contribute to the final evaluation. Extensive experiments across four datasets spanning diverse sports and surgical domains demonstrate that LVDR achieves competitive quantitative performance while providing interpretable visual reasoning trajectories leading to the final predictions. Source codes and models can be found through the following link: https://github.com/XiruiTeng/LVDR_Official.git.

AI.tldr: LVDR integrates keypoint-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search with latent visual diffusion to perform interpretable step-by-step visual reasoning for fine-grained skill assessment, achieving competitive accuracy while revealing the reasoning trajectories behind its judgments.
AI.motivation: Existing action quality assessment models are black boxes that cannot explicitly reveal the reasoning processes underlying their judgments; there is a need for interpretable visual reasoning in fine-grained skill activity analysis.
AI.method: Latent Visual Diffusion Reasoning (LVDR) framework that combines keypoint-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with latent visual diffusion to model and visualize step-by-step visual reasoning sequences for skill assessment.
AI.result: Competitive quantitative performance across four datasets spanning sports and surgical domains, with the added benefit of interpretable visual reasoning trajectories leading to final predictions.
AI.conclusion: LVDR effectively addresses the interpretability gap in action quality assessment by providing both accurate skill evaluations and transparent reasoning processes, with open-source code and models available.

title: PhysisForcing: Physics Reinforced World Simulator for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Video generation models have emerged as a promising paradigm for embodied world simulation. However, both general-domain video generators and robot-specific data fine-tuned models can still produce physically implausible manipulations, including discontinuous motion trajectories and inconsistent robot-object interactions, which limits their reliability as world simulators. Through extensive experiments, we find that such physical instability mainly arises from two factors: deformation of moving objects and implausible spatio-temporal correlations among interacting entities, particularly during contact. Building on this observation, we propose PhysisForcing, a scalable training framework that strengthens physical consistency by focusing supervision on physics-informative regions through joint optimization of pixel-level and semantic-level features. The framework consists of a pixel-level trajectory alignment loss, which supervises DiT features using reference point trajectories, and a semantic-level relational alignment loss, which aligns DiT features with inter-region relations extracted from a frozen video understanding encoder. Extensive experiments on R-Bench, PAI-Bench, and EZS-Bench show that PhysisForcing consistently improves embodied video generation over strong baselines, improving the Wan2.2-I2V-A14B and Cosmos3-Nano base models on R-Bench by 22.3\% and 9.2\% (7.1\% and 3.7\% over vanilla finetuning), with the Cosmos3-Nano variant attaining the best overall score. Beyond generation, as a world model under the WorldArena action-planner protocol it raises the closed-loop success rate from 16.0\% to 24.0\% and further improves downstream policy success, indicating that physically aligned video models yield stronger representations for robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: PhysisForcing is a training framework that improves physical consistency in embodied video generation by supervising physics-informative regions via pixel-level trajectory alignment and semantic-level relational alignment losses, achieving significant gains across multiple benchmarks and improving downstream robotic policy success.
AI.motivation: Video generation models for embodied world simulation often produce physically implausible manipulations (discontinuous motion, inconsistent robot-object interactions), limiting reliability. The authors identify two key causes: deformation of moving objects and implausible spatio-temporal correlations during contact.
AI.method: PhysisForcing: a scalable training framework with (1) pixel-level trajectory alignment loss that supervises DiT features using reference point trajectories, and (2) semantic-level relational alignment loss that aligns DiT features with inter-region relations from a frozen video understanding encoder.
AI.result: Improves Wan2.2-I2V-A14B and Cosmos3-Nano on R-Bench by 22.3% and 9.2% (7.1% and 3.7% over vanilla finetuning); Cosmos3-Nano variant achieves best overall score. Under WorldArena, raises closed-loop success rate from 16.0% to 24.0% and improves downstream policy success.
AI.conclusion: Physically aligned video models yield stronger representations for robotic manipulation, demonstrating that focusing supervision on physics-informative regions improves both generation quality and downstream policy performance.

title: COCOLogic-V2: Identifying Logical Inconsistencies via Truly Hard-Negatives
summary: While interpretable models such as concept bottleneck models (CBMs) and program synthesis methods enable verification of model decisions, their evaluation is typically limited to simple tasks, leaving complex reasoning on real-world images largely unexplored. We introduce COCOLogic-V2, an object-centric dataset for visual inductive reasoning on real-world images covering a broad subset of first-order logic. By categorizing samples into positive variants, near-boundary (NB), and far-from-boundary (FB) negatives, COCOLogic-V2 enables fine-grained diagnosis of model accountability. Our evaluations show that models tend to separate positive and FB samples well but fail on NB samples, while perceptual noise and large rule-induced search spaces pose additional challenges in few-shot settings. Together, these results highlight that visual inductive reasoning remains an open challenge and COCOLogic-V2 provides a concrete foundation for advancing methods in this direction.

AI.tldr: COCOLogic-V2 is a new object-centric dataset for visual inductive reasoning on real-world images using first-order logic, with fine-grained sample categorization (positive, near-boundary negatives, far-boundary negatives) that reveals models struggle with near-boundary samples, perceptual noise, and large search spaces in few-shot settings, showing visual inductive reasoning remains an open challenge.
AI.motivation: Existing interpretable models like CBMs and program synthesis are typically evaluated on simple tasks, leaving complex reasoning on real-world images largely unexplored. The authors aim to bridge this gap by providing a benchmark for visual inductive reasoning with fine-grained diagnostic capability.
AI.method: The authors introduce COCOLogic-V2, an object-centric dataset built on real-world images covering a broad subset of first-order logic. Samples are categorized into positive variants, near-boundary (NB) negatives, and far-from-boundary (FB) negatives for fine-grained diagnosis. Models are evaluated on this dataset across various settings.
AI.result: Models separate positive and FB samples well but fail on NB samples. Perceptual noise and large rule-induced search spaces pose additional challenges, especially in few-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: Visual inductive reasoning remains an open challenge. COCOLogic-V2 provides a concrete foundation for advancing methods in this direction.

title: HumanMoveVQA: Can Video MLLMs reason about human movement in videos?
summary: Despite the rapid advance of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in high-level video understanding, a fundamental bottleneck remains: these models collapse complex human motion into coarse semantic labels. Existing benchmarks mostly focus on scene-centric events or local joint articulations, failing to probe global human motion in space over time (trajectory and orientation changes). We introduce HumanMoveVQA, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate global trajectory and orientation reasoning from an exocentric perspective. Our benchmark utilizes a first-frame anchored world coordinate system, preserving translation and rotation relative to a fixed starting point. We propose a scalable, multi-stage pipeline that lifts 2D video observations into world-consistent 3D motion tracks to generate over 10K structured question-answer pairs across seven reasoning categories, including motion aggregation, sequential ordering, and trajectory-level inference. Our extensive evaluation reveals a critical capability gap in state-of-the-art proprietary models on deep human motion understanding. However, we demonstrate that this is a learnable problem; by fine-tuning an open-source baseline with our targeted, world-consistent supervision, we achieve a significant improvement.HumanMoveVQA establishes a rigorous geometric foundation for developing next-generation, movement-aware video understanding models.

AI.tldr: HumanMoveVQA is a benchmark evaluating MLLMs on global human motion reasoning (trajectory & orientation) using a first-frame anchored 3D world coordinate system. It reveals a capability gap in proprietary models but shows fine-tuning with world-consistent supervision significantly improves performance.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs and benchmarks fail at global human motion understanding, collapsing motion into coarse labels or focusing on local joints/scene events, lacking spatial-temporal trajectory and orientation reasoning from an exocentric perspective.
AI.method: A scalable multi-stage pipeline lifts 2D video observations into world-consistent 3D motion tracks using a first-frame anchored world coordinate system, generating 10K+ QA pairs across 7 reasoning categories (motion aggregation, sequential ordering, trajectory-level inference).
AI.result: State-of-the-art proprietary MLLMs exhibit a critical capability gap on deep human motion understanding; fine-tuning an open-source baseline with targeted world-consistent supervision yields significant improvement.
AI.conclusion: HumanMoveVQA establishes a rigorous geometric foundation for developing movement-aware video understanding models, demonstrating that global motion reasoning is a learnable problem with appropriate supervision.

title: Towards Value-Constrained Credit Assignment in Fully Delegated AI Cooperatives
summary: We propose a framework for reward allocation in fully delegated AI cooperatives where humans are represented by agents that contribute data and participate in model updates under heterogeneous value constraints. The key idea is to credit only those updates that remain admissible after screening them against each principal's value profile. We formulate value-conditioned gradient filtering, online marginal contribution signals, and cumulative revenue settlement within a traversal learning (TL) substrate. TL is especially attractive here because it performs decentralized backpropagation without the quality loss associated with aggregation-centric distributed learning and, we argue, offers a finer attribution substrate than FedAvg-style federated learning by preserving explicit traversal and gradient paths. The framework is positioned against data valuation, federated contribution estimation, personalized federated learning, and pluralistic alignment.

AI.tldr: A reward allocation framework for AI cooperatives using value-conditioned gradient filtering and traversal learning to credit only admissible updates from human-represented agents under heterogeneous value constraints.
AI.motivation: To enable fair and value-aligned reward allocation in fully delegated AI cooperatives where human principals have heterogeneous value systems and are represented by contributing agents.
AI.method: Value-conditioned gradient filtering screens updates against each principal's value profile; online marginal contribution signals and cumulative revenue settlement are implemented within a traversal learning (TL) substrate, which performs decentralized backpropagation preserving explicit traversal and gradient paths.
AI.result: TL offers finer attribution than FedAvg-style federated learning by maintaining explicit gradient paths, avoiding quality loss from aggregation-centric distributed learning.
AI.conclusion: The framework addresses challenges in data valuation, federated contribution estimation, personalized federated learning, and pluralistic alignment.

title: Curriculum-guided Change Detection Training: Toward Accurate Serac Fall Monitoring
summary: Change Detection (CD) aims to identify semantic or structural changes from nearly registered multi-temporal images. While recent advances in training methodologies have largely focused on semi-supervised learning and consistency regularization, alternative training paradigms remain underexplored. In particular, most deep CD methods rely on uniform sampling during training, implicitly assuming that all training samples contribute equally to the optimization process. However, such naive sampling can introduce noisy gradients and hinder robust representation learning. To address this limitation, we propose a curriculum learning framework tailored for change detection. Our approach investigates two complementary difficulty measures: the Solar Angular Gap (SAG), a physically grounded proxy for acquisition-condition variability, and the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), which evaluates appearance similarity between image pairs. Based on these criteria, the framework progressively introduces challenging samples during training, enabling models to learn robust representations in a coarse-to-fine manner. We evaluate our method on the challenging SeracFallDet benchmark, where results demonstrate consistent improvements of the proposed approach over standard uniform-sampling strategies for both pixel-based and object-based approaches. These results highlight the potential of curriculum learning to improve robustness in deep change detection. Importantly, our training framework is orthogonal to existing CD architectures, making it readily applicable to a broad range of methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a curriculum learning framework for Change Detection that uses two difficulty measures (SAG and SSIM) to progressively introduce challenging training samples, improving robustness over standard uniform sampling strategies.
AI.motivation: Current deep CD methods rely on uniform sampling during training, assuming all samples contribute equally, which introduces noisy gradients and hinders robust representation learning. Alternative training paradigms remain underexplored.
AI.method: Proposes a curriculum learning framework with two complementary difficulty measures: Solar Angular Gap (SAG) for acquisition-condition variability and Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) for appearance similarity. Models are trained progressively from easy to hard samples in a coarse-to-fine manner.
